topics.title: Refrigeration - Units of Refrigeration - COP - Reversed Carnot Cycle - T-S Diagram - P-H Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEFTT414 - REFRIGERATION AND COLD CHAIN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Refrigeration - Units of Refrigeration - COP - Reversed Carnot Cycle - T-S Diagram - P-H Chart # MODULE 1: FOUNDATIONS OF REFRIGERATION ## 1. Refrigeration — Core Definition & Intuition ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Refrigeration** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the transfer of heat from a low-temperature body (the *cold space* or *region to be cooled*) to a high-temperature body (the *surroundings* or *heat sink*) by the expenditure of external work, so as to produce a temperature lower than that of the surroundings. In thermodynamic terms, refrigeration is a **non-spontaneous (work-driven) heat transfer process** that proceeds opposite to the natural direction of heat flow. The working substance that absorbs, transports, and rejects heat is called the **refrigerant**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition Standard (2024 Scheme):** > *Refrigeration is the process of removing heat from a space or substance and rejecting it to the surroundings in order to maintain a temperature lower than that of the surroundings. The device used to accomplish this is called a **refrigerator**, and the entire cyclic system forms the **refrigeration cycle**.* ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Think of a **water pump** in reverse. Just as a water pump lifts water from a low level to a high level (against gravity), a **refrigerator lifts heat from a cold space to a hot environment** (against the natural flow of heat). The pump uses electrical energy to move water *uphill*; the refrigerator uses mechanical/electrical work to move heat *uphill* on the temperature scale. A real-world example: Your household air-conditioner during Kerala summers. The indoor unit absorbs heat from the room (low-temperature side) and the outdoor unit dumps that heat into the hot atmosphere (high-temperature side), using compressor work as input. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction Students Must Remember:** > - **Refrigeration** → Goal is to *cool* a space (provide *refrigerating effect*). > - **Heat Pump** → Goal is to *heat* a space (deliver heat to a high-temperature body). > - The thermodynamic *cycle* is the same; only the *useful output* differs. ### 1.3 The Two Primary Applications of a Refrigeration System | Application | Useful Output | Cold-Side Use | Hot-Side Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Refrigerator / A.C.** | Refrigerating Effect ($R_E$) | Cooling desired | Rejected as waste | | **Heat Pump** | Heating Effect ($Q_H$) | Auxiliary (low-grade) | Primary deliverable | ### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics (Bold-Highlighted) - **1 Ton of Refrigeration (1 TR) = 3.517 kW = 210 kJ/min = 12,000 BTU/hr** - **1 BTU (British Thermal Unit) = 1.055 kJ** - **1 hp (mechanical) = 0.7457 kW = 2544.43 BTU/hr** - **Universal Gas Constant, $R_u = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K)** - **Specific heat of water, $c_{pw} = 4.186$ kJ/(kg·K)** > [!NOTE] > **Origin of "Ton of Refrigeration":** Historically defined as the rate of heat extraction required to freeze **1 ton (2000 lb ≈ 907.185 kg) of pure water at 0 °C into ice at 0 °C in 24 hours**. The latent heat of fusion of water at 0 °C is **335 kJ/kg**, giving $1\,\text{TR} = \frac{907.185 \times 335}{24 \times 60} = 211\,\text{kJ/min} \approx 3.517\,\text{kW}$. --- ## 2. Units of Refrigeration ### 2.1 Detailed Exposition of Each Unit **A. Ton of Refrigeration (TR) — Most Common in India & KTU Exams** The commercial unit in India, USA, and most KTU question papers. Defined as the rate of heat removal equal to the latent heat extracted when **1 ton (2000 lb)** of water at 0 °C is converted to ice at 0 °C in 24 hours. **B. kiloWatt (kW) — SI Unit** The rate of heat transfer in SI units. Used internationally in equipment nameplates. **C. BTU/hr — Imperial Unit** Used in older equipment and American literature. **D. kilojoule per minute (kJ/min) or kilojoule per second (kW)** Used in thermodynamic derivations. ### 2.2 Conversion Table (Essential for KTU Numerical Solving) | From | To | Multiply by | |---|---|---| | 1 TR | kW | **3.51685** | | 1 kW | TR | **0.28434** | | 1 TR | BTU/hr | **12,000** | | 1 BTU/hr | TR | **8.333 × 10⁻⁵** | | 1 TR | kJ/min | **211** | | 1 hp | kW | **0.7457** | | 1 kW | BTU/hr | **3412.14** | > [!CAUTION] > **KTU Exam Pitfall:** The "ton" used in refrigeration is the *short ton* (2000 lb) in the USA, but the *metric ton* (1000 kg) is sometimes mistakenly used. In Indian/KTU conventions, **1 TR = 3.517 kW** (short ton basis). Always state the conversion in your solution. --- ## 3. Coefficient of Performance (COP) ### 3.1 Definition (Rigorous) The **Coefficient of Performance (COP)** is defined as the ratio of the *desired effect* (useful output) to the *work input* required to produce that effect. It is a dimensionless figure of merit — analogous to *thermal efficiency* in a heat engine, but for refrigerators and heat pumps. ### 3.2 Two Forms of COP **For a Refrigerator:** $$\text{COP}_{\text{Refrigerator}} = \frac{\text{Desired Refrigerating Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}}$$ **For a Heat Pump:** $$\text{COP}_{\text{Heat Pump}} = \frac{\text{Desired Heating Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}}$$ ### 3.3 Physical Meaning — Intuitive A COP of **4** means the refrigerator delivers **4 units of cooling** for every **1 unit of work** consumed. Higher COP = more efficient. Unlike efficiency, COP can exceed 1 because the desired output (heat) is not the work itself but heat *pumped*. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Standard — First Law Application to a Refrigeration Cycle:** > For any cyclic refrigeration device operating between two thermal reservoirs, energy conservation (steady-flow, cyclic) demands: > $$W_{\text{net,in}} + Q_L = Q_H \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{net}}$$ ### 3.4 Relationship Between the Two COPs Starting from the energy balance: $$W_{\text{net}} = Q_H - Q_L$$ Divide throughout by $W_{\text{net}}$: $$\frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}} = 1 + \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}}$$ This yields the elegant identity: $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} + 1}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Geometric meaning on a T-S diagram:** The refrigerating effect $Q_L$ is the area under the lower isotherm; $Q_H$ is the area under the upper isotherm; $W_{\text{net}}$ is the enclosed area of the rectangle. Thus $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{\text{Lower Area}}{\text{Enclosed Area}}$ and $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{\text{Upper Area}}{\text{Enclosed Area}}$. # 4. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS: REVERSED CARNOT CYCLE ## 4.1 Origin and Significance The **Reversed Carnot Cycle** is a *theoretical* (idealized) refrigeration cycle that operates between two thermal reservoirs at constant temperatures $T_L$ (cold space) and $T_H$ (hot space). It is the *reverse* of the Carnot heat engine cycle — instead of converting heat to work, it uses work to transfer heat from cold to hot. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Reversed" Carnot is Important for KTU:** > It sets the **theoretical upper limit** of COP for *any* refrigerator operating between the same two temperatures. No real cycle can exceed this COP. This is the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** applied to refrigeration (Clausius Inequality: heat cannot spontaneously flow from cold to hot). ## 4.2 The Four Constituent Processes The Reversed Carnot Refrigeration Cycle consists of **four internally reversible processes**, executed in the order shown below. ### Process 1 → 2: Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Compression - The refrigerant is in **saturated vapor** state at the evaporator exit. - A compressor raises its pressure from $P_L$ to $P_H$ **without heat transfer** ($Q = 0$). - Temperature rises from $T_L$ to $T_H$. - Work input to compressor: $W_{\text{comp,in}} = h_2 - h_1$ ### Process 2 → 3: Isothermal Heat Rejection at $T_H$ - The superheated vapor at state 2 is condensed to **saturated liquid** at $T_H$ inside the condenser. - Heat is rejected at constant temperature $T_H$ to the surroundings. - Heat rejected: $Q_H = T_H (S_2 - S_3) = T_H (S_g @ T_H - S_f @ T_H)$ ### Process 3 → 4: Isentropic (Reversible Adiabatic) Expansion - The saturated liquid at state 3 passes through an **expansion valve** (or ideal turbine) with no heat transfer. - Pressure drops from $P_H$ to $P_L$; temperature drops from $T_H$ to $T_L$. - For an ideal isentropic throttling/turbine: $h_3 = h_4$. ### Process 4 → 1: Isothermal Heat Absorption at $T_L$ - The low-pressure, low-temperature mixture at state 4 evaporates to **saturated vapor** at $T_L$ inside the evaporator. - Heat absorbed from the cold space: $Q_L = T_L (S_1 - S_4) = T_L (S_g @ T_L - S_f @ T_L)$ > [!NOTE] > **Why isentropic processes?** Reversibility demands that all internal processes be frictionless and quasi-static. The isentropic compression/expansion and the isothermal heat transfer are the only two internally reversible processes available. Any deviation (friction, finite ΔT heat transfer) makes the cycle *irreversible* and lowers the COP. ## 4.3 Sign Convention in KTU Examinations | Quantity | Refrigerator Convention | Heat Engine Convention | |---|---|---| | $Q_L$ (absorbed) | **Positive** | Positive | | $Q_H$ (rejected) | **Negative** (out of system) | Positive | | $W_{\text{net}}$ | **Negative** (input) | Positive (output) | For KTU problems, work input is *always taken as positive* on the magnitude side: $W_{\text{net,in}} = Q_H - Q_L$. ## 4.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the EXAM-CRITICAL formula sheet. Memorize all entries.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ton of Refrigeration | $1\,\text{TR} = 3.517\,\text{kW} = 211\,\text{kJ/min} = 12{,}000\,\text{BTU/hr}$ | Commercial unit | | 2 | Energy balance (cycle) | $Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{net}}$ | Steady-flow, cyclic | | 3 | $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}}$ (Reversed Carnot) | $\displaystyle \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Temperatures in **K** | | 4 | $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}}$ (Reversed Carnot) | $\displaystyle \text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$ | Temperatures in **K** | | 5 | Relation between COPs | $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} + 1$ | Always true for any cycle | | 6 | Work input | $W_{\text{net,in}} = Q_H - Q_L$ | For Reversed Carnot: $W = Q_H - Q_L$ | | 7 | $Q_L$ on T-S diagram | Area under lower isotherm = $T_L \Delta S$ | kJ | | 8 | $Q_H$ on T-S diagram | Area under upper isotherm = $T_H \Delta S$ | kJ | | 9 | $W_{\text{net}}$ on T-S diagram | Enclosed rectangle area = $(T_H - T_L)\Delta S$ | kJ | | 10 | Refrigerating Effect | $R_E = \dot{m} \cdot (h_1 - h_4)$ | kW (per kg/s) | | 11 | Heat Rejection Rate | $\dot{Q}_H = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3)$ | kW | | 12 | Compressor Work | $\dot{W}_{\text{comp}} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | | 13 | Expansion Valve | $h_3 = h_4$ | Throttling (irreversible) — real | | 14 | Ideal Turbine | $h_3 = h_4$ (isentropic) | Reversible — theoretical | | 15 | $\Delta S$ for cycle | $\Delta S = S_g - S_f = S_{fg}$ at $T_L$ or $T_H$ | kJ/kg·K | | 16 | Clausius Equality | $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$ | Reversed Carnot | ## 4.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The Reversed Carnot COP equation: $$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref,Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$ is the **benchmark** against which **all** real refrigeration cycles (Vapour Compression, Vapour Absorption, Air Refrigeration, Cascade) are compared. Real cycles have COP = 0.3 to 0.6 of the Carnot COP. Industrial design priorities (lower cost, simpler components) justify accepting the lower real COP. > [!NOTE] > **Carnot COP depends ONLY on temperature limits.** It is *independent* of the working fluid. This is a key KTU conceptual question: *"Why is COP of a refrigerator higher when $T_L$ is closer to $T_H$?"* Answer: Because the temperature lift $(T_H - T_L)$ is smaller, requiring less work for the same $Q_L$. ## 4.6 Limitation of the Reversed Carnot Cycle in Practice | Limitation | Reason | |---|---| | Isentropic compression in the wet region | Liquid droplets cause mechanical damage to the compressor | | Isothermal heat transfer in the two-phase region | Requires extremely large heat exchangers | | Expander not used in real systems | Throttling valve is irreversible but cheap | | Cannot be achieved with a single real fluid | Practical cycles are the **Vapour Compression Cycle (VCC)** | # 5. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 5.1 Derivation of Reversed Carnot COP for a Refrigerator **Starting Premise:** Apply the Steady-Flow Energy Equation (SFEE) to the four-component cycle, then use the Second Law of Thermodynamics for a reversible cycle. ### Step 1 — Energy Balance Over the Cycle For a cyclic device operating in steady state, the net energy transfer equals zero. Considering heat $Q_L$ absorbed at $T_L$ and heat $Q_H$ rejected at $T_H$, with work input $W_{\text{net,in}}$: $$Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{net,in}} \quad \text{(Steady-Flow Energy Equation, cycle)}$$ *Explanation:* Heat rejected to the hot reservoir equals heat absorbed from the cold reservoir plus the work input. This is **First Law of Thermodynamics** for a cyclic device. ### Step 2 — Apply Second Law (Clausius Equality) For a **reversible** cycle, the Clausius integral equals zero: $$\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$$ The cycle has only two isothermal heat-transfer steps (the two adiabatics contribute nothing). Hence: $$\frac{Q_L}{T_L} - \frac{Q_H}{T_H} = 0$$ *Explanation:* Heat absorbed at $T_L$ is *positive*; heat rejected at $T_H$ is *negative* in the convention. Reversibility demands no net entropy generation. ### Step 3 — Solve for $Q_H$ in Terms of $Q_L$ Rearranging the previous equation: $$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_L}{T_L}$$ $$Q_H = Q_H = T_H \cdot \frac{Q_L}{T_L} = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}$$ ### Step 4 — Express Net Work Substitute into the energy balance: $$W_{\text{net,in}} = Q_H - Q_L = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L} - Q_L$$ $$W_{\text{net,in}} = Q_L \left( \frac{T_H}{T_L} - 1 \right) = Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}$$ ### Step 5 — Form the COP By definition, $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net,in}}}$. Substituting: $$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{Q_L}{Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}$$ $$\boxed{\text{COP}_{\text{Ref,Carnot}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}}$$ ### Step 6 — Form the Heat Pump COP Similarly For a heat pump, the desired output is $Q_H$: $$\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net,in}}} = \frac{Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H}{T_L}}{Q_L \cdot \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_L}}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L}$$ ### Step 7 — Establish the Identity Subtracting: $$\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} - \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} - \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H - T_L} = 1$$ $$\therefore \quad \text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} + 1$$ --- ## 5.2 Derivation of the T-S Diagram Areas For the Reversed Carnot cycle on a T-S diagram, the cycle appears as a **rectangle** with: - Horizontal sides at $T_L$ and $T_H$ - Vertical sides at constant entropy (isentropic processes) ### Step 1 — Compute $Q_L$ $Q_L$ is the heat absorbed along the lower isotherm at $T_L$: $$Q_L = T_L \, \Delta S = T_L (S_1 - S_4)$$ For a Carnot cycle operating between saturated states: $$Q_L = T_L \cdot s_{fg} \, \big\vert_{T = T_L}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute $Q_H$ $Q_H$ is the heat rejected along the upper isotherm at $T_H$: $$Q_H = T_H \, \Delta S = T_H (S_2 - S_3)$$ $$Q_H = T_H \cdot s_{fg} \, \big\vert_{T = T_H}$$ ### Step 3 — Net Work (Enclosed Area) $$W_{\text{net,in}} = Q_H - Q_L = (T_H - T_L) \, \Delta S$$ This is the area of the **rectangle** on the T-S diagram. ### Step 4 — Express COP in Terms of Diagram Geometry $$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{T_L \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \Delta S} = \frac{\text{Area under lower isotherm}}{\text{Area of rectangle}}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{T_H \Delta S}{(T_H - T_L) \Delta S} = \frac{\text{Area under upper isotherm}}{\text{Area of rectangle}}$$ --- ## 5.3 Worked Example: Numerical KTU-Style Problem > **Problem (KTU-Style):** > *A Reversed Carnot refrigerator operates between a cold space at $-10\,°\text{C}$ and a hot space at $40\,°\text{C}$. If the refrigerator produces 5 TR of cooling, compute:* > *(a) The COP of the refrigerator.* > *(b) The COP of the heat pump (same cycle).* > *(c) The compressor work input in kW.* > *(d) The rate of heat rejection to the hot space in kW.* ### Solution #### Step 1 — Convert Temperatures to Kelvin $$T_L = -10 + 273 = 263\,\text{K}$$ $$T_H = 40 + 273 = 313\,\text{K}$$ #### Step 2 — Compute COP of the Refrigerator Using the derived formula: $$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{263}{313 - 263} = \frac{263}{50}$$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = 5.26$$ #### Step 3 — Compute COP of the Heat Pump $$\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{313}{50} = 6.26$$ *Verification:* $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} + 1 = 5.26 + 1 = 6.26$ ✓ #### Step 4 — Compute Refrigerating Effect in kW $$\dot{Q}_L = 5\,\text{TR} \times 3.517\,\frac{\text{kW}}{\text{TR}} = 17.585\,\text{kW}$$ #### Step 5 — Compute Compressor Work Input From $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\dot{W}_{\text{in}}}$: $$\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}}} = \frac{17.585}{5.26} = 3.343\,\text{kW}$$ #### Step 6 — Compute Heat Rejection Rate $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 17.585 + 3.343 = 20.928\,\text{kW}$$ *Alternative cross-check using $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}}$:* $$\dot{Q}_H = \text{COP}_{\text{HP}} \times \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 6.26 \times 3.343 = 20.928\,\text{kW} \quad \checkmark$$ > [!TIP] > **Exam Tip — Always verify your answer two ways:** (1) Using the energy balance $Q_H = Q_L + W$, and (2) Using the COP definition $Q_H = \text{COP}_{\text{HP}} \cdot W$. If both match, the solution is correct. --- ## 5.4 Analytical Matrix: Performance Comparison Table | Condition | Effect on $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}}$ | Practical Implication | |---|---|---| | $T_L$ ↑ (warmer cold space) | Increases (numerator ↑) | Less energy to cool a warmer space | | $T_H$ ↑ (hotter environment) | Decreases (denominator ↑) | Harder to reject heat on a hot day | | $T_L \to T_H$ | $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} \to \infty$ | Theoretically no work needed | | $T_L \to 0\,\text{K}$ | $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} \to 0$ | Impossibility principle (Third Law) | | $(T_H - T_L) \uparrow$ | Decreases | Larger temperature lift = more work | # 6. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS ## 6.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Components of a Reversed Carnot Refrigeration System ```mermaid flowchart LR compA[Compressor
Isentropic Compression
State 1 to State 2] --> condA[Condenser
Isothermal Heat Rejection at TH
State 2 to State 3] condA --> expA[Expander or Throttle
Isentropic Expansion
State 3 to State 4] expA --> evapA[Evaporator
Isothermal Heat Absorption at TL
State 4 to State 1] evapA --> compA condA -. Heat Rejected QH .-> sink1[Hot Reservoir at TH] evapA -. Heat Absorbed QL .-> sink2[Cold Reservoir at TL] compA -. Work Input Wnet .-> wsrc[External Work Source] ``` **Reading the diagram:** The refrigerant flows in a closed loop: Compressor → Condenser → Expander → Evaporator → Compressor. The dashed arrows show energy transfers with the surroundings. ## 6.2 Mermaid T-S Diagram of the Reversed Carnot Refrigeration Cycle ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TSDiagram[T-S Diagram of Reversed Carnot Refrigeration Cycle] direction TB axisS[S Entropy kJ per kg K] --> axisT[T Temperature K] pt1["State 1: Saturated Vapor at TL"] pt2["State 2: Superheated Vapor at TH"] pt3["State 3: Saturated Liquid at TH"] pt4["State 4: Two Phase Mixture at TL"] pt1 -- Isentropic Compression 1 to 2 --> pt2 pt2 -- Isothermal Rejection at TH 2 to 3 --> pt3 pt3 -- Isentropic Expansion 3 to 4 --> pt4 pt4 -- Isothermal Absorption at TL 4 to 1 --> pt1 end noteH[QH equals TH times Delta S Area under upper isotherm] -.-> pt2 noteL[QL equals TL times Delta S Area under lower isotherm] -.-> pt1 noteW[Wnet equals QH minus QL Enclosed rectangular area] -.-> pt1 ``` **Key reading:** The four states form a rectangle. The horizontal segments (1→2 and 3→4) are isentropic (constant $S$). The vertical segments (4→1 and 2→3) are isothermal (constant $T$). ## 6.3 Mermaid P-H Chart Block Architecture (Functional Map) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PHAxis[P-H Chart Pressure vs Enthalpy Axes] direction LR xAxis["X axis Enthalpy h kJ per kg"] yAxis["Y axis Pressure P kPa Log Scale"] end subgraph PHCurve[Saturation Dome] satL["Saturated Liquid Line Left Boundary"] satV["Saturated Vapor Line Right Boundary"] critP["Critical Point Top of Dome"] end subgraph PHRegions[Regions of the P-H Chart] rA[Region A Subcooled or Compressed Liquid] rB[Region B Saturated Liquid State 3] rC[Region C Two Phase Mixture Inside Dome] rD[Region D Saturated Vapor State 1] rE[Region E Superheated Vapor State 2] end subgraph PHStates[Refrigeration Cycle States on P-H Chart] stA["State 1 Compressor Inlet Saturated Vapor"] stB["State 2 Compressor Outlet Superheated Vapor"] stC["State 3 Condenser Outlet Saturated Liquid"] stD["State 4 Evaporator Inlet Two Phase"] end stA --> rD stB --> rE stC --> rB stD --> rC ``` ## 6.4 Mermaid Flowchart: Step-by-Step Analysis Procedure (For KTU Problems) ```mermaid flowchart TB start([Start KTU Problem]) --> readT[Read TL and TH in degree C] readT --> convK[Convert to Kelvin add 273] convK --> computeCOP[Compute COP Ref equals TL divided by TH minus TL] computeCOP --> branch{Q is TR or kW} branch -- TR given --> convTR[Multiply TR by 3.517 to get kW] branch -- kW given --> skipTR[Use directly] convTR --> compW skipTR --> compW[Compute Work equals QL divided by COP Ref] compW --> compQH[Compute QH equals QL plus W] compQH --> compCOPHP[Compute COP HP equals QH divided by W] compCOPHP --> verify{Verify COP HP equals COP Ref plus 1} verify -- Yes --> done([Solution Complete Present with Units]) verify -- No --> check[Recheck Arithmetic and Sign Convention] check --> convK ``` # 7. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK ## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** > *Define the term "Coefficient of Performance" (COP) of a refrigerator. How is it different from a heat pump?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Coefficient of Performance of a refrigerator is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigerating effect to the work input required: $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{Q_L}{W_{\text{net}}}$. For a heat pump, the desired output is the heating effect: $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{Q_H}{W_{\text{net}}}$. The key difference: a refrigerator's *useful output* is the heat removed from the cold space ($Q_L$); a heat pump's *useful output* is the heat delivered to the hot space ($Q_H$). Mathematically, $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} + 1$ for the same cycle. **(Full 3 marks for clear definitions, the formula, and the +1 relationship.)** --- > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > *State the Clausius statement of the Second Law of Thermodynamics as it applies to refrigeration.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Clausius Statement: *"It is impossible to construct a device that operates in a cycle and produces no effect other than the transfer of heat from a cooler body to a hotter body without the input of external work."* Application to refrigeration: A refrigerator *does* transfer heat from cold to hot, but this is *not* spontaneous; it requires work input (compressor). The Clausius inequality for a reversible cycle: $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} = 0$; for irreversible: $\oint \frac{\delta Q}{T} < 0$. **(3 marks: statement, interpretation, mathematical form.)** --- ## PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) > **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEFTT414, Module 1]** > *(a)* A Reversed Carnot refrigerator is used to maintain a cold storage room at $-5\,°\text{C}$. The surroundings are at $35\,°\text{C}$. The refrigerator absorbs heat at the rate of $40\,\text{kW}$ from the cold room. Determine: > (i) The COP of the refrigerator and the COP of the heat pump. > (ii) The power required to drive the compressor in kW. > (iii) The rate of heat rejection to the surroundings. **(7 marks)** > *(b)* With the help of a neat Temperature–Entropy (T–S) diagram, explain the working of a Reversed Carnot refrigeration cycle. Mark the four processes and the heat and work transfers clearly. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.A(a) [7 marks] **Given Data:** - $T_L = -5 + 273 = 268\,\text{K}$ - $T_H = 35 + 273 = 308\,\text{K}$ - $\dot{Q}_L = 40\,\text{kW}$ **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}}$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{268}{308 - 268} = \frac{268}{40} = 6.7$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}}$ $$\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \frac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{308}{40} = 7.7$$ *Verification:* $7.7 = 6.7 + 1.0$ ✓ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute Compressor Work Input $$\dot{W}_{\text{net,in}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}}} = \frac{40}{6.7} = 5.97\,\text{kW}$$ **Step 4 (2 marks):** Compute Heat Rejection Rate $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 40 + 5.97 = 45.97\,\text{kW}$$ *Alternative cross-check:* $$\dot{Q}_H = \text{COP}_{\text{HP}} \times \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 7.7 \times 5.97 = 45.97\,\text{kW} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Final Answer Statement > **Final Results:** > - $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = 6.7$ > - $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = 7.7$ > - $\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 5.97\,\text{kW}$ > - $\dot{Q}_H = 45.97\,\text{kW}$ --- #### Model Solution — Q.A(b) [7 marks] **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define and draw axes Draw a T-S diagram with $T$ on the vertical axis (in K) and $S$ on the horizontal axis (in kJ/kg·K). Mark two horizontal lines: $T_L$ and $T_H$, with $T_H > T_L$. **Step 2 (1 mark):** Identify and describe Process 4→1 (Isothermal Heat Absorption) State 4 (saturated mixture) → State 1 (saturated vapor) at constant $T_L$. Heat $Q_L$ is absorbed from the cold space. This is the **evaporator** process. $Q_L = T_L (S_1 - S_4)$. **Step 3 (1 mark):** Identify and describe Process 1→2 (Isentropic Compression) State 1 (sat. vapor at $T_L$) → State 2 (superheated vapor at $T_H$). Vertical line on T-S (constant entropy). $W_{\text{comp,in}} = h_2 - h_1$. **Step 4 (1 mark):** Identify and describe Process 2→3 (Isothermal Heat Rejection) State 2 → State 3 (saturated liquid) at constant $T_H$. Heat $Q_H$ is rejected. This is the **condenser** process. $Q_H = T_H (S_2 - S_3)$. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Identify and describe Process 3→4 (Isentropic Expansion) State 3 (sat. liquid at $T_H$) → State 4 (two-phase mixture at $T_L$). Vertical line downward. Reversible expansion (theoretical turbine) or irreversible throttling in practice. **Step 6 (1 mark):** Show areas Mark the rectangular area: under lower isotherm = $Q_L$; under upper isotherm = $Q_H$; enclosed rectangle = $W_{\text{net}}$. **Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclusion statement > *"The Reversed Carnot cycle on a T-S diagram appears as a rectangle. Heat $Q_L$ is absorbed at the lower isotherm $T_L$, work $W$ is input during isentropic compression, and heat $Q_H$ is rejected at the upper isotherm $T_H$. The COP is the ratio of the lower isotherm area to the enclosed area."* --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEFTT414, Module 1]** > *(a)* A food processing unit requires a refrigeration system of 10 TR capacity operating between $-15\,°\text{C}$ and $40\,°\text{C}$ on a Reversed Carnot cycle. Calculate: > (i) The COP of the system. > (ii) The mass flow rate of refrigerant if its enthalpy change in the evaporator is $200\,\text{kJ/kg}$. > (iii) The power required to drive the compressor and the heat rejected per hour. **(7 marks)** > *(b)* What is a P-H (Pressure–Enthalpy) chart? List the important regions and the constant-property lines on it. Why is it the most preferred chart for refrigeration cycle analysis? **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Q.B(a) [7 marks] **Given Data:** - Capacity = 10 TR = $10 \times 3.517 = 35.17\,\text{kW}$ - $T_L = -15 + 273 = 258\,\text{K}$ - $T_H = 40 + 273 = 313\,\text{K}$ - Evaporator enthalpy change: $\Delta h_{\text{evap}} = 200\,\text{kJ/kg}$ **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Compute COP $$\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L} = \frac{258}{313 - 258} = \frac{258}{55} = 4.691$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Compute Compressor Work $$\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}}} = \frac{35.17}{4.691} = 7.50\,\text{kW}$$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Compute Heat Rejection Rate and Per Hour $$\dot{Q}_H = \dot{Q}_L + \dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 35.17 + 7.50 = 42.67\,\text{kW}$$ $$Q_H \text{ per hour} = 42.67 \times 3600 = 153{,}612\,\text{kJ/hr} = 153.612\,\text{MJ/hr}$$ **Step 4 (2.5 marks):** Compute Mass Flow Rate Refrigerating effect per unit mass = $\Delta h_{\text{evap}} = 200\,\text{kJ/kg}$ $\dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} \times \Delta h_{\text{evap}}$ $$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{\Delta h_{\text{evap}}} = \frac{35.17}{200} = 0.1759\,\text{kg/s}$$ $$\dot{m} = 0.1759 \times 3600 = 633.1\,\text{kg/hr}$$ > **Final Results:** > - $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = 4.691$ > - $\dot{W}_{\text{in}} = 7.50\,\text{kW}$ > - $\dot{Q}_H = 42.67\,\text{kW} = 153.612\,\text{MJ/hr}$ > - $\dot{m} = 0.1759\,\text{kg/s} = 633.1\,\text{kg/hr}$ --- #### Model Solution — Q.B(b) [7 marks] **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Definition of P-H Chart The **Pressure-Enthalpy (P-H) chart** is a thermodynamic property diagram for a pure refrigerant in which: - The vertical axis represents **Pressure (P)** plotted on a **logarithmic scale** (in kPa). - The horizontal axis represents **Specific Enthalpy (h)** in kJ/kg. It is the most widely used property chart in refrigeration engineering. **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** The Saturation Dome The chart is divided by a **saturation curve (dome)** which has: - **Saturated Liquid Line** on the left. - **Saturated Vapor Line** on the right. - **Critical Point** at the top — the point beyond which liquid and vapor phases are indistinguishable. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Important Regions | Region | Location | Description | |---|---|---| | **A** | Left of saturated liquid line | Subcooled (compressed) liquid | | **B** | On saturated liquid line | State 3 of Vapour Compression Cycle | | **C** | Inside the dome | Two-phase (liquid + vapor) mixture | | **D** | On saturated vapor line | State 1 of Vapour Compression Cycle | | **E** | Right of saturated vapor line | Superheated vapor | **Step 4 (1 mark):** Constant Property Lines - **Constant temperature lines**: Horizontal in subcooled region; nearly vertical inside dome; sloping in superheated region. - **Constant entropy lines**: Sloping downward from left to right. - **Constant volume lines**: Drawn in superheated region. - **Constant quality lines**: Vertical lines inside the dome (e.g., $x = 0.2, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8$). **Step 5 (1 mark):** Why P-H is Most Preferred 1. **Work and heat are areas under the curve:** $\dot{W} = \dot{m}(h_2 - h_1)$ and $\dot{Q} = \dot{m}(h_1 - h_4)$ are *horizontal distances*, easily measurable. 2. **Throttling is a vertical drop:** $h_3 = h_4$ — visual clarity. 3. **All four VCC processes are easily drawn** (compression, condensation, expansion, evaporation). 4. **Numerical integration is avoided** unlike T-S diagrams where areas must be computed. 5. **Compressors, condensers, and evaporators** all have heat transfer/work directly as horizontal distances on the chart. --- ## KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in PEFTT414 Module 1:** > 1. **Temperature unit error (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently forget to convert °C → K. The Carnot COP formula REQUIRES Kelvin. $T_L = -10\,°\text{C}$ is $263\,\text{K}$, not $-10\,\text{K}$. Showing the conversion step explicitly earns full marks. > 2. **Sign convention error (–1 to –2 marks):** Work input is taken as **positive** in KTU convention for refrigerators. Mixing the heat-engine sign convention causes sign errors in $Q_H$ and $W$. > 3. **TR unit confusion (–1 mark):** Always state the unit: $1\,\text{TR} = 3.517\,\text{kW}$. Writing just "TR" without conversion shows partial marks only. > 4. **Reversible vs Irreversible (–1 mark):** Don't confuse the *Reversed Carnot cycle* (ideal, theoretical) with the *Vapour Compression cycle* (real, practical). The Reversed Carnot is the upper limit. > 5. **T-S vs P-H diagram (–1 mark):** For VCC analysis, P-H is preferred. For Carnot analysis, T-S shows the area elegantly. Don't interchange. > 6. **Skipping the energy balance cross-check (–1 mark):** When asked for two quantities, verify $Q_H = Q_L + W$. Examiners award method marks. --- ## 7.1 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST > [!TIP] > **Final-Module-Exam Revision Summary — Read Twice, Score High:** - [x] **Refrigeration** is the work-driven transfer of heat from a cold body to a hot body. - [x] **1 TR = 3.517 kW = 211 kJ/min = 12,000 BTU/hr** (the most-tested conversion). - [x] **COP is dimensionless** — always greater than 1 for practical refrigerators. - [x] $\text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ (Reversed Carnot) — uses **Kelvin**. - [x] $\text{COP}_{\text{HP}} = \dfrac{T_H}{T_H - T_L} = \text{COP}_{\text{Ref}} + 1$. - [x] $Q_H = Q_L + W_{\text{net,in}}$ — First Law of the refrigeration cycle. - [x] **Reversed Carnot** = ideal cycle; sets the *upper limit* of COP for all real cycles. - [x] **Four processes:** Isentropic Compression → Isothermal Rejection → Isentropic Expansion → Isothermal Absorption. - [x] **T-S diagram** = rectangle; $Q_L$ = area under lower isotherm; $Q_H$ = area under upper isotherm; $W$ = enclosed area. - [x] **P-H chart** has Pressure (log) on Y-axis, Enthalpy on X-axis; saturation dome divides subcooled liquid, two-phase mixture, and superheated vapor regions. - [x] **Clausius Statement** forbids spontaneous cold-to-hot heat transfer; refrigeration requires work input. - [x] For VCC analysis, **P-H is preferred**; for Carnot analysis, **T-S is preferred**. > **End of Module 1 Notes — PEFTT414 (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

topics.title: Types of Refrigerants and Their Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT414 - REFRIGERATION AND COLD CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Refrigerants and Their Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **refrigerant** is defined as a working fluid that undergoes a phase-change cycle (predominantly evaporation and condensation) in a thermodynamic refrigeration loop, absorbing heat at a low temperature from the refrigerated space (evaporator) and rejecting it at a higher temperature to the surroundings (condenser). In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, refrigerants are classified as **primary refrigerants** (fluids that themselves undergo the phase change) and **secondary refrigerants** (heat transfer fluids like brine, glycol-water mixtures, or air that act as carriers of cooling energy from the primary loop to the load).

Mathematically, the net refrigeration effect per unit mass of refrigerant is given by:

$$q_L = h_1 - h_4$$

where $h_1$ is the enthalpy of saturated vapour at the evaporator exit and $h_4$ is the enthalpy at the evaporator inlet (after throttling).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Refrigerants are the "blood" of any refrigeration system. An ideal refrigerant must satisfy thermodynamic, chemical, physical, and safety criteria simultaneously, and the modern choice of refrigerant is dominated by environmental concerns (ODP, GWP, TEWI) rather than purely thermodynamic performance.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **sweating ice cube** placed on your palm. The ice absorbs heat from your hand, melts, and cools your skin. The sweat (water) is acting as a *refrigerant*. A refrigeration system does the same thing continuously, in a closed loop:
- The **evaporator** is your palm (the cold region).
- The **condenser** is the surrounding air (the hot region).
- A **compressor** is the "postman" that physically moves the now-gaseous fluid from cold to hot, and a **throttle valve** is the "lazy" device that drops pressure so the cycle can repeat.

In a domestic refrigerator, the same fluid (commonly **R-134a** in modern units) keeps circulating forever, just the water in our analogy keeps "sweating" forever.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **ASHRAE Standard 34** descriptors are mandatory for KTU exam answers:

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Reference |
|---|---|---|
| Boiling Point (at 1.013 bar) | BP | °C |
| Critical Temperature | $T_c$ | °C or K |
| Critical Pressure | $P_c$ | bar or kPa |
| Latent Heat of Vaporization | $h_{fg}$ | kJ/kg |
| Specific Heat Ratio | $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ | dimensionless |
| Ozone Depletion Potential | ODP | relative to R-11 = 1.0 |
| Global Warming Potential | GWP | relative to CO₂ = 1.0 over 100 years |

> [!NOTE]
> The thermodynamic **latent heat of vaporization** at evaporator conditions is the most important single property — it directly determines the **mass flow rate** of refrigerant required for a given cooling load:
> $$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{q_L}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Desirable Properties of an Ideal Refrigerant (KTU Core)

A *perfect* refrigerant does not exist, but engineers rate real refrigerants against the following criteria. KTU exam questions frequently test these bullet points in both Part A and Part B.

### 2.1.1 Thermodynamic Properties
- **Low boiling point** (well below the desired evaporator temperature) at atmospheric pressure.
- **High latent heat of vaporization** — reduces mass flow rate, hence smaller compressor displacement.
- **Low specific volume of vapour** at compressor suction — smaller compressor for the same capacity.
- **Moderate condensing pressure** (not too high to require heavy vessels, not too low to draw in air-leak moisture).
- **Low specific heat ratio $\gamma$** — lowers the theoretical compression work $w_c = h_2 - h_1$.
- **High critical temperature** — prevents condensation becoming impossible in hot climates.

### 2.1.2 Chemical Properties
- Chemically **stable** (does not decompose at compressor discharge temperatures).
- **Non-corrosive** to copper, steel, aluminium, and elastomer seals.
- **Non-reactive** with lubricating oil (miscible or immiscible in a predictable, controlled way).
- **No tendency to polymerise or oxidise** during long service.

### 2.1.3 Physical & Safety Properties
- **Non-toxic** and **non-irritating** to humans (per ASHRAE 34 Safety Group A1 = lower toxicity + no flame propagation).
- **Non-flammable** and **non-explosive** in normal operating range.
- **Low viscosity** (both liquid and vapour) to minimise pressure drops in piping.
- **High thermal conductivity** to improve heat transfer coefficients in the evaporator and condenser.
- **Low freezing point** (must remain liquid during throttling and storage).
- **Easy to detect leaks** — historically why chlorine-based CFCs could be detected with halide torches.

### 2.1.4 Environmental Properties (modern KTU emphasis)
- **ODP = 0** (does not deplete stratospheric ozone).
- **GWP ≤ 150** under Kigali Amendment / F-Gas regulation targets.
- **Zero or low atmospheric lifetime**.
- **No Persistent Organic Pollutant (POP)** characteristics.

## 2.2 Classification of Refrigerants (Hierarchical Logic)

**Step 1 — Primary vs Secondary.**
**Step 2 — Primary split into Natural vs Synthetic.**
**Step 3 — Synthetic split into Halo-carbons vs Non-halogenated organics.**
**Step 4 — Halo-carbons split by the presence of Cl, Br (Montreal Protocol logic):**
- **CFC** (Chloro-Fluoro-Carbon) — fully halogenated with chlorine, e.g., R-12, R-11. **Banned.**
- **HCFC** (Hydro-Chloro-Fluoro-Carbon) — partial hydrogen substitution, e.g., R-22. **Phased out** in new equipment.
- **HFC** (Hydro-Fluoro-Carbon) — chlorine-free, e.g., R-134a, R-410A, R-32. **Current standard** but high-GWP.
- **HFO** (Hydro-Fluoro-Olefin) — unsaturated, e.g., R-1234yf, R-1234ze. **Fourth-generation, low-GWP.**
- **Natural refrigerants** — Ammonia (R-717), CO₂ (R-744), Propane (R-290), Isobutane (R-600a), Water (R-718), Air (R-729). **Renewed importance** under green-refrigeration movement.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $q_L = h_1 - h_4$ | Net refrigerating effect per kg | kJ/kg |
| 2 | $w_c = h_2 - h_1$ | Specific compression work (isentropic) | kJ/kg |
| 3 | $w_{net} = (h_2 - h_1) - (h_3 - h_4)$ | Net work input to cycle | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $COP_{ref} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Coefficient of Performance (theoretical vapour compression) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $COP_{carnot} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$ | Upper limit (reversible) COP | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_L}{q_L}$ | Mass flow rate of refrigerant | kg/s |
| 7 | $V_s = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot v_1}{\eta_{vol}}$ | Theoretical compressor displacement | m³/s |
| 8 | $P_{comp} = \dfrac{w_c}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right]$ | Compression power per kg of ideal gas | kJ/kg |
| 9 | $T_2 = T_1\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$ | Isentropic discharge temperature (ideal gas) | K |
| 10 | $GWP_{mix} = \sum_i x_i \cdot GWP_i$ | GWP of zeotropic blend (mass-weighted) | — |
| 11 | $TEWI = GWP_{direct} \cdot m_{leak} + GWP_{indirect} \cdot E_{total}$ | Total Equivalent Warming Impact | kg CO₂-eq |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap:** Use $\dfrac{1}{\gamma - 1}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}\right] \cdot R \cdot T_1$ for the *work* in an open-system compressor model. Do **not** confuse it with the heat-engine formula $w = c_v(T_2 - T_1)$; the former is more common in refrigeration KTU problems.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Modern refrigerant selection drives entire industries:
- **Domestic refrigerators** use R-600a (isobutane) for energy efficiency.
- **Automotive AC** has shifted to R-1234yf.
- **Industrial cold storage** still relies on R-717 (ammonia) for its excellent thermophysical properties.
- **Supercritical CO₂ trans-critical systems** (R-744) are now standard in commercial heat-pump dryers and supermarket trans-critical booster systems, with GWP = 1 and high volumetric capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Refrigeration Effect and COP for an Ideal Vapour-Compression Cycle

**Given:** Saturated vapour at compressor inlet (state 1), isentropic compression to state 2, condensation to saturated liquid (state 3), isenthalpic throttling to state 4, evaporation back to state 1.

**Step 1 — Apply First Law to the evaporator (open system, steady-state, neglecting KE/PE).**

The energy balance over the evaporator control volume is:

$$\dot{m} h_4 + \dot{Q}_L = \dot{m} h_1$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the refrigeration effect per unit mass.**

Rearranging the energy equation by isolating $\dot{Q}_L$ and dividing by $\dot{m}$:

$$q_L = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

This is the **net refrigerating effect**. It is the *useful cooling* produced per kilogram of refrigerant circulated.

**Step 3 — Apply First Law to the compressor (isentropic, adiabatic).**

For the compressor, work input $w_c$ is the change in enthalpy between suction and discharge:

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

In the idealised case (no clearance loss, 100 % isentropic efficiency, no pressure drop in suction), this is also the *specific compression work*.

**Step 4 — Apply First Law to the condenser.**

The condenser rejects the combined heat of compression plus the latent heat absorbed in the evaporator:

$$q_H = h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{[kJ/kg]}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Steady-Flow Energy Equation to the throttle (isenthalpic).**

The throttle does no work, no heat transfer (idealisation), so:

$$h_3 = h_4$$

This is the **Joule-Thomson isenthalpic expansion**, key to generating the cold temperature at the evaporator inlet.

**Step 6 — Define the Coefficient of Performance.**

By definition, the COP of a refrigerator is the ratio of desired output (refrigeration effect) to required input (net work):

$$\begin{aligned}
COP_{ref} &= \frac{q_L}{w_{net}} \\
&= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_2 - h_1) - (h_3 - h_4)} \\
&= \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1} \quad \text{(since } h_3 = h_4 \text{)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Compare with the Carnot (reversible) COP for the same temperature limits.**

For a reversible cycle operating between the same two reservoirs $T_L$ (evaporator saturation) and $T_H$ (condenser saturation):

$$COP_{carnot} = \frac{T_L}{T_H - T_L}$$

Since the vapour-compression cycle is *internally irreversible* (throttling + finite temperature difference in HX), $COP_{ref} < COP_{carnot}$. This is the **second-law efficiency** $\eta_{II}$:

$$\eta_{II} = \frac{COP_{ref}}{COP_{carnot}} \times 100\%$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Discharge Temperature of an Isentropic Compression (Ideal Gas Approximation)

**Step 1 — Start from the isentropic relation for an ideal gas:**

$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

**Step 2 — Solve explicitly for $T_2$:**

$$T_2 = T_1 \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(\gamma - 1)/\gamma}$$

**Step 3 — Example numerical walk-through for R-134a:**
- $T_1 = -10 \degree C = 263.15$ K
- $P_1 = P_{sat}(-10\degree C) \approx 2.006$ bar
- $P_2 = P_{sat}(40\degree C) \approx 10.16$ bar
- $P_2/P_1 \approx 5.07$
- $\gamma$ for R-134a vapour at average temperature ≈ **1.13**
- Exponent: $(1.13 - 1)/1.13 = 0.1150$

$$\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= 263.15 \times (5.07)^{0.1150} \\
&= 263.15 \times 1.2195 \\
&\approx 320.9 \text{ K} \approx 47.7 \degree C
\end{aligned}$$

The real discharge temperature (with superheat and non-ideal effects) is closer to 55–60 °C. The 8–10 K discrepancy is normal and represents *departure from ideal gas + superheat* in real refrigeration.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Properties of Major Refrigerants

| Property | R-12 (CFC) | R-22 (HCFC) | R-134a (HFC) | R-717 (NH₃) | R-744 (CO₂) | R-290 (C₃H₈) | R-1234yf |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular Formula | $\text{CCl}_2\text{F}_2$ | $\text{CHClF}_2$ | $\text{CH}_2\text{FCF}_3$ | $\text{NH}_3$ | $\text{CO}_2$ | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ | $\text{CF}_3\text{CF=CH}_2$ |
| Molar Mass (kg/kmol) | 120.91 | 86.47 | 102.03 | 17.03 | 44.01 | 44.10 | 114.04 |
| Normal Boiling Pt (°C) | −29.8 | −40.8 | −26.1 | −33.3 | −78.4 (sublimes) | −42.1 | −29.4 |
| Critical Temp (°C) | 112.0 | 96.1 | 101.1 | 133.4 | 31.0 | 96.7 | 94.7 |
| Critical Pressure (bar) | 41.4 | 49.9 | 40.6 | 113.3 | 73.8 | 42.5 | 33.8 |
| $h_{fg}$ at 0 °C (kJ/kg) | 152.0 | 195.4 | 198.0 | 1260 | (sub-critical) | 372.0 | 167.0 |
| ODP | 1.0 | 0.055 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP (100-yr) | 10900 | 1810 | 1430 | 0 | 1 | ~3 | 4 |
| Safety Group (ASHRAE 34) | A1 | A1 | A1 | B2L | A1 | A3 | A2L |
| Toxicity | Low | Low | Low | High (B2) | Low | Low | Low |
| Flammability | No | No | No | Mild | No | **Yes** | Mild |
| Oil Miscibility | High | High | Limited (needs POE) | Immiscible | Limited | High | Limited |
| Typical Application | Legacy (banned) | Old AC | Domestic/Automotive | Industrial cold storage | Trans-critical | Domestic fridge | Automotive R-1234yf |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** R-717 (ammonia) has $h_{fg}$ about **6× higher** than R-134a per unit mass, which is why industrial ammonia plants use *very small* mass flow rates and *very small* piping. The trade-off is its toxicity (B2 classification) and mild flammability (L denotes very low burning velocity).

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — GWP of a Zeotropic Blend (KTU Calculation)

A **zeotropic blend** like R-407C is a non-azeotropic mixture. The GWP is the mass-weighted sum of component GWPs:

$$GWP_{blend} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot GWP_i$$

where $x_i$ is the mass fraction of component $i$.

**Worked Example — R-407C (R-32 / R-125 / R-134a in 23/25/52 mass %):**

$$\begin{aligned}
GWP_{407C} &= (0.23 \times 675) + (0.25 \times 3500) + (0.52 \times 1430) \\
&= 155.25 + 875.0 + 743.6 \\
&= 1773.85 \approx 1774
\end{aligned}$$

The widely tabulated value is **GWP = 1774** — confirms the formula.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Hierarchical Classification of Refrigerants

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a refrigerant. List any four desirable thermodynamic properties of an ideal refrigerant.
**[KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

A refrigerant is a working fluid used in a refrigeration system that absorbs heat at low temperature by evaporating and rejects heat at high temperature by condensing, thereby producing a cooling effect.

Four desirable thermodynamic properties:

1. **Low boiling point** at atmospheric pressure, so that it evaporates at the desired low temperature.
2. **High latent heat of vaporization** to minimise the mass flow rate required for a given cooling load.
3. **Low specific volume of vapour** at compressor suction to reduce the swept volume of the compressor.
4. **Moderate condensing pressure**, neither too high (heavy vessels) nor too low (air ingress / moisture issues).
5. *(Bonus)* Low specific heat ratio $\gamma$, so that the compression work is small.

---

### Q2. What is ODP and GWP? Mention the ODP value of R-22 and R-134a.
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**Model Answer:**

- **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** is a relative index that measures the ability of a refrigerant molecule to destroy stratospheric ozone, normalised to R-11 (CFC-11) which is assigned ODP = 1.0.
- **GWP (Global Warming Potential)** is a relative index that measures the radiative forcing of 1 kg of a refrigerant over 100 years, relative to 1 kg of CO₂ (GWP = 1).

| Refrigerant | ODP | GWP (100-yr) |
|---|---|---|
| R-22 (HCFC) | 0.055 | 1810 |
| R-134a (HFC) | 0.000 | 1430 |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify refrigerants in detail. Discuss the environmental impact of CFCs and HCFCs, and explain how the Montreal Protocol governs their phase-out.
**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the properties of R-134a, R-717 (ammonia), and R-744 (CO₂) as refrigerants. State one typical application of each.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (a) — Classification & Environmental Impact

**Classification of Refrigerants:**

1. **Primary Refrigerants:** Fluids that themselves undergo the phase change inside the cycle.
2. **Secondary Refrigerants:** Heat-transfer fluids (brine, glycol-water, air) that transport cooling from the primary loop to the load without phase change.

Primary refrigerants are further split into:

- **Natural refrigerants:**
  - Inorganic: R-717 (NH₃), R-744 (CO₂), R-718 (H₂O), R-729 (Air)
  - Organic: R-290 (propane), R-600a (isobutane), R-601a (pentane)
- **Synthetic refrigerants (Halo-carbons):**
  - **CFC (Chloro-Fluoro-Carbon):** Fully halogenated with Cl and F, e.g., R-12, R-11 — **highest ODP**, banned.
  - **HCFC (Hydro-Chloro-Fluoro-Carbon):** Contain H, Cl, F, e.g., R-22 — moderate ODP, phased out.
  - **HFC (Hydro-Fluoro-Carbon):** Cl-free, e.g., R-134a, R-410A — zero ODP but high GWP.
  - **HFO (Hydro-Fluoro-Olefin):** Unsaturated, e.g., R-1234yf — zero ODP, very low GWP.

**Environmental Impact:**

- **Ozone Depletion:** CFCs released into the atmosphere drift to the stratosphere. UV-C photolysis liberates Cl radicals, which catalytically destroy O₃ via the chain:

  $$\text{Cl} \cdot + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{ClO} \cdot + \text{O}_2$$
  $$\text{ClO} \cdot + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cl} \cdot + \text{O}_2$$

  Each Cl radical destroys ~100,000 ozone molecules.

- **Global Warming:** CFCs and HFCs are strong infrared absorbers, contributing to radiative forcing. GWP of R-12 = 10,900 and R-134a = 1,430.

**Montreal Protocol (1987):** An international treaty phasing out ODS:
- 1996: Developed countries stopped CFC production.
- 2010: HCFC phase-out began (99.5 % reduction by 2020 for developed, 2030 for developing).
- **Kigali Amendment (2016):** Extends the protocol to HFCs (first-generation F-gas regulation).

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (b) — Comparative Study

| Property | R-134a | R-717 (NH₃) | R-744 (CO₂) |
|---|---|---|---|
| ODP | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| GWP | 1430 | 0 | 1 |
| Boiling Point (°C) | −26.1 | −33.3 | −78.4 |
| Critical Temp (°C) | 101.1 | 133.4 | 31.0 |
| Latent Heat (kJ/kg) at 0 °C | 198 | 1260 | (subcritical) |
| Toxicity | Low (A1) | High (B2L) | Low (A1) |
| Flammability | No | Mild | No |
| Pressure at 30 °C cond (bar) | ~7.7 | ~11.7 | very high ~72 |
| Oil | POE | Mineral | POE |

**Applications:**
- **R-134a** → Domestic refrigerators, automobile AC (older), chillers.
- **R-717** → Industrial cold storage, ice plants, large-capacity ammonia absorption systems.
- **R-744** → Trans-critical CO₂ systems in supermarket booster refrigeration, heat-pump dryers, hot-water heat pumps.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of P–h and T–s diagrams, derive the expression for the COP of an ideal vapour-compression refrigeration cycle and explain why the actual COP is always less than the Carnot COP.
**(b) [7 Marks]** A refrigeration system uses R-134a as the refrigerant with evaporator temperature −10 °C and condenser temperature 40 °C. The refrigerant is dry-saturated at compressor inlet and saturated liquid at condenser exit. Calculate (i) refrigeration effect, (ii) compressor work, (iii) COP, (iv) mass flow rate for 5 TR of refrigeration capacity.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (a) — COP Derivation

(Refer to the complete derivation in **Section 3.1** above. Briefly:)

$$\boxed{COP_{ref} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}}$$

**Why $COP_{ref} < COP_{carnot}$:**

1. **Throttling is irreversible** — entropy increases across the expansion valve.
2. **Finite temperature difference** in evaporator and condenser — additional entropy generation.
3. **Superheat and sub-cooling** add to compression work.
4. **Pressure drops** in suction and discharge lines cause extra work.

Hence the *second-law efficiency* $\eta_{II} = COP_{actual} / COP_{carnot} < 1$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (b) — Numerical

**Given:**
- $T_{evap} = -10 \degree C$ → $h_1 = 392.30$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 1.7335$ kJ/kg·K (saturated vapour for R-134a)
- $T_{cond} = 40 \degree C$ → saturated liquid $h_3 = 256.41$ kJ/kg
- Isentropic compression: $s_2 = s_1 = 1.7335$ kJ/kg·K
- Capacity: 5 TR = 5 × 3.517 = 17.585 kW

**Step 1 — Find state 2 by interpolation on the R-134a superheat table at 40 °C condenser saturation pressure ≈ 10.16 bar.**

At 10.16 bar, $s_2 = 1.7335$ kJ/kg·K interpolating between 40 °C ($h = 271.71$ kJ/kg) and 50 °C ($h = 282.30$ kJ/kg) — for R-134a superheated table. Using a linear interpolation factor, we obtain $h_2 \approx 273.6$ kJ/kg (actual value depends on the reference table; for exam, accept $h_2 \approx 274$ kJ/kg).

**Step 2 — Throttling:** $h_4 = h_3 = 256.41$ kJ/kg.

**Step 3 — Refrigeration effect:**

$$q_L = h_1 - h_4 = 392.30 - 256.41 = 135.89 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 4 — Compressor work:**

$$w_c = h_2 - h_1 = 273.6 - 392.30 = -118.7 \text{ kJ/kg (work input)}$$

Magnitude: $|w_c| = 118.7$ kJ/kg.

**Step 5 — COP:**

$$COP_{ref} = \frac{135.89}{118.7} = 1.145$$

**Step 6 — Mass flow rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_L}{q_L} = \frac{17.585}{135.89} = 0.1294 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 7 — Compressor power:**

$$P_{comp} = \dot{m} \cdot w_c = 0.1294 \times 118.7 = 15.36 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly:
> 1. Forget to convert 5 TR → kW (1 TR = 3.517 kW). Lose 1 mark.
> 2. Confuse sign of $w_c$ — write the *magnitude* of work input; the negative sign indicates work is *done on* the system, not by it.
> 3. Use $T_L/(T_H - T_L)$ for COP — that's Carnot, not actual. Always use $h_1 - h_4 / (h_2 - h_1)$ for the vapour-compression cycle.
> 4. Forget the throttling relation $h_3 = h_4$ — losing 1 mark for not even showing the throttling step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Refrigerant** is the working fluid; **secondary refrigerant** is a carrier (brine, glycol).
- **Ideal refrigerant properties** = thermodynamic + chemical + physical + safety + environmental.
- **CFC** → high ODP, **banned** (R-12, R-11).
- **HCFC** → lower ODP, **phased out** (R-22).
- **HFC** → ODP = 0, but **GWP = 1430 for R-134a** (regulated under Kigali).
- **HFO** → 4th generation, low GWP (R-1234yf = 4).
- **Natural refrigerants** R-717 (NH₃), R-744 (CO₂), R-290, R-600a, R-718 — environmental champions.
- **ASHRAE 34 Safety Grouping:** A1 = low toxicity, no flame; B2L = higher toxicity, very low flame; A2L = low toxicity, mild flammability; A3 = low toxicity, higher flammability.
- **Key formulae to memorise:**
  - $q_L = h_1 - h_4$
  - $w_c = h_2 - h_1$
  - $COP = q_L / w_c$
  - $\dot{m} = \dot{Q}_L / q_L$
- **TEWI** = (Direct emissions × GWP) + (Indirect emissions from electricity × grid emission factor).
- **Montreal Protocol (1987) + Kigali Amendment (2016)** govern refrigerant phase-down globally.
- **TEWI** is the modern KTU metric to compare refrigerants on a complete life-cycle basis.
- **Pressure ratio** $P_2 / P_1$ directly affects discharge temperature and COP.
- **Refrigeration effect per kg** depends strongly on the saturation curve; R-717 wins on a per-kg basis, R-134a wins on system simplicity.
- Always **draw the P-h diagram** for any KTU numerical — it carries marks even if values are approximate.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychrometric Properties - Psychrometric Chart
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT414 - REFRIGERATION AND COLD CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Psychrometric Properties - Psychrometric Chart

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Psychrometry?

**Psychrometry** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the study of **thermodynamic properties of moist air** (a binary mixture of dry air and water vapour) and the processes involving changes in those properties. It is the foundational science behind all **air-conditioning, refrigeration, cold storage humidity control, and cold chain logistics** applications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> *"Psychrometry is the science that studies the thermodynamic properties of a mixture of dry air and water vapour, treated as a single ideal gas mixture, under conditions commonly encountered in air-conditioning and refrigeration engineering."*

## 1.2 Why Psychrometry Matters in Cold Chain Engineering

In cold chain applications (PEFTT414), psychrometry is **NOT optional** because:

- **Cold Storage Design**: Fruits, vegetables, pharmaceuticals, and frozen foods require **specific relative humidity ranges** to prevent desiccation (moisture loss) or microbial growth.
- **HVAC Load Calculation**: Sensible and latent cooling loads cannot be computed without psychrometric analysis.
- **Condensation Control**: Cold chain facilities suffer from condensation on walls/ceilings if the dew point is not properly managed.
- **Frozen Food Quality**: Ice crystal growth in frozen foods is governed by the moisture content in the surrounding air.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge and Steam" Model

Imagine a **dry kitchen sponge** (representing the dry air component) and **steam from a boiling kettle** (representing water vapour). The sponge can hold a certain amount of steam before it becomes "saturated" and starts dripping.

- **Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT)** → The temperature of the mixture measured by a normal thermometer (the overall warmth).
- **Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)** → What a thermometer reads when its bulb is wrapped in a wet wick (the sponge is "sweating").
- **Relative Humidity (RH)** → How "full" the sponge is. **100% RH** = fully saturated; **0% RH** = bone dry.
- **Dew Point Temperature (DPT)** → The temperature at which the sponge (air) becomes so full that water starts condensing out.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** On a humid Kerala monsoon day, your clothes don't dry quickly because the air sponge is already full of moisture (high RH). On a dry winter day in North India, the same clothes dry in hours because the air sponge has high "absorption capacity" (low RH).

## 1.4 Composition of Moist Air

Atmospheric air is treated as a **binary mixture**:

$$\text{Moist Air} = \text{Dry Air} + \text{Water Vapour}$$

- **Dry Air Composition (by volume):** $\text{N}_2 \approx 78\%$, $\text{O}_2 \approx 21\%$, $\text{Ar} \approx 0.93\%$, $\text{CO}_2 \approx 0.03\%$, others $\approx 0.04\%$
- **Molecular Weight of Dry Air:** $M_a = 28.97$ **kg/kmol**
- **Molecular Weight of Water Vapour:** $M_v = 18.02$ **kg/kmol**
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R_u = 8.314$ **kJ/(kmol·K)**
- **Gas Constant for Dry Air:** $R_a = 0.287$ **kJ/(kg·K)**
- **Gas Constant for Water Vapour:** $R_v = 0.4615$ **kJ/(kg·K)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Assumption (Dalton's Law):** Both dry air and water vapour occupy the same volume and are at the same temperature. The total pressure is the **sum of partial pressures**: $P_t = P_a + P_v$.

---

## 1.5 The Twelve Fundamental Psychrometric Properties

For the KTU 2024 syllabus, mastery of these **twelve properties** is mandatory. Each is a "coordinate" that locates a point on the psychrometric chart.

| # | Property | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|----------|--------|---------------|
| 1 | Dry Bulb Temperature | DBT ($T_{db}$) | °C |
| 2 | Wet Bulb Temperature | WBT ($T_{wb}$) | °C |
| 3 | Dew Point Temperature | DPT ($T_{dp}$) | °C |
| 4 | Specific Humidity (Humidity Ratio) | $\omega$ | kg water/kg dry air |
| 5 | Relative Humidity | $\phi$ or RH | % |
| 6 | Specific Enthalpy | $h$ | kJ/kg dry air |
| 7 | Specific Volume | $v$ | m³/kg dry air |
| 8 | Partial Pressure of Water Vapour | $P_v$ | kPa or bar |
| 9 | Partial Pressure of Dry Air | $P_a$ | kPa or bar |
| 10 | Degree of Saturation | $\mu$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Humidity Ratio at Saturation | $\omega_s$ | kg/kg dry air |
| 12 | Total Pressure | $P_t$ | kPa (assumed 101.325 at sea level) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Property Definitions and Physical Meaning

### A. Dry Bulb Temperature (DBT) — $T_{db}$
The temperature of moist air measured by a **standard mercury-in-glass or RTD thermometer** whose bulb is **dry and exposed** to the air stream. It is the **true thermodynamic temperature** of the air-vapour mixture. DBT is the **x-axis** of the psychrometric chart.

### B. Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT) — $T_{wb}$
The temperature a parcel of air would have if it were **cooled adiabatically to saturation by evaporation of water into it**. Measured by a thermometer with a **wet wick (muslin cloth)** covering the bulb, with air flowing over it at $\ge 4$ m/s.

> [!NOTE]
> **WBT and DBT — The Crucial Difference:**
> - At **100% RH** (saturated air): $T_{wb} = T_{db} = T_{dp}$
> - At **lower RH**: $T_{wb} < T_{db}$
> - **WBT approaches DBT as air dries out? NO** — actually, as RH drops, the difference $(T_{db} - T_{wb})$ **increases**.

### C. Dew Point Temperature (DPT) — $T_{dp}$
The temperature at which water vapour in the air becomes **saturated** (i.e., begins to condense) when cooled at **constant pressure and constant humidity ratio**. Below $T_{dp}$, condensation occurs (fog, dew, frost on evaporator coils).

### D. Specific Humidity (Humidity Ratio) — $\omega$
The mass of water vapour **per unit mass of dry air** in the mixture. This is **NOT** the same as relative humidity.

$$\omega = \frac{m_v}{m_a} \quad [\text{kg water vapour / kg dry air}]$$

### E. Relative Humidity — $\phi$
The ratio of the **actual partial pressure of water vapour** to the **saturation pressure of water vapour** at the same DBT.

$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T_{db})} \times 100\%$$

### F. Specific Enthalpy — $h$
The total energy content per kg of **dry air** in the mixture, measured from a reference state ($0$ °C for both dry air and water).

$$h = c_{pa} \cdot T_{db} + \omega \cdot (h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot T_{db})$$

### G. Specific Volume — $v$
The volume occupied per **kg of dry air**. Decreases with humidity (water vapour is lighter than dry air per kmol).

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Board-Exam Master Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL CONSTANTS for KTU Numerical Problems (Memorize these):**
> - $c_{pa} = 1.005$ **kJ/(kg·K)** (specific heat of dry air)
> - $c_{pv} = 1.88$ **kJ/(kg·K)** (specific heat of water vapour)
> - $c_{pw} = 4.186$ **kJ/(kg·K)** (specific heat of liquid water)
> - $h_{fg,0} = 2501$ **kJ/kg** (latent heat of vaporization at 0 °C)
> - $h_{fg} = 2501 - 2.381 \cdot T_{db}$ **kJ/kg** (at DBT $T_{db}$ in °C)
> - $P_t = 101.325$ **kPa** (standard atmospheric pressure at sea level)

| # | Property | Governing Equation | Units |
|---|----------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Humidity Ratio** (from partial pressure) | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$ | kg/kg da |
| 2 | **Saturation Humidity Ratio** | $\omega_s = 0.622 \cdot \dfrac{P_{vs}(T_{db})}{P_t - P_{vs}(T_{db})}$ | kg/kg da |
| 3 | **Relative Humidity** | $\phi = \dfrac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T_{db})}$ | fraction |
| 4 | **Degree of Saturation** | $\mu = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_s}$ | fraction |
| 5 | **Specific Enthalpy** | $h = 1.005 T_{db} + \omega(2501 + 1.88 T_{db})$ | kJ/kg da |
| 6 | **Specific Volume (per kg da)** | $v = \dfrac{R_a T_k}{P_a} = \dfrac{0.287 T_k}{P_t - P_v}$ | m³/kg da |
| 7 | **Dew Point (implicit)** | $T_{dp} : P_{vs}(T_{dp}) = P_v$ | °C |
| 8 | **Wet Bulb (energy balance)** | $c_{pa}(T_{wb}-T_{db}) = (h_{fg,wb})\cdot(\omega_s - \omega)$ | °C |
| 9 | **Partial Pressure of Dry Air** | $P_a = P_t - P_v$ | kPa |
| 10 | **Gas Law for Dry Air** | $P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T_k$ | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **TYPING SAFEGUARD NOTE:** All absolute-value or "given" separators in the table above use the word *"fraction"* or plain English — never the **pipe symbol** `vert` in row content, as it breaks the markdown table parser. The LaTeX `\dfrac` is used inside math-mode `$$` blocks only.

---

## 2.3 Saturation Pressure of Water Vapour — $P_{vs}$

Since $P_{vs}$ appears in nearly every formula, KTU problems typically **provide a steam-table excerpt** or expect interpolation. Two methods exist:

### Method 1: Direct Steam Table Lookup
Use the saturation pressure table at the given $T_{db}$ (interpolating between adjacent rows).

### Method 2: Approximate Correlation (Antoine-type)
For quick estimates (valid 0–60 °C):

$$P_{vs} \approx 0.61078 \cdot \exp\!\left(\dfrac{17.27 \cdot T_{db}}{T_{db} + 237.3}\right) \quad [\text{kPa}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Magnus formula** — useful for sanity checks but KTU board problems **always provide steam-table data** in the question paper.

## 2.4 Physical Significance of Each Property

- **DBT** → governs **sensible heat transfer**; read directly.
- **WBT** → governs **evaporative cooling potential**; used in cooling tower & adiabatic saturation analysis.
- **DPT** → governs **condensation onset**; critical for cold-room wall design.
- **$\omega$** → governs **latent heat load**; mass of moisture to be removed.
- **$\phi$** → governs **human comfort** and **product preservation quality**.
- **$h$** → governs **total energy balance** of the air-handling unit.
- **$v$** → governs **air-handling unit (AHU) sizing** and fan power.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Critical Property |
|----------|-------------|-------------------|
| Cold Storage (Fruits) | Prevent weight loss | $\phi = 85-95\%$ |
| Pharmaceutical Cold Chain | Prevent drug degradation | $T_{db} = 2-8$ °C, $\phi$ controlled |
| Frozen Food Storage | Prevent ice recrystallization | $T_{db} \le -18$ °C |
| HVAC (Human Comfort) | ASHRAE comfort zone | $T_{db} = 22-26$ °C, $\phi = 40-60\%$ |
| Data Centre Cooling | Prevent static discharge | $\phi = 40-55\%$ |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Humidity Ratio Formula

### Starting Point — Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures

Let $m_a$ = mass of dry air, $m_v$ = mass of water vapour, $V$ = total volume, $T_k$ = absolute temperature.

Applying ideal gas law to each component:

$$P_a \cdot V = \frac{m_a}{M_a} R_u T_k$$

$$P_v \cdot V = \frac{m_v}{M_v} R_u T_k$$

**Step 1:** Divide the second equation by the first to eliminate $V$, $R_u$, and $T_k$:

$$\frac{P_v}{P_a} = \frac{m_v / M_v}{m_a / M_a} = \frac{m_v}{m_a} \cdot \frac{M_a}{M_v}$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange to isolate $m_v / m_a$ (the definition of $\omega$):

$$\frac{m_v}{m_a} = \omega = \frac{P_v}{P_a} \cdot \frac{M_v}{M_a}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $P_a = P_t - P_v$ (Dalton's Law) and $M_v / M_a = 18.02 / 28.97 = 0.622$:

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}}$$

**Step 4:** Engineering Interpretation — The factor **0.622** is the molecular mass ratio. Since water vapour is **lighter** per molecule, a given partial pressure contributes **less mass** than dry air, hence the factor < 1.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Specific Enthalpy Formula

### Reference State Definition

Choose reference: dry air at 0 °C, liquid water at 0 °C → $h_a = 0$, $h_{w,0} = 0$.

### Enthalpy of the Mixture

Consider 1 kg of dry air + $\omega$ kg of water vapour at DBT $T_{db}$ (°C).

**Component 1 — Sensible heat of dry air:**

$$h_a = c_{pa} \cdot T_{db} = 1.005 \, T_{db} \quad [\text{kJ/kg da}]$$

**Component 2 — Enthalpy of water vapour (vaporization + sensible heating):**

$$h_v = h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} \cdot T_{db} = 2501 + 1.88 \, T_{db} \quad [\text{kJ/kg vapour}]$$

**Step 3 — Total enthalpy per kg of dry air:**

$$h = h_a + \omega \cdot h_v = c_{pa} T_{db} + \omega(h_{fg,0} + c_{pv} T_{db})$$

$$\boxed{h = 1.005\, T_{db} + \omega(2501 + 1.88\, T_{db}) \quad [\text{kJ/kg dry air}]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most-tested formula in KTU PEFTT414 Module 1** — expect a 14-mark question asking you to compute $\omega$, $h$, and $v$ for a given DBT + RH condition.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Specific Volume Formula

### Volume Per kg of Dry Air

From ideal gas law applied to dry air only:

$$P_a \cdot V = m_a \cdot R_a \cdot T_k$$

For $m_a = 1$ kg:

$$v = \frac{V}{m_a} = \frac{R_a \cdot T_k}{P_a}$$

Substituting $P_a = P_t - P_v$ and $R_a = 0.287$ kJ/(kg·K):

$$v = \frac{0.287 \cdot T_k}{P_t - P_v} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{kg da}]$$

where $T_k = T_{db} + 273.15$ in Kelvin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Many students forget that specific volume is **per kg of dry air**, not per kg of moist air. This is a **2-mark trap** in KTU valuation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Full KTU Board-Style Problem

### Problem Statement

> Moist air at $P_t = 101.325$ kPa has DBT = 35 °C and RH = 60%. Compute:
> (a) Humidity ratio $\omega$
> (b) Specific enthalpy $h$
> (c) Specific volume $v$
> (d) Dew point temperature $T_{dp}$
>
> Given: $P_{vs}(35°C) = 5.6291$ kPa

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Part (a): Humidity Ratio**

**Step 1:** Find partial pressure of vapour:
$$P_v = \phi \cdot P_{vs} = 0.60 \times 5.6291 = 3.3775 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply humidity ratio formula:
$$\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{P_v}{P_t - P_v}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{3.3775}{101.325 - 3.3775}$$

$$\omega = 0.622 \times \frac{3.3775}{97.9475} = 0.622 \times 0.03448$$

$$\boxed{\omega = 0.02145 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}}$$

**Part (b): Specific Enthalpy**

**Step 1:** Compute $h_{fg}$ at DBT:
$$h_{fg}(35°C) = 2501 - 2.381 \times 35 = 2501 - 83.335 = 2417.67 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Step 2:** Apply enthalpy equation:
$$h = 1.005 \times 35 + 0.02145 \times (2501 + 1.88 \times 35)$$

$$h = 35.175 + 0.02145 \times (2501 + 65.8)$$

$$h = 35.175 + 0.02145 \times 2566.8$$

$$h = 35.175 + 55.06$$

$$\boxed{h = 90.23 \text{ kJ/kg dry air}}$$

**Part (c): Specific Volume**

**Step 1:** Convert DBT to Kelvin:
$$T_k = 35 + 273.15 = 308.15 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2:** Apply specific volume equation:
$$v = \frac{0.287 \times 308.15}{101.325 - 3.3775}$$

$$v = \frac{88.439}{97.9475}$$

$$\boxed{v = 0.9030 \text{ m}^3/\text{kg dry air}}$$

**Part (d): Dew Point Temperature**

**Step 1:** Identify principle — at dew point, $P_{vs}(T_{dp}) = P_v = 3.3775$ kPa.

**Step 2:** Interpolate from steam table:

| $T$ (°C) | $P_{vs}$ (kPa) |
|----------|----------------|
| 25 | 3.1698 |
| 26 | 3.3639 |
| 27 | 3.5670 |

**Step 3:** Linear interpolation between 25 °C and 26 °C:

$$T_{dp} = 25 + \frac{3.3775 - 3.1698}{3.3639 - 3.1698} \times 1$$

$$T_{dp} = 25 + \frac{0.2077}{0.1941} \times 1 = 25 + 1.07$$

$$\boxed{T_{dp} \approx 26.07 \text{ °C}}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Master Reference Table for Problem Solving

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Mapping** — Which Formula to Apply for a Given Question Type

| Given Information | Find | Direct Formula Path |
|------------------|------|---------------------|
| DBT, RH | $\omega$ | $\phi \to P_v \to \omega$ |
| DBT, RH | $h$ | Compute $\omega$, then $h$ equation |
| DBT, RH | $v$ | Compute $P_v$, then $v$ equation |
| DBT, RH | $T_{dp}$ | $\phi \to P_v \to$ interpolate $P_{vs}^{-1}$ |
| DBT, $\omega$ | RH | $\omega \to P_v \to \phi = P_v/P_{vs}(T_{db})$ |
| DBT, $T_{wb}$ | $\omega$ | Energy balance on WBT (involves iteration) |
| DBT, DPT | $\omega$ | $P_v = P_{vs}(T_{dp}) \to \omega$ |
