# Section 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Evaporative Condensers

### 1.1.1 Cooling Tower

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Cooling Tower** is an evaporative heat rejection device that transfers waste heat from the condenser water to the atmosphere by exploiting the latent heat of vaporization of a small portion of the circulating water. It is essentially a **direct-contact air-water heat exchanger** in which cooling is achieved primarily by **evaporative cooling**, supplemented by **sensible heat transfer** to the air stream.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — The Sweating Pitcher:**
> Imagine pouring cold water into a clay pot (matka) on a hot summer day. The outer surface "sweats" and the water inside becomes ice-cold. Why? Because the water that evaporates from the pot's surface absorbs its latent heat of vaporization **from the remaining water**, cooling it down. A cooling tower does **exactly the same thing** — it deliberately evaporates a tiny fraction (≈ 1.5 % – 2 %) of the circulating water to dump a huge chunk of heat into the atmosphere. The fan, packing, and pumps are simply engineering tools to **accelerate the natural sweating process** of that clay pot by orders of magnitude.

**Standard Engineering Metrics (memorize for KTU):**
- **Approach** = Cold water temperature (leaving tower) − Wet-bulb temperature of air
- **Range** = Hot water temperature (entering tower) − Cold water temperature (leaving tower)
- **Effectiveness (ε)** = Range / (Range + Approach) = Range / (Hot water − WBT)
- **L/G ratio** = Mass flow rate of water ÷ Mass flow rate of air

### 1.1.2 Spray Pond

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Spray Pond** is a natural-draft, open-air evaporative cooling installation in which warm condenser water is sprayed vertically upward through a set of stationary nozzles. Cooling is achieved by intimate mixing of water droplets with ambient air, with **no fan assistance** and **no fill/packing material**.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Garden Hose Mist:**
> On a dry, hot day, a fine mist from a garden hose nozzle makes the surrounding air feel cool. The droplets flash-evaporate, drawing their latent heat from the air. A spray pond exploits this exact principle at industrial scale — millions of tiny water droplets, suspended momentarily in air, dump their heat to the sky. However, since no fan forces the air through the spray, performance is **highly weather-dependent** and **inefficient** compared to a cooling tower.

---

## 1.2 Evaporator Classification

### 1.2.1 Flooded Evaporator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Flooded Evaporator** is a shell-and-tube (or shell-and-coil) type evaporator in which the **entire shell side is completely filled with liquid refrigerant** up to a controlled level. The refrigerant evaporation takes place on the **outside** of the tubes, while the chilled fluid (water/brine) flows inside the tubes.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Deep-Fried Vada in Batter:**
> When you drop a *vada* into a deep vessel of batter, the *vada* is completely **submerged** in the liquid on all sides. Heat transfer is extremely efficient because every square inch of the *vada* surface is in contact with the hot medium. A flooded evaporator does the same: every square inch of the chilled-water tubes is bathed in boiling refrigerant, giving **maximum heat transfer coefficient**.

### 1.2.2 Dry Expansion Evaporator

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> A **Dry Expansion (DX) Evaporator** is a direct-expansion type evaporator in which the refrigerant **does not fill** the evaporator. Instead, a **liquid-vapor mixture** enters at the inlet, progressively boils as it flows through, and exits at the outlet mostly as vapor (with some superheat). The dryness fraction progressively increases from inlet to outlet.

#### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Tea Kettle on Stove:**
> When you boil water in a kettle, you do not fill the kettle with cold water; you pour a measured quantity. As the flame heats the bottom, the water at the bottom vaporizes first, creating a **mixture of liquid and vapor** that bubbles upward. By the time the steam exits the spout, almost all the water has turned to vapor. A dry expansion evaporator behaves the same way — it carries a **2-phase mixture** that progressively dries out along the length of the tube.

---

## 1.3 Refrigeration Controls

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Refrigeration Controls** are the **"brain and gatekeeper"** of any vapor-compression system. They perform two crucial jobs:
> 1. **Meter the refrigerant flow** from the high-pressure liquid side to the low-pressure evaporator side.
> 2. **Maintain the system at design operating conditions** (correct liquid level, correct superheat, or correct evaporator pressure).

### 1.3.1 Low-Side Float Valve (LSV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Low-Side Float Valve** is a modulating expansion device that **maintains a constant liquid refrigerant level inside the flooded evaporator**. The float rises/falls with the liquid level and throttles the refrigerant inlet to match the rate of evaporation.

**Analogy:** Like the **ballcock valve in a toilet tank** — as water drains, the ball drops, opening the valve until the tank is full again.

### 1.3.2 High-Side Float Valve (HSV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **High-Side Float Valve** is fitted on the **high-pressure side** — either in the condenser or the receiver — and maintains a **constant level of condensed liquid refrigerant**, ensuring that only liquid (not vapor) flows to the expansion device.

**Analogy:** Like the **petcock in a gasoline storage drum** — it ensures you never accidentally pour vapor (air) into the engine.

### 1.3.3 Capillary Tube

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Capillary Tube** is a fixed-bore, fixed-length, small-diameter copper tube that acts as a **passive expansion device**. Pressure drop is achieved purely by **fluid friction** along its length. It has no moving parts, is non-adjustable, and is widely used in **small hermetic systems** (domestic refrigerators, window ACs).

**Analogy:** Like the **narrow drinking straw** in a juice box — liquid must squeeze through a long, thin passage, losing pressure as it goes.

### 1.3.4 Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV / TEV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** A **Thermostatic Expansion Valve** is a **modulating, superheat-controlled** expansion device. The bulb senses the suction-line temperature at the evaporator outlet; a pressure imbalance actuates the needle valve, modulating the refrigerant flow to maintain a **constant superheat** (typically 4 °C – 8 °C).

**Analogy:** Like a **car's automatic cruise control** — it continuously adjusts the throttle (valve opening) to keep the speed (superheat) steady, regardless of the slope of the road (load).

### 1.3.5 Automatic Expansion Valve (AXV)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** An **Automatic Expansion Valve** is a **modulating, pressure-controlled** expansion device. It uses a spring-loaded diaphragm sensing the **evaporator pressure** directly (not superheat). It maintains a **constant evaporator pressure**, but the **superheat varies with load**.

**Analogy:** Like a **thermostat-controlled shower** — it keeps the water pressure constant, but the temperature (superheat) can vary if you suddenly turn on the geyser elsewhere in the house.

---

## 1.4 Visual Summary — Where Each Control Fits

| Control | Location in Cycle | Sensing Variable | Maintains |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LSV** | Between receiver & flooded evap. | Liquid level in evaporator | Constant level |
| **HSV** | In condenser/receiver | Liquid level on high side | Constant level |
| **Capillary Tube** | Between condenser & evap. | None (passive) | Fixed flow |
| **TXV** | Between condenser & evap. | Bulb temp. at evap. outlet | Constant superheat |
| **AXV** | Between condenser & evap. | Evaporator pressure | Constant pressure |

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cooling Tower — Theory of Operation

### 2.1.1 Mass and Energy Balance
The cooling tower is analyzed as a **steady-flow, adiabatic, direct-contact heat exchanger** between air and water. Two parallel streams exchange heat and mass:

- **Air stream:** gains water vapor + sensible heat
- **Water stream:** loses mass (a small fraction evaporates) + sensible heat

For a control volume around the entire tower:

$$\dot{m}_{w,in} = \dot{m}_{w,out} + \dot{m}_{evap}$$

The energy balance on the air-side is:

$$\dot{m}_a (h_{a2} - h_{a1}) = \dot{m}_w c_{p,w} (T_{w1} - T_{w2}) + \dot{m}_{evap} \, h_{fg,w}$$

Where:
- $\dot{m}_a$ = mass flow rate of dry air
- $h_{a1}, h_{a2}$ = enthalpy of air at inlet and outlet
- $T_{w1}, T_{w2}$ = water temperature at inlet and outlet
- $h_{fg,w}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water at wet-bulb conditions (≈ 2400 kJ/kg)

### 2.1.2 Merkel's Theory (The Rigorous Approach)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Merkel's Method** (1925) is the **only** theoretically correct way to design a cooling tower. It integrates the **enthalpy potential** between the bulk water and the saturated air film at the water-air interface. Merkel defined an **NTU-type number** that accounts for both sensible and latent heat transfer simultaneously.

$$d\dot{q} = K \, dA \, (h_s - h_a)$$

Integrating over the entire packing height $A$:

$$\frac{KA}{L} = \int_{T_{w2}}^{T_{w1}} \frac{c_{p,w} \, dT_w}{h_s - h_a}$$

Where:
- $K$ = overall mass-transfer coefficient (kg/m²·s)
- $A$ = contact area (m²)
- $L$ = water mass flow rate (kg/s)
- $h_s$ = enthalpy of saturated air at water temperature $T_w$
- $h_a$ = enthalpy of unsaturated air in the bulk stream

### 2.1.3 Key Performance Metrics (KTU 2024)

**1. Range (R)**
$$R = T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}$$

**2. Approach (A)**
$$A = T_{w,out} - T_{wb,air}$$

**3. Effectiveness (ε)**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}}{T_{w,in} - T_{wb,air}} = \frac{R}{R + A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Range** is **not the same** as **Approach**. Range is a measure of how much **heat was rejected** (water-side metric). Approach is a measure of **how close to the theoretical limit** (ambient wet-bulb) the tower can cool (a thermodynamic metric).

### 2.1.4 Make-up Water & Drift Loss
> [!NOTE]
> A typical cooling tower loses water through three mechanisms:
> 1. **Evaporation loss** ≈ 0.85 % of circulation rate per 10 °C range
> 2. **Drift loss** ≈ 0.005 % to 0.02 % (entrained droplets escaping)
> 3. **Blowdown loss** = deliberate discharge to control TDS (Total Dissolved Solids)
>
> **Cycles of Concentration (COC)** = TDS in circulating water ÷ TDS in make-up water

### 2.1.5 Classifications of Cooling Towers

| Type | Air-driving Force | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Natural Draft** (Atmospheric) | Wind + density difference | Small industrial, where land is cheap |
| **Natural Draft (Hyperbolic)** | Chimney effect (density) | Large power plants, nuclear stations |
| **Mechanical Induced Draft** | Fan at top, pulling air | Most common industrial type |
| **Mechanical Forced Draft** | Fan at bottom, pushing air | Older plants, lower cost |
| **Counter-flow** | Air flows upward, water downward | Highest performance |
| **Cross-flow** | Air flows horizontally across falling water | Lower static resistance |

---

## 2.2 Spray Pond — Theory of Operation

### 2.2.1 Mechanism
The spray pond relies on the **height of the spray plume** (typically 5 – 7 m) to give droplets time to exchange heat with ambient air. The droplets:

1. Fall through ambient air → sensible cooling.
2. Partially evaporate → evaporative cooling (dominant effect).

> [!NOTE]
> **No fill / no packing** → the contact time is short and uncontrollable. Hence the **approach is large (8 °C – 15 °C)** and the tower is **incapable of approaching the wet-bulb** closer than a mechanical tower can.

### 2.2.2 Limitation
- Approach is **3× to 4× worse** than an induced-draft tower.
- Performance **degrades sharply** in humid weather (since the wet-bulb is high).
- Risk of **drift** in windy conditions → loss of water and potential Legionella issues.

---

## 2.3 Flooded vs. Dry Expansion Evaporator — Comparative Analysis

| Parameter | Flooded Evaporator | Dry Expansion (DX) |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerant fill | **Complete** (shell fully flooded) | **Partial** (inlet is liquid-vapor mix) |
| Evaporator inlet condition | Saturated liquid | Liquid-vapor mixture (typically quality x = 0.15 – 0.25) |
| Evaporator outlet condition | Saturated (or slightly superheated) vapor | Superheated vapor |
| Heat transfer coefficient | **High** (complete wetting) | Lower (varies with dryness fraction) |
| Compressor liquid slugging risk | **High** (if level control fails) | Low |
| Capacity modulation | Excellent (large wetted area) | Poor at part loads |
| Expansion device used | **Low-side float valve** | **Capillary / TXV / AXV** |
| Typical application | Large industrial chillers, ammonia systems | Domestic fridges, window ACs, package units |
| Refrigerant charge | **Very large** | Relatively small |

---

## 2.4 Refrigeration Controls — Operating Logic & Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following four critical control devices. The matrix below is a **must-memorize summary** for the 14-mark ESE question.

### 2.4.1 Capillary Tube — Hagen–Poiseuille Derivation

For a **fixed-bore capillary tube** of length $L$ and diameter $D$, the refrigerant flow is governed by viscous friction. Assuming isenthalpic (throttling) flow, applying Bernoulli with friction:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \rho_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho_1 \left( f \frac{L}{D} + K_{entrant} + K_{exit} \right)}}$$

Simplified form used in practice:

$$\dot{m} = C_D \cdot A_c \sqrt{2\rho_1(P_1 - P_2)}$$

Where:
- $C_D$ = discharge coefficient (≈ 0.7 – 0.85)
- $A_c$ = cross-section of capillary = $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$
- $P_1 - P_2$ = pressure drop across the capillary
- $\rho_1$ = density of liquid refrigerant at inlet

> [!WARNING]
> The flow through a capillary tube is **not linearly proportional** to $\sqrt{P_1 - P_2}$. As pressure drops, **flash gas forms** at the tube inlet (choked flow), which **drastically reduces** the mass flow rate.

### 2.4.2 Thermostatic Expansion Valve (TXV) — Force Balance

The TXV operates on a **three-force balance** at the diaphragm:

$$P_{bulb} = P_{evap} + P_{spring}$$

Expanded:

$$P_{sat}(T_{bulb}) = P_{evap} + P_{spring}$$

When the load increases → suction line gets warmer → bulb pressure rises → diaphragm lifts → more refrigerant flows → superheat returns to setpoint.

**Superheat definition:**

$$SH = T_{evap,out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap})$$

### 2.4.3 Automatic Expansion Valve (AXV) — Operating Logic

$$P_{spring} + P_{evap} = P_{line} \text{ (at diaphragm)}$$

The spring is **adjustable** by an external screw. The valve **opens** when $P_{evap}$ drops below the spring setting, and **closes** when $P_{evap}$ exceeds it.

### 2.4.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{R}{R+A}$ | ε dimensionless; R, A in °C | Tower effectiveness |
| 2 | $R = T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}$ | T in °C or K | Range (water-side cooling) |
| 3 | $A = T_{w,out} - T_{wb}$ | T in °C | Approach (gap from WBT) |
| 4 | $L/G = \dfrac{\dot{m}_w}{\dot{m}_a}$ | kg/s | Water-to-air ratio |
| 5 | $\dot{q} = \dot{m}_a (h_{a2} - h_{a1})$ | kW | Heat rejected to air |
| 6 | $\dot{m}_{cap} = C_D A_c \sqrt{2\rho_1 \Delta P}$ | kg/s | Capillary mass flow |
| 7 | $SH = T_{out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap})$ | K or °C | Superheat |
| 8 | $P_{bulb} = P_{evap} + P_{spring}$ | kPa or bar | TXV force balance |
| 9 | $COC = \dfrac{TDS_{circ}}{TDS_{makeup}}$ | dimensionless | Cycles of concentration |
| 10 | $\dot{m}_{evap} \approx 0.00085 \cdot \dot{m}_w \cdot R$ | kg/s | Evaporation loss (per 10 °C range rule) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's Real-World Utility:** The Range / Approach / Effectiveness triplet is the **design specification language** of every cooling-tower data sheet worldwide. When a chemical plant asks for a tower with *Range 8 °C and Approach 4 °C*, the engineer immediately knows to design around a wet-bulb of (cold-water − 4 °C) and a water-flow rate that absorbs the process heat with 8 °C drop.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are **exhaustive, line-by-line, and fully worked out** to KTU board-exam standard. No step has been skipped. Use these as your **model answer template** for Part B (14-mark) derivations.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capillary Tube Mass Flow Rate

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup
Derive an expression for the steady-state mass flow rate of refrigerant through a capillary tube of length $L$ and diameter $D$, given the upstream pressure $P_1$ and downstream pressure $P_2$. Assume incompressible laminar flow with entrance and exit losses.

### 3.1.2 Solution

**Step 1: Apply the steady-flow energy equation (Bernoulli with losses).**

For a horizontal capillary tube (no elevation change), ignoring velocity differences at the two ends for simplicity:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{f L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} + K_{entrant} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} + K_{exit} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

**Step 2: Group all loss terms into a single resistance coefficient.**

Define the **total loss coefficient**:

$$K_{tot} = f \frac{L}{D} + K_{entrant} + K_{exit}$$

So:

$$P_1 - P_2 = K_{tot} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

**Step 3: Solve for velocity $v$.**

Rearranging:

$$v^2 = \frac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{K_{tot} \cdot \rho}$$

Taking the positive root (flow is in the downstream direction):

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{K_{tot} \cdot \rho}}$$

**Step 4: Convert velocity to mass flow rate.**

The cross-sectional area of the capillary is:

$$A_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

Therefore, the mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot A_c \cdot v = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 (P_1 - P_2)}{K_{tot} \cdot \rho}}$$

**Step 5: Simplify the expression.**

Pulling $\rho$ inside the square root:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho (P_1 - P_2)}{K_{tot}}}$$

**Step 6: Introduce a discharge coefficient $C_D$.**

In practice, the theoretical value overestimates the actual flow because of non-idealities (choked flow, flash gas, surface roughness, etc.). We therefore introduce $C_D$:

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = C_D \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho_1 (P_1 - P_2)}{f L/D + K_{entrant} + K_{exit}}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical engineering equation** for capillary-tube sizing. Note that $\rho_1$ is taken at the **inlet saturated liquid** condition. For a domestic R-134a system, $C_D$ is empirically determined to be in the range 0.7 – 0.85.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cooling Tower Effectiveness from First Principles

### 3.2.1 Problem Setup
The cooling tower receives water at $T_{w,in} = 40$ °C and discharges it at $T_{w,out} = 30$ °C. The ambient air has a wet-bulb temperature of $T_{wb} = 25$ °C. Calculate the **Range**, **Approach**, and **Effectiveness** of the tower.

### 3.2.2 Solution

**Step 1: Compute the Range (R).**

$$R = T_{w,in} - T_{w,out} = 40 - 30 = 10 \, °C$$

**Step 2: Compute the Approach (A).**

$$A = T_{w,out} - T_{wb} = 30 - 25 = 5 \, °C$$

**Step 3: Compute the Effectiveness (ε).**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{R}{R + A} = \frac{10}{10 + 5} = \frac{10}{15} = 0.6667 \text{ or } 66.67\%$$

**Step 4: Physical interpretation.**

> [!NOTE]
> The tower can cool water to a temperature that is **5 °C above the theoretical limit** (the wet-bulb). A well-designed induced-draft counter-flow tower has $\varepsilon \approx 0.7$ to 0.85 under favorable ambient conditions. Our result of 66.7 % indicates a **reasonably well-designed** mechanical-draft tower.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Make-Up Water Calculation

### 3.3.1 Problem Setup
A cooling tower circulates $\dot{m}_w = 100$ kg/s of water with a Range of 10 °C. The blowdown is set to maintain **Cycles of Concentration (COC) = 4**. The make-up water TDS is 200 ppm. Compute:
1. The evaporation loss
2. The blowdown loss
3. The total make-up water requirement.

### 3.3.2 Solution

**Step 1: Evaporation loss (per the 0.85 % per 10 °C rule).**

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = 0.0085 \times \dot{m}_w \times R = 0.0085 \times 100 \times 10 = 8.5 \text{ kg/s}$$

Alternatively, using the latent-heat-based exact formula:

$$\dot{m}_{evap} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{rej}}{h_{fg,w}} = \frac{\dot{m}_w c_{p,w} R}{h_{fg,w}} = \frac{100 \times 4.18 \times 10}{2400} = 1.742 \text{ kg/s}$$

We will use the **latent-heat method** (more accurate).

**Step 2: TDS mass balance to find blowdown.**

For a circulating system at steady state, the TDS entering with the make-up water must equal the TDS leaving via blowdown + drift. Ignoring drift:

$$\dot{m}_{makeup} \times TDS_{makeup} = \dot{m}_{blowdown} \times TDS_{circ}$$

Where $TDS_{circ} = COC \times TDS_{makeup} = 4 \times 200 = 800$ ppm.

So:

$$\dot{m}_{blowdown} = \frac{\dot{m}_{makeup} \times 200}{800} = \frac{\dot{m}_{makeup}}{4}$$

**Step 3: Mass balance on water.**

$$\dot{m}_{makeup} = \dot{m}_{evap} + \dot{m}_{blowdown} = 1.742 + \frac{\dot{m}_{makeup}}{4}$$

Solving for $\dot{m}_{makeup}$:

$$\dot{m}_{makeup} \left(1 - \frac{1}{4}\right) = 1.742$$

$$\dot{m}_{makeup} = \frac{1.742}{0.75} = 2.323 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute blowdown.**

$$\dot{m}_{blowdown} = \frac{2.323}{4} = 0.581 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5: Final summary.**

| Stream | Mass Flow Rate (kg/s) | % of Circulation |
|---|---|---|
| Circulation | 100.000 | 100.00 % |
| Evaporation | 1.742 | 1.74 % |
| Blowdown | 0.581 | 0.58 % |
| **Total Make-up** | **2.323** | **2.32 %** |

> [!NOTE]
> A **typical rule of thumb** for cooling tower make-up is 1.5 % – 2.5 % of circulation rate. Our result of 2.32 % is consistent with this engineering benchmark.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: TXV Superheat Setting Calculation

### 3.4.1 Problem Setup
A TXV has a **spring pressure equivalent** of $P_{spring} = 0.5$ bar (gauge). The evaporator pressure is $P_{evap} = 2.5$ bar (absolute). The bulb senses a temperature of $T_{bulb} = 5$ °C at the evaporator outlet. If the saturation temperature of R-134a at 2.5 bar is $T_{sat} = -5.2$ °C, calculate the **operating superheat** of the system.

### 3.4.2 Solution

**Step 1: Determine the bulb saturation pressure from its temperature.**

For R-134a at $T_{bulb} = 5$ °C (from standard property tables):

$$P_{bulb} = P_{sat}(5\,°C) = 2.45 \text{ bar (absolute)}$$

(You must quote the property-table value; KTU allows interpolation.)

**Step 2: Apply the TXV force-balance equation.**

The TXV is in equilibrium when:

$$P_{bulb} = P_{evap} + P_{spring}$$

Checking if this is satisfied:

$$P_{evap} + P_{spring} = 2.5 + 0.5 = 3.0 \text{ bar}$$

But $P_{bulb} = 2.45$ bar, which is **less than 3.0 bar**.

**Step 3: Interpret the imbalance.**

Since $P_{bulb} < (P_{evap} + P_{spring})$, the **net force on the diaphragm closes the valve slightly**, reducing refrigerant flow. This will cause:

- Evaporator outlet temperature to **drop** (less liquid is boiling off)
- Bulb pressure to **drop** further
- Eventually the valve reopens to a new equilibrium where the new bulb pressure matches $P_{evap} + P_{spring}$.

**Step 4: Equilibrium superheat calculation.**

At the new equilibrium, the bulb pressure equals 3.0 bar, so we look up the corresponding saturation temperature for R-134a at 3.0 bar:

$$T_{sat}(3.0 \text{ bar}) = +0.9 \, °C \text{ (interpolated from property tables)}$$

Therefore, the **equilibrium superheat** is:

$$SH = T_{bulb,eq} - T_{sat,evap} = 0.9 - (-5.2) = 6.1 \, °C$$

**Step 5: Final answer.**

$$\boxed{SH_{operating} = 6.1 \, °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU board answers must explicitly cite the **two key TXV equations**: (a) the force balance $P_{bulb} = P_{evap} + P_{spring}$ and (b) the definition $SH = T_{out} - T_{sat}(P_{evap})$. Skipping either costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Five Controls at a Glance

| Feature | LSV | HSV | Capillary | TXV | AXV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Type | Modulating | Modulating | Fixed | Modulating | Modulating |
| Sensing parameter | Liquid level | Liquid level | None | Superheat | Evaporator pressure |
| Output controlled | Liquid level | Liquid level | Mass flow | Superheat | Evaporator pressure |
| Moving parts | Yes (float) | Yes (float) | None | Yes (needle) | Yes (needle) |
| Adjustability | Limited | Limited | None (must change tube) | Yes (spring) | Yes (spring) |
| Application | Flooded evap. | Receiver / condenser | Domestic fridges, window AC | Commercial A/C, supermarkets | Older transport refrig. |
| Superheat behavior | Fixed at saturation | Fixed at saturation | Variable, load-dependent | **Constant** | **Variable** |
| Liquid slugging risk | High (if float fails) | Low | Low | Very low (TXV protects) | Low |
| Cost | Medium | Medium | **Lowest** | High | High |
| Maintenance | Periodic float check | Periodic float check | None (replace if choked) | Bulb & diaphragm check | Diaphragm & spring check |

---

## 4.3 Schematic 3: Flooded vs. Dry Expansion Evaporator — Comparative Block Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    F1["HIGH PRESSURE LIQUID FROM CONDENSER"] --> F2["LOW SIDE FLOAT VALVE LSV"]
    F2 --> F3["FLOODED EVAPORATOR SHELL"]
    F3 --> F4["LIQUID LEVEL CONTROLLED BY FLOAT"]
    F4 --> F5["BOILING ON TUBE EXTERIOR"]
    F5 --> F6["SUCTION VAPOR TO COMPRESSOR"]
    F3 --> F7["LIQUID REFRIGERANT IN SHELL"]

    D1["HIGH PRESSURE LIQUID FROM CONDENSER"] --> D2["THERMOSTATIC EXPANSION VALVE TXV"]
    D2 --> D3["DRY EXPANSION EVAPORATOR TUBES"]
    D3 --> D4["LIQUID VAPOR MIXTURE INSIDE TUBES"]
    D4 --> D5["PROGRESSIVE BOILING INCREASING QUALITY"]
    D5 --> D6["SUPERHEATED VAPOR EXIT"]
    D6 --> D7["SUCTION LINE TO COMPRESSOR"]
    D6 --> D8["BULB SENSES OUTLET TEMPERATURE"]
    D8 --> D2

    style F1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style F6 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D1 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D7 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.4 Schematic 4: Master Refrigeration Cycle with All Five Controls

```mermaid
graph LR
    M1["COMPRESSOR"] -->|DISCHARGE VAPOR HIGH P| M2["CONDENSER OR COOLING TOWER"]
    M2 -->|HIGH P LIQUID| M3["RECEIVER"]
    M3 -->|LIQUID| M4["EXPANSION DEVICE"]
    M4 -->|LOW P MIXTURE| M5["EVAPORATOR"]
    M5 -->|LOW P VAPOR| M1
    M5 -.->|BULB SIGNAL| M4
    M3 -.->|FLOAT SIGNAL HIGH SIDE| M4

    subgraph M6["FIVE EXPANSION CONTROLS"]
        X1["CAPILLARY TUBE FIXED"]
        X2["TXV SUPERHEAT SENSING"]
        X3["AXV PRESSURE SENSING"]
        X4["LSV FLOAT ON EVAP"]
        X5["HSV FLOAT ON RECEIVER"]
    end

    M4 --> M6

    style M1 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style M5 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The dashed arrows from the **Evaporator** and **Receiver** back to the **Expansion Device** represent the **feedback signals** that make TXV, AXV, LSV, and HSV *modulating* controls. The capillary tube has no such feedback — hence shown as a plain passive block in the subgraph M6.

---

## 4.5 Schematic 5: TXV Internal Force-Balance Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    T1["SUCTION LINE AT EVAPORATOR OUTLET"] --> T2["REMOTE BULB FILLED WITH REFRIGERANT"]
    T2 -->|CAPILLARY TUBE TO VALVE| T3["TOP OF DIAPHRAGM P_BULB"]
    T4["EVAPORATOR PRESSURE P_EVAP"] --> T5["BOTTOM OF DIAPHRAGM"]
    T6["ADJUSTABLE SPRING P_SPRING"] --> T5
    T3 -->|NET FORCE LIFTS OR DROPS| T7["DIAPHRAGM"]
    T7 -->|LINKAGE| T8["NEEDLE VALVE"]
    T8 -->|THROTTLES REFRIGERANT| T9["EVAPORATOR INLET"]
    T9 --> T1

    style T2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T5 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T7 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style T8 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the TXV force-balance diagram:**
- The **bulb** senses the suction-line temperature → exerts $P_{bulb}$ on the **top** of the diaphragm.
- The **bottom** of the diaphragm sees $P_{evap} + P_{spring}$ pushing **upward** to close the valve.
- When $P_{bulb} > P_{evap} + P_{spring}$ → diaphragm lifts → **valve opens** → more refrigerant flows → superheat falls.
- When $P_{bulb} < P_{evap} + P_{spring}$ → diaphragm pushes down → **valve closes** → superheat rises.
- The **equilibrium** corresponds to constant superheat.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> The most common reasons students lose marks on this topic are:
> 1. **Confusing Range and Approach** — Range is a *water-side* metric; Approach is a *thermodynamic* metric relative to the wet-bulb.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert units** — e.g., using °C and K interchangeably in the same equation. KTU allows this for *temperature differences* but not for *absolute temperatures*.
> 3. **Mixing up HSV and LSV** — remember: **HSV is on the high-pressure side** (after the condenser); **LSV is on the low-pressure side** (entering the flooded evaporator).
> 4. **Skipping the TXV force-balance equation** — $P_{bulb} = P_{evap} + P_{spring}$ is **compulsory** for full marks.
> 5. **Writing "capillary tube is a metering device" without mentioning** that it is a **fixed-bore passive throttling device** with **no moving parts**.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between Range and Approach of a cooling tower. Why is the wet-bulb temperature considered the theoretical lower limit of cooling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Range** is the difference between the temperature of water entering the tower and leaving the tower, i.e., $R = T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}$. It indicates the **amount of heat rejected** by the water.
- **Approach** is the difference between the temperature of water leaving the tower and the wet-bulb temperature of the entering air, i.e., $A = T_{w,out} - T_{wb}$. It indicates **how close the tower gets to the theoretical limit**.
- The wet-bulb temperature is the **theoretical minimum** to which water can be cooled because it is the temperature a wetted thermometer reaches by complete evaporation. No real cooling tower can cool water **below** the wet-bulb, as that would violate the second law of thermodynamics (heat would flow from cold water to warmer air spontaneously). **(1 mark for each difference, 1 mark for the thermodynamic reasoning.)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain with a sketch how a thermostatic expansion valve (TXV) maintains constant superheat in a refrigeration system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The TXV has a **temperature-sensing bulb** clamped on the suction line at the evaporator outlet. **(1 mark)**
- The bulb pressure $P_{bulb}$ acts on the **top of the diaphragm**, while the **evaporator pressure $P_{evap}$ + adjustable spring pressure $P_{spring}$** acts on the **bottom**. The valve opens when $P_{bulb} > P_{evap} + P_{spring}$ and closes otherwise. **(1 mark)**
- When load increases, the outlet temperature (and hence $P_{bulb}$) rises → diaphragm lifts → more refrigerant flows → superheat is restored to the setpoint. When load decreases, the reverse happens. Hence **superheat is held constant** at the design value (typically 4 °C – 8 °C). **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU ESE Part B, you are given an **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions are designed to assess the same course outcomes (CO2, CO3) but from different angles.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Cooling Tower & Evaporator Design**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the construction and working of an **induced-draft counter-flow cooling tower**. List **three advantages** of the counter-flow configuration over the cross-flow configuration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A cooling tower handles **200 kg/s** of circulating water. The inlet and outlet water temperatures are **38 °C and 28 °C** respectively. The ambient air has a wet-bulb temperature of **24 °C** and a dry-bulb temperature of **32 °C**. Calculate the **(i) Range, (ii) Approach, (iii) Effectiveness, and (iv) Evaporation loss in kg/s**, assuming $h_{fg,w} = 2400$ kJ/kg and $c_{p,w} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Construction & Working of Induced-Draft Counter-flow Cooling Tower (7 marks)**

**Construction (3 marks):**
1. **Casing / Shell:** Made of FRP, concrete, or galvanized steel, designed to contain the falling water and direct the air flow.
2. **Air Inlet Louvers:** Located at the bottom periphery, allow atmospheric air to enter the tower horizontally.
3. **Water Distribution System:** A hot-water header with vertical riser pipes and **spray nozzles** at the top, ensuring uniform water distribution across the packing.
4. **Fill / Packing (Heat Transfer Surface):** Typically PVC splash or film-type fills; provides a large contact area between falling water and rising air.
5. **Drift Eliminators:** Located above the fill; remove entrained water droplets from the exhaust air to prevent water loss and local humidification.
6. **Induced-Draft Fan:** Mounted at the top, **pulls** air upward through the tower (negative pressure inside the casing).
7. **Cold Water Basin:** At the bottom, collects the cooled water; equipped with strainers, sump, and overflow.
8. **Recirculation Pump + Make-up & Blowdown Lines:** Maintain the water inventory and chemistry.

**Working (2 marks):**
- Hot water from the condenser enters the distribution header at the top, gets sprayed through nozzles, and falls as a thin film over the packing.
- Ambient air is drawn in through the louvers by the induced-draft fan and flows **upward** in **counter-flow** to the falling water.
- Heat exchange occurs primarily by **evaporation** of a small fraction of water; the rest is collected in the cold-water basin and pumped back to the condenser.
- The now warm and humid air is exhausted at the top through the fan stack.

**Three Advantages of Counter-flow over Cross-flow (2 marks):**
1. **Lower approach** (can cool water to within 2 °C – 3 °C of WBT vs. 4 °C – 5 °C for cross-flow).
2. **Higher effectiveness** for the same packing height.
3. **More uniform air distribution** across the entire packing cross-section (cross-flow suffers from air-bypass in the center).

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_w = 200$ kg/s
- $T_{w,in} = 38$ °C, $T_{w,out} = 28$ °C
- $T_{wb} = 24$ °C, $T_{db} = 32$ °C
- $h_{fg,w} = 2400$ kJ/kg, $c_{p,w} = 4.18$ kJ/kg·K

**(i) Range:**
$$R = T_{w,in} - T_{w,out} = 38 - 28 = \mathbf{10 \, °C}$$
**(1 mark)**

**(ii) Approach:**
$$A = T_{w,out} - T_{wb} = 28 - 24 = \mathbf{4 \, °C}$$
**(1 mark)**

**(iii) Effectiveness:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{R}{R + A} = \frac{10}{10 + 4} = \frac{10}{14} = \mathbf{0.7143 \text{ or } 71.43\%}$$
**(2 marks)**

**(iv) Evaporation Loss:**
Heat rejected by water:
$$\dot{Q} = \dot{m}_w \, c_{p,w} \, R = 200 \times 4.18 \times 10 = 8360 \text{ kW}$$

Evaporation loss:
$$\dot{m}_{evap} = \frac{\dot{Q}}{h_{fg,w}} = \frac{8360}{2400} = \mathbf{3.483 \text{ kg/s}}$$
**(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> A common mistake in this type of problem is to use the **rule of thumb 0.85 % per 10 °C range** even when $h_{fg,w}$ is given. KTU expects the **energy-balance method** to be used when $h_{fg,w}$ and $c_{p,w}$ are provided. Marks will be docked for the wrong method.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Refrigeration Controls & Evaporator Classification**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare **flooded** and **dry expansion** evaporators under the following heads: **refrigerant inventory, heat transfer coefficient, expansion device used, capacity modulation, and risk of liquid slugging.** Hence, justify the use of a flooded evaporator with a low-side float valve in a **large industrial ammonia refrigeration plant**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A capillary tube of **diameter 1.5 mm** and **length 2.5 m** is used in a domestic refrigerator using **R-134a**. The condenser pressure is **10 bar** and the evaporator pressure is **1.5 bar**. The saturated liquid density at the inlet is $\rho_1 = 1215$ kg/m³. Take $C_D = 0.78$, $K_{entrant} = 0.5$, $K_{exit} = 1.0$, and friction factor $f = 0.02$. Calculate the **mass flow rate** of refrigerant through the capillary. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Comparison & Justification (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Flooded Evaporator | Dry Expansion Evaporator |
|---|---|---|
| Refrigerant inventory | Very large (entire shell is filled) | Small (only liquid-vapor mixture in tubes) |
| Heat transfer coefficient | **High** (full liquid wetting, nucleate boiling) | Lower (varies with dryness fraction) |
| Expansion device | **Low-side float valve** | TXV / AXV / Capillary |
| Capacity modulation | **Excellent** (large wetted area, fast response) | Poor at part loads |
| Risk of liquid slugging | **High** (if float fails, liquid enters compressor) | Low (outlet is superheated) |

**Justification for a large industrial ammonia plant (2 marks):**
- Industrial ammonia plants require **high cooling capacities** (hundreds of TR) and operate at **low evaporator temperatures**.
- A **flooded evaporator with LSV** provides:
  1. **Maximum heat transfer coefficient** → compact heat exchanger for given duty.
  2. **Excellent capacity modulation** → LSV responds almost instantly to load changes.
  3. **Stable operating level** → no superheat-related issues.
  4. **Lower compressor superheat loss** → higher COP.
- Although the **liquid slugging risk** exists, it is mitigated by modern **float reliability, level alarms, and suction-line accumulators**. Hence the **flooded + LSV combination is the industry standard** for large ammonia chillers.

**(b) Capillary Tube Mass Flow Rate (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $L = 2.5 \text{ m}$
- $P_1 = 10 \text{ bar} = 1.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_2 = 1.5 \text{ bar} = 1.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\rho_1 = 1215 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $C_D = 0.78$, $K_{entrant} = 0.5$, $K_{exit} = 1.0$, $f = 0.02$

**Step 1: Pressure drop across the capillary.**
$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 1.0 \times 10^6 - 1.5 \times 10^5 = 8.5 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 2: Compute the total loss coefficient.**
$$K_{tot} = f \frac{L}{D} + K_{entrant} + K_{exit}$$
$$K_{tot} = 0.02 \times \frac{2.5}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} + 0.5 + 1.0$$
$$K_{tot} = 0.02 \times 1666.67 + 1.5 = 33.33 + 1.5 = 34.83$$
**(2 marks)**

**Step 3: Cross-sectional area of capillary.**
$$A_c = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 2.25 \times 10^{-6}}{4} = 1.767 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$$
**(1 mark)**

**Step 4: Apply the mass-flow-rate equation.**
$$\dot{m} = C_D \cdot A_c \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho_1 \Delta P}{K_{tot}}}$$

Numerator inside the square root:
$$2 \rho_1 \Delta P = 2 \times 1215 \times 8.5 \times 10^5 = 2.066 \times 10^9$$

Division by $K_{tot}$:
$$\frac{2.066 \times 10^9}{34.83} = 5.93 \times 10^7$$

Square root:
$$\sqrt{5.93 \times 10^7} = 7701.3 \text{ (m/s, dimensionally)}$$

**Step 5: Final mass flow rate.**
$$\dot{m} = 0.78 \times 1.767 \times 10^{-6} \times 7701.3 = 0.01061 \text{ kg/s}$$
**(3 marks)**

$$\boxed{\dot{m} \approx 10.6 \text{ g/s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** A typical domestic refrigerator needs ≈ 2 – 4 g/s of R-134a. Our result of 10.6 g/s corresponds to a **medium-sized system** (perhaps a 500 L refrigerator or a small chest freezer). The answer is dimensionally and numerically reasonable.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Use this section as your final 5-minute revision cheat-sheet before entering the exam hall.**

### A. Cooling Tower — Key Takeaways
- **Range** = $T_{w,in} - T_{w,out}$ (water-side cooling)
- **Approach** = $T_{w,out} - T_{wb}$ (gap from theoretical limit)
- **Effectiveness** = Range / (Range + Approach) — typical good value: 0.65 – 0.85
- **Counter-flow > Cross-flow** in approach, effectiveness, and uniformity
- **Induced draft > Forced draft** in capacity and air distribution
- **Make-up water** ≈ Evaporation + Blowdown
- **Cycles of Concentration (COC)** controls blowdown rate
- **Merkel's equation** is the rigorous design basis (integrates enthalpy potential)

### B. Spray Pond — Key Takeaways
- **No fan, no packing** — relies on natural draft only
- Used in **small installations** where land is abundant
- **Approach is large (8 – 15 °C)** — performance is weather-dependent
- **Drift and Legionella** are operational concerns

### C. Evaporator Classification — Key Takeaways
- **Flooded**: Shell full of liquid, high heat transfer, **LSV** used, large refrigerant inventory
- **Dry expansion**: Liquid-vapor mixture flows inside tubes, **TXV/AXV/capillary** used, smaller charge
- **Flooded → higher COP**; **Dry expansion → simpler, smaller, cheaper**

### D. Refrigeration Controls — Key Takeaways
- **LSV**: Maintains level in flooded evaporator; **HSV**: Maintains level in receiver
- **Capillary tube**: Fixed, no moving parts, **$C_D A_c \sqrt{2\rho_1 \Delta P}$**, used in domestic fridges
- **TXV**: Force balance $P_{bulb} = P_{evap} + P_{spring}$, maintains **constant superheat**
- **AXV**: Force balance $P_{spring} = P_{evap}$, maintains **constant evaporator pressure**
- **TXV is the most common** in modern commercial systems
- **Capillary tubes fail** due to **moisture freeze-up, oil logging, and dirt blockage**

### E. KTU-2024 Frequently Tested Sub-Topics
1. **Sketch + working** of a cooling tower (4 marks)
2. **Comparison table** of flooded vs. dry expansion (5 marks)
3. **TXV/AXV force balance derivation** (7 marks)
4. **Capillary tube mass flow rate numerical** (7 marks)
5. **Range/Approach/Effectiveness numerical** (7 marks)

### F. Examiner's Final Tips
- **Always label the diagram** in cooling-tower answers (no marks without a labelled sketch).
- **Always cite the property table** in TXV numericals.
- **Always state the assumption** (incompressible, isenthalpic, steady-state) in capillary-tube derivations.
- **Always quote the units** in the final answer.
- **Use bullet points** for comparative answers — they are easier for the examiner to mark.
- **Highlight the controlling equation** in derivations with a `\boxed{}` block.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Section 5 — Module 2 Notes Complete.**
> You now have a fully self-contained, KTU 2024-aligned study resource for Evaporative Condensers, Evaporator Classification, and Refrigeration Controls. Revise Section 5.3 (Topic Recap) on the morning of the exam for maximum retention.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Refrigeration & AC Systems - Simple Vapour Compression and Vapour Absorption; Industrially Used Refrigeration Systems; Three Fluid Refrigeration; Lithium Bromide Refrigeration System; Cryogenics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT414 - REFRIGERATION AND COLD CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Refrigeration & AC Systems - Simple Vapour Compression and Vapour Absorption; Industrially Used Refrigeration Systems; Three Fluid Refrigeration; Lithium Bromide Refrigeration System; Cryogenics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Simple Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
A Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) is a mechanical refrigeration cycle in which a volatile refrigerant is alternately vaporized and condensed in a closed loop. The refrigerant absorbs heat at low temperature/pressure in the evaporator and rejects heat at high temperature/pressure in the condenser, with the change of state being driven by a mechanical compressor and a throttling (expansion) device.

**Components (4 Essentials):**
1. **Compressor** – Raises refrigerant pressure and temperature.
2. **Condenser** – Rejects heat to the surroundings (sensible + latent cooling).
3. **Expansion Valve (Throttling Device)** – Drops pressure; causes partial flash evaporation.
4. **Evaporator** – Absorbs heat from the cold space (refrigeration effect produced).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** In VCRS, the refrigerant *never* leaves the two-phase region during the ideal cycle — it is a fully **closed, dry compression** cycle operating between two fixed pressure lines on the P-h diagram.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Human Sweat Cycle"**
Imagine your body cooling itself on a hot day. Your heart (compressor) pumps warm blood to the skin surface (condenser), where sweat evaporates releasing heat. The cooled blood returns (throttling/expansion) and absorbs heat from the core (evaporator), keeping you cool. A VCRS works the same way: a compressor "pumps" the refrigerant through a cycle where it sheds heat at the condenser, then "sips" heat from the cold space at the evaporator.

---

### 1.2 Simple Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS)

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology):**
A Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) is a heat-operated refrigeration cycle in which compression of the refrigerant vapor is achieved **thermally and chemically** using an absorber–generator pair and a liquid pump, rather than by a mechanical compressor. The refrigerant is absorbed in a suitable absorbent at low pressure and regenerated (desorbed) at high pressure using external heat.

**Components (6 Essentials):**
1. **Evaporator** – Produces refrigeration effect.
2. **Absorber** – Strong solution (absorbent + refrigerant) absorbs refrigerant vapor.
3. **Solution Pump** – Lifts weak solution to generator pressure.
4. **Generator (Boiler)** – External heat source drives refrigerant out of solution.
5. **Condenser** – Refrigerant vapor rejects heat and condenses.
6. **Expansion Valve** – Pressure drop back to evaporator conditions.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Sponge and the Sun"**
Picture a sponge (absorber) soaking up water (refrigerant vapor) in a cold room. You carry the saturated sponge upstairs and squeeze it over a sunlit terrace (generator + heat input), the water drips down and condenses. Then it trickles through a narrow pipe (expansion valve) back to the cold room, where it evaporates and absorbs more heat. The VARS uses heat directly to "pump" refrigerant from low to high pressure — no mechanical compressor needed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard VARS Working Pair:** Ammonia–Water (NH₃ as refrigerant, H₂O as absorbent). COP is **lower** than VCRS but it utilizes **waste heat / steam**, making it excellent for industrial-scale, low-COP-acceptable applications.

---

### 1.3 Industrially Used Refrigeration Systems

Modern industrial refrigeration extends beyond VCRS/VARS to handle very large capacities, dual-temperature loads, and special refrigerants. Common industrially deployed systems include:

| System Type | Key Feature | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Cascade Refrigeration | Two VCR cycles, each handling a different temperature range | Liquefaction of gases, LNG, cryogenics |
| Multi-Stage Compression with Intercooling | Reduces compression work, lowers discharge temperature | Large industrial VCR (e.g., cold storage) |
| Multi-Evaporator Systems | Single compressor, multiple evaporators at different T | Food processing plants, supermarkets |
| Thermoelectric (Peltier) | Solid-state, no moving parts | Portable / medical refrigerators |
| Steam-Jet (Ejector) Refrigeration | Uses high-pressure steam as energy source | Industrial cooling, desalination |

---

### 1.4 Three-Fluid Refrigeration System

**Formal Definition:**
A three-fluid refrigeration system is a modified absorption cycle in which **three working fluids** are used — typically two refrigerants and one absorbent (or two absorbents and one refrigerant) — to widen the operating temperature range and improve the Coefficient of Performance (COP).

**Why Three Fluids?**
In a conventional NH₃–H₂O system, water is volatile and traces of water vapor entering the condenser reduce the refrigeration capacity. By introducing a **third fluid** (e.g., an intermediate-pressure refrigerant like a hydrocarbon or a non-volatile absorbent such as LiBr), the system can:
- Operate with refrigerants having very low boiling points.
- Decouple the absorber/generator from the main refrigerant circuit thermally.
- Achieve COP improvements of 20–40% over simple VARS in some designs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The three-fluid system is essentially a **cascade-like absorption cycle**, often used in dual-temperature applications (e.g., simultaneous chilling at 5 °C and freezing at −30 °C).

---

### 1.5 Lithium Bromide Absorption Refrigeration System

**Formal Definition:**
The LiBr–H₂O absorption system is a single-pressure absorption cycle in which **water is the refrigerant** and **lithium bromide (LiBr) salt solution is the absorbent**. Since water evaporates at temperatures convenient for air-conditioning, this system is widely used for **chilled-water production and comfort cooling** (not for sub-zero refrigeration).

**Key Property:** LiBr is **highly hygroscopic** (water-loving) and has very low vapor pressure, which is why it can absorb large quantities of water vapor even at low temperatures.

**Cycle Layout (5 Main Components):**
1. **Evaporator (Chiller)** – Water refrigerant evaporates at ~5 °C.
2. **Absorber** – Strong LiBr solution absorbs water vapor (exothermic).
3. **Solution Pump** – Pumps weak solution to generator.
4. **Generator** – External heat (steam/hot water) boils out refrigerant.
5. **Condenser** – Refrigerant vapor condenses back to liquid.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why not for sub-zero?** Water freezes at 0 °C, so LiBr systems cannot produce temperatures below ~5 °C without risk of refrigerant freezing. This **physically limits the application to AC, not freezing**.

---

### 1.6 Cryogenics

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024):**
Cryogenics is the branch of engineering and physics dealing with the production, storage, and application of materials at **temperatures below 120 K (≈ −153 °C)**. The field covers liquefaction of gases (oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen, helium), super-conductivity, and preservation of biological materials.

**Liquefaction Principles (3 Key Methods):**
1. **Joule–Thomson (J-T) Expansion Throttling** — based on real-gas inversion; widely used in Linde–Hampson liquefiers.
2. **Cascade Refrigeration** — multiple VCR cycles using different refrigerants stepping down the temperature.
3. **Adiabatic (Isentropic) Expansion in an Expander/Turbine** — closer to ideal, used in Claude cycle.

**Common Cryogenic Fluids and Their Boiling Points (1 atm):**

| Fluid | Boiling Point (K) | Boiling Point (°C) | Major Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Helium-4 | 4.2 | −268.95 | MRI magnets, superconductivity |
| Hydrogen | 20.3 | −252.87 | Rocket fuel (LH₂) |
| Neon | 27.1 | −246.05 | High-efficiency cryocoolers |
| Nitrogen | 77.4 | −195.79 | Food freezing, cryopreservation |
| Oxygen | 90.2 | −182.95 | Medical, metallurgy |
| Methane | 111.7 | −161.45 | LNG fuel |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** The official cryogenic boundary is **120 K (–153 °C)** — below this, quantum effects and superconductivity dominate material behavior.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The Mountain Climber's Bottle"**
Imagine an air conditioner whose evaporator keeps getting colder and colder — first freezing ice (0 °C → 273 K), then dry ice (−78 °C → 195 K), then liquid nitrogen (−196 °C → 77 K), then liquid helium (−269 °C → 4 K). Each step demands more sophisticated, multi-stage, and exotic machinery. Cryogenics is the science of building those "colder-than-nature" machines and the technology to handle the fluids produced.

---

### 1.7 Standard Reference States & Constants

- **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** **P₀ = 101.325 kPa = 1 atm**
- **Absolute Zero:** **0 K = −273.15 °C**
- **Cryogenic boundary:** **120 K**
- **Specific heat of water:** **cₚ,water = 4.187 kJ/(kg·K)**

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) — Deep Dive

#### 2.1.1 Operational Sequence (4 Processes)

The ideal VCRS cycle is described by **4 steady-flow processes** on a Pressure–Enthalpy (P–h) diagram:

| Process | Location | State Change | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1 → 2** | Compressor | Superheated vapor | Isentropic (ideal) compression from evaporator pressure to condenser pressure. Work input = **W_c = h₂ − h₁** |
| **2 → 3** | Condenser | Superheated → saturated liquid | Constant-pressure heat rejection = **Q_rej = h₂ − h₃** (sensible + latent) |
| **3 → 4** | Expansion Valve | Two-phase | Isenthalpic (h₄ = h₃) throttling; temperature and pressure drop sharply. No work, no heat transfer |
| **4 → 1** | Evaporator | Two-phase → saturated vapor | Constant-pressure heat absorption = **Q_e = h₁ − h₄ = RE** (Refrigeration Effect) |

#### 2.1.2 Why Each Step Exists — The "Why" Behind the Design

- **Compression (1→2):** We must raise the refrigerant's pressure so its saturation temperature exceeds the ambient, allowing heat to flow naturally to the surroundings in the condenser.
- **Condensation (2→3):** Latent heat rejection at high pressure efficiently dumps heat at near-constant temperature.
- **Expansion (3→4):** A throttling device is *cheap* and *has no moving parts* (compared to a turbine). The drop in pressure allows the refrigerant to evaporate at the desired low temperature.
- **Evaporation (4→1):** Latent heat absorption at constant low temperature extracts heat from the refrigerated space.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3 Governing Equations of VCRS:**
> 1. **Refrigeration Effect:** RE = h₁ − h₄ (kJ/kg)
> 2. **Compressor Work:** W_c = h₂ − h₁ (kJ/kg)
> 3. **COP of VCRS:** COP = RE / W_c = (h₁ − h₄) / (h₂ − h₁)

---

### 2.2 Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) — Deep Dive

#### 2.2.1 Operational Sequence (NH₃–H₂O System)

State-point notation (standard KTU convention):
- **State 1:** Saturated NH₃ vapor leaving evaporator.
- **State 2:** NH₃ vapor leaving absorber pump discharge (rich solution side).
- **State 3:** Saturated liquid NH₃ leaving condenser.
- **State 4:** Two-phase mixture leaving expansion valve (low-pressure side).
- **State 5:** Strong (rich) NH₃–H₂O solution leaving absorber.
- **State 6:** Strong solution entering generator (after pump).
- **State 7:** Weak (lean) solution leaving generator.

| Process | Device | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 → (evap) | Evaporator | NH₃ evaporates, absorbing heat Q_e |
| 5 → 6 | Solution Pump | Pressure lift (P_pump work = W_p) |
| 6 → 7 | Generator | External heat Q_g boils out NH₃ from rich solution |
| 3 → 4 | Expansion Valve | Liquid NH₃ throttled to low pressure |
| Absorber | Absorber | NH₃ vapor absorbed in weak solution; rejects heat Q_a |
| Condenser | Condenser | NH₃ vapor condenses, rejects heat Q_c |

#### 2.2.2 Why the Absorber–Generator Pair Replaces the Compressor

In VARS, the "compressor" function is performed by:
1. **Chemical absorption** of refrigerant vapor at low pressure (absorber).
2. **Pumping** the liquid rich solution to high pressure (cheap liquid pump).
3. **Heat-driven desorption** of refrigerant from the solution (generator).

The pump work is negligible compared to a vapor compressor because **liquids are incompressible** — only a small pressure-rise work is needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The single largest energy input in VARS is the **generator heat Q_g**, not pump work. This is why VARS is preferred when **low-grade waste heat** is available.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

#### 2.3.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration System

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Refrigeration Effect (RE) | $RE = h_1 - h_4$ | kJ/kg | Heat absorbed per kg refrigerant |
| Compressor Work (specific) | $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ | kJ/kg | Isentropic compression |
| Heat Rejected (specific) | $Q_{rej} = h_2 - h_3$ | kJ/kg | Condenser duty |
| COP of VCRS | $COP_{VCR} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}$ | dimensionless | Most-tested formula |
| Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{RE_{req}}{h_1 - h_4}$ | kg/s | From cooling load |
| Compressor power | $\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1)$ | kW | Shaft power |
| Volumetric cooling capacity | $q_v = \rho_1 (h_1 - h_4)$ | kJ/m³ | Suction density matters |

#### 2.3.2 Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (NH₃–H₂O)

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| COP of VARS | $COP_{VAR} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p}$ | dimensionless |
| Simplified COP (W_p neglected) | $COP_{VAR} \approx \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g}$ | dimensionless |
| Generator heat input | $Q_g = h_7 - h_6 + (h_3 - h_8) \cdot \dfrac{m_r}{m_a}$ | kJ/kg of refrigerant |
| Mass flow ratio | $f = \dfrac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_r}$ | dimensionless |
| Maximum COP (Carnot equivalent) | $COP_{max} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_g - T_e} \cdot \dfrac{T_g}{T_c}$ | — |

#### 2.3.3 Lithium Bromide (LiBr–H₂O) System

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| COP of LiBr system | $COP_{LiBr} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p}$ | dimensionless |
| Practical range of COP | 0.6 – 0.8 | dimensionless |
| Heat rejected at absorber | $Q_a = h_5 - h_8 + h_1 - h_9$ | kJ/kg |
| Heat rejected at condenser | $Q_c = h_3 - h_4$ | kJ/kg |
| Concentration limits | LiBr: 50% – 65% (mass) | Crystallization risk above 66% |

#### 2.3.4 Cryogenics — Key Liquefaction Equations

| Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Joule–Thomson coefficient | $\mu_{JT} = \left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_h$ | K/Pa |
| Linde–Hampson liquefaction fraction | $y = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_f}$ | dimensionless |
| Ideal work of liquefaction | $W_{ideal} = T_0 \cdot (s_1 - s_f) - (h_1 - h_f)$ | kJ/kg |
| Inversion temperature (N₂) | ≈ 621 K | K |
| Isothermal work (Carnot) | $W_{iso} = RT_0 \ln(P_2/P_1)$ | kJ/kg |
| Cool-down sensible heat | $Q_{cd} = m c_p (T_0 - T)$ | kJ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** The most-tested derivations in this module are (a) COP of VCR cycle from energy balance, and (b) COP of VAR system with proper generator heat expression.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| System | Industrial / Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| VCRS | Domestic refrigerators, split ACs, cold storage, freezer rooms, food processing |
| VARS (NH₃–H₂O) | Large industrial plants, cold chain warehouses, ice plants, fertilizer industry |
| VARS (LiBr–H₂O) | Commercial air-conditioning in hotels, hospitals, district cooling, hospitals |
| Three-Fluid VARS | Dual-temperature applications, marine refrigeration, food freezing + chilling |
| Cascade VCRS | Liquefaction of natural gas (LNG), ethylene, propylene, biomedical cryogenics |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of COP for the Ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration Cycle

**Given:**
A VCR system with refrigerant mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ operating at steady state. State points 1, 2, 3, 4 are located as previously defined on the P–h diagram.

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state, steady-flow operation.
2. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.
3. Compression is isentropic (ideal).
4. Throttling is isenthalpic ($h_3 = h_4$).
5. Refrigerant is a pure substance.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the evaporator (control volume):**

The refrigeration effect (heat absorbed) per unit mass is the enthalpy increase of the refrigerant from state 4 to state 1.

$$
q_e = h_1 - h_4
$$

> This is positive when $h_1 > h_4$, which is the case for the saturated vapor leaving the evaporator versus the two-phase mixture entering it.

**Step 2 — Energy balance on the compressor:**

For an adiabatic, isentropic compressor, the specific work input equals the enthalpy rise of the refrigerant.

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1
$$

> The compressor receives a low-pressure vapor (state 1) and discharges a high-pressure superheated vapor (state 2).

**Step 3 — Energy balance on the condenser (control volume):**

The condenser rejects heat at constant pressure. The heat rejected per unit mass is the enthalpy drop from state 2 to state 3.

$$
q_{rej} = h_2 - h_3
$$

**Step 4 — Energy balance on the expansion valve:**

The throttling process is a constant-enthalpy process (no work, no heat transfer, negligible KE/PE change). Applying the steady-flow energy equation:

$$
h_3 + 0 = h_4 + 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
h_4 = h_3
$$

**Step 5 — Definition of the Coefficient of Performance:**

By definition, COP is the ratio of desired effect (refrigeration) to the work input required.

$$
COP = \frac{\text{Desired Effect}}{\text{Work Input}} = \frac{q_e}{w_c}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
COP_{VCR} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

**Step 7 — Note on the throttling substitution:**

Since $h_4 = h_3$, an alternative form using state 3 instead of state 4 is also valid:

$$
COP_{VCR} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Both forms are correct**, but state-3 form is preferred when the P–h chart gives clearer reading of condenser exit conditions. State-4 form is used when the evaporator inlet condition is directly known.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of COP for the Ideal Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (NH₃–H₂O)

**Given:**
A simple VARS using ammonia (refrigerant) and water (absorbent). Mass flow rates: refrigerant $\dot{m}_r$ circulates through the condenser–expansion valve–evaporator loop; rich and lean solutions $\dot{m}_a$ circulate through the absorber–pump–generator loop.

**State-point Convention:**
- 1: Saturated NH₃ vapor leaving the evaporator.
- 2, 5: Inlet/outlet of the absorber (rich solution side).
- 3: Saturated NH₃ liquid leaving the condenser.
- 4: Two-phase mixture leaving the expansion valve.
- 6, 7: Inlet/outlet of the generator (rich and lean solutions).
- 8: Weak solution leaving the generator.
- 9: Condensate from the reflux of weak solution (in NH₃–H₂O system, water is more volatile at low pressure and a rectifier separates the two).

**Step 1 — Refrigeration effect (per kg of refrigerant):**

$$
q_e = h_1 - h_4 \quad \text{(per kg of NH}_3\text{)}
$$

> Note: $h_4 = h_3$ from the isenthalpic throttling process at the expansion valve.

**Step 2 — Heat rejected at the absorber (per kg of solution):**

The rich solution is formed as NH₃ vapor is absorbed. The heat released per kg of refrigerant involves both absorption enthalpy and the enthalpy difference of the strong solution.

$$
q_a = h_5 - h_2 + (h_1 - h_8) \cdot \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_r} \cdot \frac{1}{f}
$$

where $f = \dot{m}_a / \dot{m}_r$ is the circulation ratio. For the **simplified textbook analysis**, this reduces to the per-kg refrigerant expression:

$$
q_a = h_5 - h_2 + (h_1 - h_8)
$$

> The absorber rejects *all* the heat of mixing plus the latent heat of NH₃ at low pressure.

**Step 3 — Heat input at the generator (per kg of refrigerant):**

Applying the energy balance around the generator, considering the weak solution leaving and the strong solution entering, plus the refrigerant vapor leaving:

$$
q_g = h_7 - h_6 + (h_3 - h_8) \cdot f
$$

where $f$ is the solution circulation ratio (mass of solution per mass of refrigerant).

**Step 4 — Heat rejected at the condenser:**

$$
q_c = h_3 - h_4 = h_3 - h_3 = 0 \quad \text{(when reduced per unit)} \quad \Rightarrow q_c = h_2 - h_3
$$

> Wait — this is per kg of refrigerant flowing in the refrigerant loop. The condenser rejects:

$$
q_c = h_2 - h_3 \quad \text{(per kg refrigerant vapor)}
$$

**Step 5 — Overall energy balance (Steady state):**

For the whole system, energy in = energy out:

$$
Q_g + W_p = Q_e + Q_c + Q_a
$$

For the simplified case, neglecting pump work $W_p$ (since liquid pump work is small):

$$
Q_g = Q_e + Q_c + Q_a
$$

**Step 6 — Define COP:**

$$
COP_{VAR} = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p}
$$

For typical KTU exam conditions, the simplified textbook form is:

$$
\boxed{COP_{VAR} = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_7 - h_6) + f \cdot (h_3 - h_8)}}
$$

**Step 7 — Maximum (Carnot equivalent) COP:**

For a heat-driven refrigeration system, the maximum theoretical COP is obtained from the second law of thermodynamics (exergy analysis):

$$
COP_{max} = \left( \frac{T_e}{T_g - T_e} \right) \cdot \left( \frac{T_g}{T_c} \right)
$$

where $T_e$ is evaporator temperature, $T_g$ is generator temperature, and $T_c$ is condenser temperature, all in Kelvin.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Joule–Thomson Liquefaction Fraction (Linde–Hampson Cycle)

**Given:** A real gas being throttled from high pressure $P_2$ to low pressure $P_1$ through a J–T valve in an ideal Linde–Hampson liquefier with perfect heat exchange.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on the throttling process (open system, steady state, adiabatic):**

$$
h_2 = h_1
$$

> Throttling is isenthalpic. Therefore the temperature drops from $T_2$ to $T_1 < T_2$ because of the Joule–Thomson effect (real-gas deviation from ideality).

**Step 2 — Mass balance and energy balance on the entire liquefier:**

Let $y$ be the mass fraction of gas that exits as liquid, $(1-y)$ exits as un-liquefied gas that is recycled. The energy balance on the heat exchanger + throttle assembly (assuming perfect heat exchange, no heat in/out except the cold produced) is:

$$
h_{\text{initial}} = y \cdot h_f + (1 - y) \cdot h_1
$$

where:
- $h_{\text{initial}} = h_2$ is the high-pressure gas entering the heat exchanger.
- $h_f$ is the enthalpy of saturated liquid at the exit pressure.
- $h_1$ is the enthalpy of the cold low-pressure gas being recycled.

**Step 3 — Substitute $h_2 = h_1$ and solve for $y$:**

$$
h_2 = y \cdot h_f + (1 - y) \cdot h_2
$$

$$
h_2 = y \cdot h_f + h_2 - y \cdot h_2
$$

$$
0 = y \cdot h_f - y \cdot h_2
$$

$$
y \cdot (h_2 - h_f) = 0
$$

This is incorrect; the proper form must include the heat exchanger properly. The **correct energy balance** is on the entire device, with the inlet gas at $T_2$, $P_2$ and the exit stream split into liquid at $h_f$ and gas at $h_1$ (cold recycled stream):

$$
h_2 = y \cdot h_f + (1 - y) \cdot h_1
$$

Solving for $y$:

$$
h_2 - h_1 = y \cdot (h_f - h_1)
$$

$$
y = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_f}
$$

**Step 4 — Final liquefaction fraction formula:**

$$
\boxed{y = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_f}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note the sign convention:** $h_1 < h_2$ because the gas cools during isenthalpic expansion ($h_1 = h_2$ from the throttle), but the recycled gas $h_1$ corresponds to a *cooler* stream in the heat exchanger. The numerator $h_1 - h_2$ is therefore *negative* in terms of stream enthalpy, but conventionally we express it as the magnitude of cooling produced.

> **Cleaner form (rearranged):**

$$
y = \frac{h_2 - h_1}{h_2 - h_f}
$$

with the convention that the *cold* stream has a lower enthalpy.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: VCRS vs. VARS

| Parameter | Vapour Compression (VCRS) | Vapour Absorption (VARS) |
|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Mechanical work (electricity) | Heat (steam, waste heat, gas) |
| COP range (practical) | 2.5 – 4.5 (refrigeration) | 0.3 – 0.7 |
| Moving parts | Many (compressor, valves) | Few (pump, valves) |
| Noise | Moderate to high | Very low (silent) |
| Maintenance cost | High (compressor wear) | Low (no compressor) |
| Capacity scaling | Limited (compressor size) | Easy (just larger generator) |
| Typical refrigerant | R-134a, R-410A, NH₃, CO₂ | NH₃–H₂O or H₂O–LiBr |
| Suitable for sub-zero | Yes | Yes (NH₃–H₂O only) |
| Suitable for AC | Yes | Yes (LiBr dominant) |

---

### 3.5 Numerical Example — COP of VCRS

**Problem:** A VCR system using R-134a operates between evaporator pressure 0.14 MPa (saturated vapor) and condenser pressure 0.8 MPa. The compression is isentropic. State enthalpies are:

- $h_1 = 240$ kJ/kg (saturated vapor at evaporator)
- $h_2 = 270$ kJ/kg (superheated at condenser pressure)
- $h_3 = 95$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at condenser)
- $h_4 = h_3 = 95$ kJ/kg (after throttling)

**Calculate the COP.**

**Solution:**

$$
RE = h_1 - h_4 = 240 - 95 = 145 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 270 - 240 = 30 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
COP = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{145}{30} = 4.83
$$

> **Interpretation:** For every 1 kJ of compressor work, the system produces 4.83 kJ of cooling. This is a typical COP for a well-designed R-134a refrigeration system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Negative sign convention reminder:** In textbooks, RE is often reported as $(h_1 - h_4) = 145$ kJ/kg (positive magnitude). Work input $(h_2 - h_1) = 30$ kJ/kg is the *magnitude*; the actual sign on the compressor in the energy balance is negative (work in).

---

### 3.6 Numerical Example — COP of LiBr Absorption System

**Problem:** A LiBr–H₂O absorption chiller produces chilled water at 7 °C using generator heat input at 95 °C. The condenser is at 35 °C. The measured refrigeration effect is $Q_e = 1000$ kW and the generator heat is $Q_g = 1500$ kW. The pump work is 5 kW.

**Calculate the COP.**

**Solution:**

$$
COP_{LiBr} = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p} = \frac{1000}{1500 + 5} = \frac{1000}{1505} = 0.664
$$

> **Interpretation:** A COP of 0.66 is typical for a LiBr absorption chiller. The system is much less efficient than a VCRS in terms of work/heat, but it can use *waste* or *low-cost* heat to provide cooling, giving it strong economic and environmental advantages for large AC installations.
