## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Compressor<br/>Raises P and T<br/>Wc in kJ per kg]
    nodeB[Condenser<br/>Rejects Qrej to ambient<br/>High P latent removal]
    nodeC[Expansion Valve<br/>Throttling h3 equals h4<br/>P and T drop]
    nodeD[Evaporator<br/>Absorbs Qe from cold space<br/>Low P latent gain]
    nodeE[Refrigerated Space<br/>Qe extracted]
    nodeF[Surroundings<br/>Qrej dumped]

    nodeA -->|Superheated Vapor h2| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Saturated Liquid h3| nodeC
    nodeC -->|Two Phase h4| nodeD
    nodeD -->|Saturated Vapor h1| nodeA
    nodeE -.->|Qe Heat In| nodeD
    nodeB -.->|Qrej Heat Out| nodeF
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows show refrigerant flow.
- Dashed arrows show thermal energy flow (heat transfer).
- The cycle is **closed**; refrigerant circulates continuously.

---

### 4.2 Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Evaporator<br/>NH3 vaporization<br/>Qe cooling]
    nodeB[Absorber<br/>NH3 absorbed in weak Liq<br/>Qa rejected]
    nodeC[Solution Pump<br/>Lifts rich solution<br/>Wp small]
    nodeD[Generator<br/>Heat Qg in<br/>NH3 desorbed]
    nodeE[Condenser<br/>NH3 condenses<br/>Qc rejected]
    nodeF[Expansion Valve<br/>h3 equals h4<br/>P drops]
    nodeG[Refrigerated Space]
    nodeH[Heat Source<br/>Steam or Waste Heat]

    nodeA -->|NH3 vapor state1| nodeB
    nodeB -->|Rich solution state5| nodeC
    nodeC -->|High P rich state6| nodeD
    nodeD -->|NH3 vapor state2| nodeE
    nodeD -->|Weak solution state7| nodeB
    nodeE -->|Liquid NH3 state3| nodeF
    nodeF -->|Two phase state4| nodeA
    nodeG -.->|Qe| nodeA
    nodeB -.->|Qa out| nodeI[Cooling Tower]
    nodeE -.->|Qc out| nodeI
    nodeH -.->|Qg in| nodeD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **top loop** is the refrigerant circuit: Evaporator → Absorber (vapor) → Pump → Generator (vapor) → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator.
- The **bottom loop** is the absorbent (rich/weak solution) cycle.
- The **only mechanical work** is the small solution pump.
- **All large energy input** is the generator heat $Q_g$.

---

### 4.3 Three-Fluid Refrigeration System (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HIGH["HIGH TEMPERATURE CIRCUIT"]
        nA[Primary Refrigerant<br/>R1 Condenser]
        nB[Primary Expansion Valve]
    end
    subgraph MID["INTERMEDIATE FLUID LOOP"]
        nC[Intermediate Heat Exchanger<br/>R1 evaporates R2 condenses]
        nD[Secondary Pump]
    end
    subgraph LOW["LOW TEMPERATURE CIRCUIT"]
        nE[Secondary Evaporator<br/>R2 absorbs Qe]
        nF[Cold Space]
    end

    nA --> nB
    nB --> nC
    nC --> nD
    nD --> nA
    nC --> nE
    nE --> nC
    nF -.->|Qe| nE
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The intermediate fluid R1/R2 acts as a **thermal buffer** between the high-temperature condenser circuit and the low-temperature evaporator.
- This decoupling allows the use of refrigerants with very different boiling points.
- The COP is typically **higher** than simple VARS but **lower** than cascade VCRS.

---

### 4.4 Lithium Bromide Absorption System (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nA[Evaporator Chiller<br/>Water refrigerant at 5 C]
    nB[Absorber<br/>LiBr absorbs water vapor<br/>Qa rejected]
    nC[Solution Pump<br/>Wp kW small]
    nD[Generator<br/>Steam at 100 C<br/>Qg input]
    nE[Condenser<br/>Water vapor condenses<br/>Qc rejected]
    nF[Expansion Valve<br/>h drops]
    nG[Cooling Tower<br/>Rejects Qa and Qc]
    nH[Steam Source<br/>Boiler or CHP]
    nI[Chilled Water Load<br/>AHU and FCU]

    nA -->|Water vapor| nB
    nB -->|Strong LiBr| nC
    nC -->|High P strong| nD
    nD -->|Water vapor| nE
    nD -->|Weak LiBr| nB
    nE -->|Liquid water| nF
    nF -->|Two phase| nA
    nI -.->|Qe load| nA
    nB -.->|Qa| nG
    nE -.->|Qc| nG
    nH -.->|Qg| nD
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Water is the refrigerant**, **LiBr is the absorbent**.
- Cooling tower removes $Q_a$ and $Q_c$ (both low-grade heat).
- Steam at ~100 °C is the only energy input (no electrical compressor).

---

### 4.5 Cryogenic Liquefaction — Linde–Hampson Cycle (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nA[Compressor<br/>High P gas]
    nB[After Cooler<br/>Removes heat of compression]
    nC[Counter Flow Heat Exchanger<br/>H1 hot stream out and H2 cold stream in]
    nD[Joule Thomson Valve<br/>Isenthalpic h1 equals h2<br/>Cooling]
    nE[Separator<br/>Liquid collected at bottom]
    nF[Liquid Product Storage]
    nG[Unliquefied Gas Recycle]

    nA --> nB
    nB --> nC
    nC --> nD
    nD --> nE
    nE -->|Liquid| nF
    nE -->|Cold vapor| nC
    nC -->|Cold return| nG
    nG -.->|Recycle back| nA
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Gas is compressed, cooled, and then throttled through a J–T valve.
- Cooling produced at the throttle pre-cools the incoming high-pressure gas in the **counter-flow heat exchanger**.
- The unliquefied fraction is recycled.
- The liquefaction fraction $y$ is given by the Linde–Hampson formula derived in Section 3.3.

---

### 4.6 Comparative Architecture — All Systems at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VCRS["VCRS"]
        sA[Compressor]
        sB[Condenser]
        sC[Expansion Valve]
        sD[Evaporator]
    end
    subgraph VARS["VARS NH3 H2O"]
        tA[Generator]
        tB[Condenser]
        tC[Expansion Valve]
        tD[Evaporator]
        tE[Absorber]
        tF[Pump]
    end
    subgraph LIBR["LiBr H2O AC"]
        uA[Generator]
        uB[Condenser]
        uC[Expansion Valve]
        uD[Evaporator]
        uE[Absorber]
        uF[Pump]
    end
    VCRS -->|Electric Work| Energy1[Energy In]
    VARS -->|Heat Qg| Energy1
    LIBR -->|Heat Qg| Energy1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator. How is it different from the COP of a heat pump?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Coefficient of Performance (COP) of a refrigerator** is defined as the ratio of the desired refrigeration effect (heat extracted from the cold space) to the work input required to produce that effect.

**Formula:**
$$
COP_{ref} = \frac{Q_e}{W_{net,in}} = \frac{\text{Desired Cooling}}{\text{Work Input}}
$$

**Difference from Heat Pump COP:**

| Quantity | Refrigerator | Heat Pump |
|---|---|---|
| Desired output | Cooling $Q_e$ | Heating $Q_h$ |
| COP definition | $\dfrac{Q_e}{W}$ | $\dfrac{Q_h}{W}$ |
| Typical value | 2.5 – 4.5 | 3.0 – 5.0 |
| Operating region | $T_e < T_{ambient}$ | $T_h > T_{ambient}$ |

**Key relationship:**
$$
COP_{HP} = COP_{Ref} + 1
$$

since $Q_h = Q_e + W$ from the first law.

> *(Distribution: 1 mark definition, 1 mark difference, 1 mark relationship — total 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List the main components of a simple Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Evaporator** | Refrigerant (e.g., NH₃) evaporates by absorbing heat from the cold space |
| 2 | **Absorber** | Refrigerant vapor is absorbed by the weak solution to form a strong solution; rejects heat of absorption |
| 3 | **Solution Pump** | Pumps the strong solution from low-pressure absorber to high-pressure generator |
| 4 | **Generator (Boiler)** | External heat (steam/waste heat) is supplied to drive refrigerant out of the strong solution |
| 5 | **Condenser** | High-pressure refrigerant vapor rejects heat to the surroundings and condenses to liquid |
| 6 | **Expansion Valve** | Liquid refrigerant is throttled to low pressure and partially flashes to two-phase mixture |

> *(Distribution: 0.5 mark per component × 6 components = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(7 marks) Derive an expression for the COP of an ideal Vapour Compression Refrigeration System (VCRS) and explain each term. State the assumptions clearly.**

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The system operates at steady state.
2. Kinetic and potential energy changes are negligible.
3. Compression is isentropic (reversible adiabatic).
4. Throttling is an isenthalpic process.
5. There is no subcooling or superheating (ideal cycle) — although slight superheat is normal in practice.

**State Point Notation (0.5 mark):**
- State 1: Saturated vapor leaving the evaporator.
- State 2: Superheated vapor leaving the compressor.
- State 3: Saturated liquid leaving the condenser.
- State 4: Two-phase mixture leaving the expansion valve.

**Step 1 — Energy balance on evaporator (1 mark):**

$$
q_e = h_1 - h_4
$$

**Step 2 — Energy balance on compressor (1 mark):**

$$
w_c = h_2 - h_1
$$

**Step 3 — Energy balance on expansion valve (1 mark):**

$$
h_4 = h_3 \quad \text{(isenthalpic throttling)}
$$

**Step 4 — Define COP and simplify (1 mark):**

$$
COP = \frac{q_e}{w_c} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{h_2 - h_1}
$$

Substituting $h_4 = h_3$:

$$
\boxed{COP_{VCR} = \frac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}}
$$

**Step 5 — Significance of terms (0.5 mark):**
- $(h_1 - h_3)$: Refrigeration Effect per kg of refrigerant.
- $(h_2 - h_1)$: Specific compressor work per kg of refrigerant.
- COP is dimensionless; higher COP means more efficient refrigeration.

---

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**(7 marks) A VCR system uses R-134a as refrigerant. The evaporator operates at −10 °C and the condenser at 40 °C. The refrigerant is dry-saturated at the compressor inlet. The isentropic compression gives the following enthalpy values:**

- $h_1 = 244.5$ kJ/kg (saturated vapor at −10 °C)
- $h_2 = 268.0$ kJ/kg (compressor exit, 40 °C condenser)
- $h_3 = 108.3$ kJ/kg (saturated liquid at 40 °C)
- $h_4 = h_3 = 108.3$ kJ/kg (after throttling)

**The required cooling load is 50 kW. Determine:**
1. The COP of the system.
2. The mass flow rate of refrigerant.
3. The compressor power input.
4. The heat rejected at the condenser.

**Model Solution (Step-by-Step, 7 marks):**

**Part (1): COP (1.5 marks)**

$$
RE = h_1 - h_4 = 244.5 - 108.3 = 136.2 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
W_c = h_2 - h_1 = 268.0 - 244.5 = 23.5 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

$$
COP = \frac{RE}{W_c} = \frac{136.2}{23.5} = 5.79
$$

**Part (2): Mass flow rate (2 marks)**

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{\dot{Q}_e}{RE} = \frac{50 \text{ kW}}{136.2 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.367 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Part (3): Compressor power (1.5 marks)**

$$
\dot{W}_c = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_1) = 0.367 \times 23.5 = 8.63 \text{ kW}
$$

**Part (4): Heat rejected at condenser (2 marks)**

$$
\dot{Q}_{rej} = \dot{m} \cdot (h_2 - h_3) = 0.367 \times (268.0 - 108.3) = 0.367 \times 159.7 = 58.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Verification by energy balance (bonus):**

$$
\dot{Q}_{rej} = \dot{Q}_e + \dot{W}_c = 50 + 8.63 = 58.63 \text{ kW} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Tabulated Results:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| COP | 5.79 |
| Mass flow rate | 0.367 kg/s |
| Compressor power | 8.63 kW |
| Heat rejected | 58.6 kW |

> *(Mark distribution: 1.5 + 2 + 1.5 + 2 = 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(7 marks) With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working of a Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System (VARS) using NH₃–H₂O as the working pair. Derive the expression for its COP.**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 marks):** The block diagram is as shown in Section 4.2 of these notes (Absorber → Pump → Generator → Condenser → Expansion Valve → Evaporator → back to Absorber).

**Working Explanation (2 marks):**
- Strong NH₃–H₂O solution in the **absorber** absorbs NH₃ vapor from the evaporator at low pressure.
- The **solution pump** raises the pressure of the strong solution.
- In the **generator**, external heat (Q_g) drives NH₃ out of the solution.
- NH₃ vapor flows to the **condenser**, where it rejects heat (Q_c) and condenses to liquid.
- The liquid NH₃ passes through the **expansion valve** (isenthalpic throttling), dropping to evaporator pressure.
- In the **evaporator**, NH₃ absorbs heat (Q_e) from the cold space and vaporizes.
- The vapor returns to the **absorber**, completing the cycle.
- The weak solution from the generator returns to the absorber through a **pressure-reducing valve** to maintain the absorption capacity.

**Derivation of COP (3 marks):**

For the entire system at steady state (neglecting pump work):

$$
Q_g = Q_e + Q_c + Q_a
$$

where $Q_a$ is the absorber heat rejection.

The **refrigeration effect** is $Q_e$ (desired).

The **work input** is the pump work $W_p$ (small, often neglected).

The **heat input** is the generator heat $Q_g$.

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{COP_{VAR} = \frac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p} \approx \frac{Q_e}{Q_g}}
$$

In terms of enthalpies (per kg of refrigerant):

$$
COP_{VAR} = \frac{h_1 - h_4}{(h_7 - h_6) + f \cdot (h_3 - h_8)}
$$

where $f$ is the solution circulation ratio.

---

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**(7 marks) Explain the working principle of a Lithium Bromide–Water absorption refrigeration system. Why is it preferred for air-conditioning applications but not for sub-zero refrigeration?**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
- **Refrigerant:** Water (evaporates at ~5 °C in the evaporator).
- **Absorbent:** Lithium Bromide (LiBr) solution (highly hygroscopic, non-volatile).
- **Components:** Evaporator, Absorber, Solution Pump, Generator, Condenser, Expansion Valve.

The cycle operates as follows:
1. In the **evaporator**, water refrigerant evaporates at low pressure (~5 °C, 0.87 kPa) by absorbing heat $Q_e$ from the chilled-water circuit.
2. The water vapor is **absorbed** by the weak LiBr solution in the **absorber**, forming a strong solution. Heat of absorption $Q_a$ is rejected to the cooling tower.
3. The **solution pump** lifts the strong LiBr solution to the generator pressure.
4. In the **generator**, external heat $Q_g$ (typically low-pressure steam at 100–200 kPa) drives water vapor out of the strong solution, leaving a weak LiBr solution.
5. The water vapor flows to the **condenser**, where it rejects heat $Q_c$ to the cooling tower and condenses to liquid.
6. The liquid water passes through the **expansion valve** to the evaporator, completing the cycle.
7. The weak LiBr solution from the generator is throttled back to the absorber, completing the solution loop.

**Why Preferred for AC (2 marks):**
- Water is an excellent, **non-toxic, non-flammable, and environmentally safe** refrigerant.
- The system has **no mechanical compressor** → silent, low maintenance, long life.
- It can use **low-grade waste heat or steam** as the energy source.
- COP of 0.6–0.8 is acceptable when waste heat is cheap or free.
- Operating temperatures (5–10 °C chilled water) match **comfort cooling** needs perfectly.

**Why Not for Sub-Zero Refrigeration (1 mark):**
- Water **freezes at 0 °C** (273.15 K), so the refrigerant itself would solidify below this temperature.
- This **physically limits** the evaporator temperature to ~5 °C (well above 0 °C to prevent ice formation).
- Cannot be used for frozen food, ice-making, or cryogenics.
- Risk of **LiBr crystallization** at high concentrations (>66%) and low temperatures, which can plug the system.

> *(Mark distribution: 4 + 2 + 1 = 7 marks.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error on Enthalpy Differences:** Students often write $h_4 - h_1$ or $h_1 - h_2$ (wrong sign). Always remember: $h_1 > h_4$ (refrigeration effect is positive).
> 2. **Confusing VARS and VCRS COP:** VARS has COP < 1 (typically 0.3–0.7) because heat is the input. VCRS has COP > 1 (typically 2.5–5.0). Many students write VARS COP > 1 by mistake.
> 3. **Forgetting the Pump Work Term:** In strict derivations, $W_p$ appears in the denominator. Dropping it is acceptable only if the problem states "neglect pump work."
> 4. **Using °C in Thermodynamic Formulas:** All temperature-dependent ratios (like $T_e / T_g$) require **absolute temperature in Kelvin**. Using °C gives wrong answers and full mark deduction.
> 5. **Mixing Up State Points in VARS:** Confusing state 2 (NH₃ vapor at generator exit) with state 3 (NH₃ liquid at condenser exit) is a frequent error. Memorize the state-point sequence.
> 6. **Calling LiBr the Refrigerant:** LiBr is the **absorbent**, NOT the refrigerant. Water is the refrigerant in the LiBr system. This conceptual error loses 1–2 marks.
> 7. **Missing the Rectifier in NH₃–H₂O System:** A real NH₃–H₂O VARS needs a **rectifier + analyzer** to separate water vapor from NH₃ vapor before the condenser. Skipping this in a block diagram is a partial-marks deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Pin This for Last-Minute Review:**

### **VCRS (Vapour Compression Refrigeration System)**

- ✓ **4 components:** Compressor, Condenser, Expansion Valve, Evaporator.
- ✓ **Refrigeration Effect:** $RE = h_1 - h_4$ (kJ/kg).
- ✓ **Compressor Work:** $W_c = h_2 - h_1$ (kJ/kg).
- ✓ **COP:** $COP_{VCR} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_3}{h_2 - h_1}$ (since $h_3 = h_4$).
- ✓ **Heat Rejected:** $Q_{rej} = h_2 - h_3$ (kJ/kg).
- ✓ **Cycle is closed and dry** — refrigerant does not leave the saturation dome.
- ✓ **P–h diagram** is the standard graphical tool.

### **VARS (Vapour Absorption Refrigeration System)**

- ✓ **6 components:** Evaporator, Absorber, Pump, Generator, Condenser, Expansion Valve.
- ✓ **Energy input:** Heat (Q_g) + small pump work.
- ✓ **Working pair (NH₃–H₂O):** NH₃ refrigerant, H₂O absorbent.
- ✓ **COP:** $COP_{VAR} = \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g + W_p} \approx \dfrac{Q_e}{Q_g}$.
- ✓ **COP is low** (0.3–0.7) but uses **waste heat**.
- ✓ **Silent operation** — no compressor.
- ✓ **Maximum COP:** $COP_{max} = \dfrac{T_e}{T_g - T_e} \cdot \dfrac{T_g}{T_c}$.

### **Three-Fluid Refrigeration**

- ✓ Three working fluids: two refrigerants + one absorbent (or two absorbents + one refrigerant).
- ✓ Used in **dual-temperature** applications.
- ✓ COP improvement of 20–40% over simple VARS.
- ✓ Essentially a **cascade-absorption hybrid** architecture.

### **Lithium Bromide Absorption (LiBr–H₂O)**

- ✓ **Refrigerant:** Water; **Absorbent:** LiBr solution.
- ✓ Used for **chilled-water AC** (5–10 °C chilled water).
- ✓ **NOT suitable for sub-zero** (water freezes at 0 °C).
- ✓ **COP:** 0.6 – 0.8.
- ✓ Energy input: **Steam at 100–200 kPa** or hot water.
- ✓ Heat rejected at absorber + condenser to **cooling tower**.

### **Cryogenics**

- ✓ Defined as **T < 120 K (−153 °C)**.
- ✓ **Liquefaction methods:** J-T throttling, cascade, isentropic expansion.
- ✓ **Linde–Hampson liquefaction fraction:** $y = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_f}$.
- ✓ **Joule–Thomson coefficient:** $\mu_{JT} = \left( \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P} \right)_h$.
- ✓ Key fluids: N₂ (77 K), O₂ (90 K), H₂ (20 K), He (4.2 K).
- ✓ Applications: **LNG, MRI, space propulsion, food flash-freezing, superconductivity**.

### **Cross-Cutting Comparison Table (Master Reference)**

| Feature | VCRS | VARS NH₃–H₂O | LiBr–H₂O |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy input | Electricity | Heat | Heat |
| COP | 2.5–4.5 | 0.3–0.7 | 0.6–0.8 |
| Noise | High | Low | Very low |
| Sub-zero | Yes | Yes | No |
| Typical use | Domestic fridge, AC | Industrial cold | Commercial AC |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cryogenic Freezers; Air Conditioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT414 - REFRIGERATION AND COLD CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cryogenic Freezers; Air Conditioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cryogenic Freezers — Formal Definition

**Cryogenic Freezing** is a rapid freezing process that uses extremely low-temperature cryogenic fluids (typically boiling below **−150 °C / 123 K**) to achieve ultra-fast heat extraction from food, biological, or pharmaceutical products. The most widely used industrial cryogens are **Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂)** boiling at **−195.79 °C (77.36 K)** and **Liquid Carbon Dioxide (LCO₂)** at **−78.5 °C (194.65 K) at atmospheric pressure**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board Exam Standard):** "Cryogenic freezing is a non-mechanical refrigeration process in which the latent heat of vaporization of a cryogenic fluid (LN₂ or LCO₂) is utilized to remove sensible and latent heat from a product, achieving freezing rates of 1–5 cm/h of product thickness, far exceeding conventional mechanical freezers."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Frost Bite in 90 Seconds"

Imagine dipping a fresh fish fillet into a tub of liquid nitrogen (−196 °C). The moment the fillet touches the LN₂, the liquid violently boils and vaporizes, sucking enormous amounts of heat out of the fish in seconds. This is the **latent heat of vaporization** (199.3 kJ/kg for LN₂) doing the cooling work. Now scale this up to a conveyorized industrial tunnel: products ride through a chamber where LN₂ is sprayed, flash-frozen, and the boiled-off nitrogen gas (safe, inert) is vented. The fish's cell walls barely have time to rupture, so when thawed, it tastes as if freshly caught.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Cryogenic Freezing Matters in Cold Chain:** Conventional mechanical freezers (R-404A cascade) struggle below −80 °C. For premium seafood, sushi-grade tuna, biotech samples, and vaccines (mRNA, viral vectors), only cryogenics can reach **−80 °C to −196 °C** fast enough to form micro-crystals (≤ 10 µm) — the gold standard for texture and viability preservation.

## 1.3 Air Conditioning — Formal Definition

**Air Conditioning** is the simultaneous control of **temperature, humidity, cleanliness (air purity), and air motion/distribution** within an enclosed space to provide human thermal comfort (PMV/PPD model) or to maintain specific environmental conditions required by industrial/biological processes.

The four pillars of air conditioning, often tested in KTU exams, are:

1. **Temperature control** (Cooling in summer, Heating in winter)
2. **Humidity control** (Dehumidification in summer, Humidification in winter)
3. **Air circulation & distribution** (Mixing, ducting, throw)
4. **Air filtration & purification** (HEPA, activated carbon, UV)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** "Air conditioning is the process of treating air so as to control simultaneously its temperature, humidity, cleanliness, and distribution to meet the requirements of the conditioned space, expressed through psychrometric processes on a psychrometric chart."

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy — "The Body's Personal Weather System"

Your body generates ~100 W of metabolic heat at rest, and ~1 kW during exercise. On a hot, humid day, your skin can't release this heat efficiently (sweat doesn't evaporate). An air conditioner acts as a **mechanical second skin** — it does three things simultaneously: (1) cools the air so heat flows from skin to air by convection, (2) condenses moisture from the air (dehumidification) so your sweat can evaporate easily, and (3) circulates fresh, filtered air so you don't suffocate on CO₂. The cooling coil inside the AC unit is essentially your "artificial sweat gland" — refrigerant evaporates inside it, absorbing both sensible heat (temperature drop) and latent heat (moisture condensation on coil fins).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** Air conditioning accounts for ~40 % of total electricity consumption in commercial buildings in India (BEE Star Rating mandate). The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes psychrometric analysis, energy-efficient system design, and the use of low-GWP refrigerants (R-32, R-290, R-1234yf) under the Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol.

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Constants Table

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boiling point of Liquid Nitrogen (LN₂) | T_b | **−195.79 °C (77.36 K)** | Cryogenic freezer primary fluid |
| Boiling point of Liquid CO₂ | T_b | **−78.5 °C (194.65 K)** | Cryogenic freezer secondary fluid |
| Latent heat of vaporization of LN₂ | h_fg | **199.3 kJ/kg** | Heat removal capacity of LN₂ |
| Latent heat of vaporization of LCO₂ | h_fg | **571.1 kJ/kg** | Heat removal capacity of LCO₂ |
| Density of LN₂ | ρ | **808 kg/m³** | Storage tank sizing |
| Density of LCO₂ | ρ | **770 kg/m³** | Storage tank sizing |
| Latent heat of vaporization of water at 0 °C | h_fg | **2501 kJ/kg** | Air conditioning latent load |
| Specific heat of dry air (c_pa) | c_pa | **1.005 kJ/kg·K** | Sensible heat calculation |
| Specific heat of water vapor (c_pv) | c_pv | **1.82 kJ/kg·K** | Psychrometric enthalpy |
| Universal Gas Constant | R | **8.314 J/mol·K** | Ideal gas calculations |
| Atmospheric pressure (standard) | P_atm | **101.325 kPa** | Psychrometric reference |
| BEE Star Rating (India) | EER/ISEER | **1★: 2.7, 5★: ≥ 4.5** | Energy efficiency benchmark |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Cryogenic Freezer — Operational Theory

### 2.1.1 The Three Industrial Cryogenic Freezing Modes

**A) Immersion Freezing (Liquid Bath)**
- Product is dipped directly into a bath of LN₂ or LCO₂.
- **Highest heat transfer coefficient** (h ≈ 500–1500 W/m²K for LN₂ nucleate boiling).
- Used for: small individual items (berries, shrimp, diced meat).
- **Disadvantage:** Product surface may crack due to ultra-rapid thermal shock; some products absorb cryogen (porous items).

**B) Spray Freezing (Cryo-Tunnel)**
- LN₂ is sprayed as a fine mist onto products moving on a conveyor through an insulated tunnel.
- Boiled-off N₂ gas continues to pre-cool incoming product (counter-flow heat exchange).
- **Most common industrial configuration** for IQF (Individually Quick Frozen) products.
- Typical residence time: **3 to 15 minutes** depending on product size.

**C) Cryomechanical Freezing (Hybrid)**
- A mechanical pre-cooler brings product to ~−30 °C, then a cryogenic section rapidly drops it to −80 °C to −196 °C.
- **Best of both worlds:** lower cryogen consumption + ultra-low final temperature.
- Used for premium seafood (tuna, swordfish) and pharmaceutical/biotech applications.

### 2.1.2 Why Cryogenics → Micro-Crystallization

When water freezes inside a cell, it forms ice crystals. **Slow freezing** (conventional freezer at −18 °C) forms large extracellular crystals that rupture cell walls → drip loss on thaw, mushy texture. **Fast freezing** (cryogenic) forms **small intracellular crystals** (< 10 µm) that don't damage membranes → product retains texture, water-holding capacity, and nutritional value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Freezing Rate Classification (IIR — International Institute of Refrigeration):**
> - **Slow:** < 0.5 cm/h → macro-crystals → poor quality
> - **Fast:** 0.5–3 cm/h → acceptable for most foods
> - **Very Fast / Cryogenic:** 3–10 cm/h → micro-crystals → premium quality
> - **Ultra-Fast:** > 10 cm/h → vitrification possible (amorphous ice)

### 2.1.3 The Mass & Energy Balance of LN₂ Freezing

For a product entering at temperature T_i and exiting at final temperature T_f, the total heat to be removed per kg of product:

$$Q_{total} = Q_{sensible\,(above\,freezing)} + Q_{latent\,(freezing)} + Q_{sensible\,(below\,freezing)}$$

$$Q = c_{p,\,ice}(T_f - T_{fp}) + L_{fusion} + c_{p,\,product}(T_{fp} - T_i)$$

The cryogen required (assuming LN₂ is vaporized and warmed to 0 °C before venting):

$$m_{LN_2} = \frac{Q_{product}}{h_{fg,\,LN_2} + c_{p,\,N_2}(T_{vent} - T_{b,\,LN_2})}$$

Where:
- $h_{fg,\,LN_2} = 199.3$ kJ/kg (latent heat at −196 °C)
- $c_{p,\,N_2} \approx 1.04$ kJ/kg·K (vapor N₂)
- $T_{vent} \approx$ 0 °C (typical design)
- Therefore effective cooling per kg LN₂ ≈ **427 kJ/kg**

### 2.1.4 Key Design Parameters of Cryogenic Freezers

1. **Heat Transfer Coefficient (h):** For spray freezing, h ≈ 200–500 W/m²K. For immersion, h ≈ 1000–1500 W/m²K (nucleate boiling regime).
2. **Freezing Time (t):** Estimated using Plank's equation (modified for cryogenic):
$$t = \frac{\rho L}{T_i - T_\infty}\left(\frac{P\cdot d}{h} + \frac{R\cdot d^2}{k}\right)$$
Where P and R are Plank's geometric constants (P = 1/6 for slab, R = 1/8 for slab, 1/16 for cylinder, 1/24 for sphere).
3. **Cryogen Loss Rate:** Typically **1.0–1.5 kg LN₂ per kg of product** for food freezing.
4. **Insulation:** Tunnel walls use vacuum-insulated panels (VIP) or multilayer radiant barriers; LN₂ boil-off from storage tank ~ 0.3–0.5 % per day.

## 2.2 Air Conditioning — Operational Theory

### 2.2.1 Psychrometric Properties (Required Foundation)

Psychrometry is the study of **moist air** (a binary mixture of dry air + water vapor). Every KTU problem in air conditioning starts here.

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dry Bulb Temperature | DBT / T | Air temperature measured by ordinary thermometer | °C |
| Wet Bulb Temperature | WBT / T_w | Temperature measured with bulb covered by wet wick, under forced ventilation | °C |
| Dew Point Temperature | DPT / T_dp | Temperature at which condensation begins when air is cooled at constant pressure | °C |
| Relative Humidity | φ / RH | Ratio of partial pressure of water vapor to saturation pressure at DBT | % (0–100) |
| Specific Humidity / Humidity Ratio | ω / W | Mass of water vapor per kg of dry air | kg water/kg dry air |
| Specific Enthalpy | h | Total heat content per kg of dry air (sensible + latent) | kJ/kg dry air |
| Specific Volume | v | Volume per kg of dry air | m³/kg dry air |
| Degree of Saturation | μ | Ratio of specific humidity to saturation specific humidity at DBT | — |

### 2.2.2 Governing Psychrometric Equations (Master Set)

**Specific Humidity (ω):**
$$\omega = 0.622\,\frac{P_v}{P - P_v}$$

Where $P_v$ = partial pressure of water vapor (kPa), $P$ = total pressure (kPa).

**Saturation Pressure of Water Vapor (Magnus equation, valid 0–60 °C):**
$$P_{vs} = 0.61078\,\exp\left(\frac{17.27\,T}{T + 237.3}\right) \quad \text{[kPa]}$$

**Relative Humidity:**
$$\phi = \frac{P_v}{P_{vs}(T)} = \frac{P_{vs}(T_{dp})}{P_{vs}(T)}$$

**Specific Enthalpy of Moist Air (per kg dry air):**
$$h = c_{pa}\,T + \omega\,(h_{fg,\,0} + c_{pv}\,T)$$
$$h = 1.005\,T + \omega\,(2501 + 1.82\,T) \quad \text{[kJ/kg dry air]}$$

**Specific Volume of Moist Air:**
$$v = \frac{R_a\,T}{P - P_v} = \frac{0.287\,T}{P - P_v} \quad \text{[m³/kg dry air]}$$

**Adiabatic Saturation Temperature (= WBT for DBT 0–100 °C):**
$$h_1 + (W_s - W_1)\,h_{fg,\,T_{wb}} = h_s$$

### 2.2.3 Eight Fundamental Psychrometric Processes

| # | Process | Property that Changes | Property that Remains Constant | Direction on Chart |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensible Heating | T, h | ω (humidity ratio) | Horizontal right |
| 2 | Sensible Cooling | T, h | ω | Horizontal left |
| 3 | Humidification (water spray) | ω, h | T (ideally) | Vertical up |
| 4 | Dehumidification | ω, h | T (ideally) | Vertical down |
| 5 | Cooling & Dehumidification | T, ω, h | — | Diagonal down-left |
| 6 | Heating & Humidification | T, ω, h | — | Diagonal up-right |
| 7 | Mixing of two air streams | T, ω, h | — | Straight line on chart |
| 8 | Adiabatic saturation / Evaporative cooling | T, h | WBT (adiabatic) | Along constant WBT |

### 2.2.4 Cooling Coil Analysis — Bypass Factor (BPF)

The **most heavily tested KTU concept in Module 3.** When air passes through a cooling coil, not all air contacts the coil surface (some bypasses). Define:

- $T_1$ = DBT of air entering coil
- $T_2$ = DBT of air leaving coil
- $T_3$ = Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) — temperature coil surface would bring air to if 100 % contact
- $BPF$ = fraction of air that **bypasses** the coil (0 to 1)
- $CF$ = Contact Factor = 1 − BPF

$$\boxed{BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_3}{T_1 - T_3} = 1 - \frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_3}}$$

### 2.2.5 Sensible Heat Factor (SHF) & Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF)

**Sensible Heat Factor (SHF):** of a cooling coil
$$SHF_{coil} = \frac{SH_{coil}}{SH_{coil} + LH_{coil}} = \frac{c_{pa}(T_1 - T_2)}{c_{pa}(T_1 - T_2) + h_{fg}(\omega_1 - \omega_2)}$$

**Room Sensible Heat Factor (RSHF):** of the conditioned space
$$RSHF = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH}$$

Where:
- **RSH** = Room Sensible Heat (walls, windows, occupants, lights, equipment)
- **RLH** = Room Latent Heat (moisture from occupants, cooking, infiltration)

**Critical KTU Rule:** For the system to work, **RSHF must equal SHF_coil** (psychrometric equilibrium). If RSHF > SHF_coil → room too warm/humid. If RSHF < SHF_coil → room overcooled/dry.

### 2.2.6 Air Conditioning Systems Classification

| Type | Capacity | Application | Features |
|---|---|---|---|
| Window AC | 0.75–2 TR | Small rooms | Single unit, noisy |
| Split AC | 0.75–3 TR | Bedrooms, offices | Quiet indoor unit |
| Cassette AC | 2–5 TR | Commercial ceiling | 4-way air throw |
| Ducted/Packaged AC | 3–20 TR | Large halls, restaurants | Ducted distribution |
| Central Chilled Water AC | 50–5000 TR | Malls, airports, hospitals | Chiller + AHU + FCU |
| VRF/VRV | 4–60 HP | Variable load buildings | Inverter-driven, multi-zone |
| Precision AC | 5–50 TR | Data centers | Tight T (±1 °C) & RH (±5 %) control |

> [!NOTE]
> **TR (Ton of Refrigeration) = 3.517 kW** (heat removal rate to freeze 1 ton of ice in 24 hours).

### 2.2.7 Energy Efficiency Metrics

- **EER (Energy Efficiency Ratio)** = Cooling output (W) / Power input (W) — at single operating point
- **COP (Coefficient of Performance)** = Q_L / W_net (dimensionless)
- **SEER (Seasonal EER)** — weighted across cooling season
- **ISEER (Indian Seasonal EER)** — Indian standard (BEE) since 2015
- **ISEER Formula (BEE):**
$$ISEER = \frac{\sum T_j \cdot PLF_j \cdot C_j}{\sum T_j \cdot PLF_j \cdot P_j}$$
Where $T_j$ = bin hours, $PLF_j$ = part load factor, $C_j$ = cooling capacity, $P_j$ = power.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use Case | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Sensible heat load | kW |
| 2 | $Q = m \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta\omega$ | Latent heat load | kW |
| 3 | $Q = m_{LN_2} \cdot (h_{fg,LN_2} + c_{p,N_2} \cdot \Delta T_{vent})$ | LN₂ mass required | kW, kg/s |
| 4 | $\omega = 0.622 \cdot \frac{P_v}{P - P_v}$ | Humidity ratio | kg/kg |
| 5 | $h = 1.005\,T + \omega(2501 + 1.82\,T)$ | Moist air enthalpy | kJ/kg d.a. |
| 6 | $v = \frac{0.287\,T}{P - P_v}$ | Specific volume | m³/kg d.a. |
| 7 | $BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_3}{T_1 - T_3}$ | Cooling coil analysis | — |
| 8 | $SHF = \frac{c_{pa}(T_1 - T_2)}{c_{pa}(T_1 - T_2) + h_{fg}(\omega_1 - \omega_2)}$ | Coil SHF | — |
| 9 | $RSHF = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH}$ | Room SHF | — |
| 10 | $m_a = \frac{Q_{total}}{h_1 - h_2}$ (kg d.a./s) | Supply air mass flow | kg/s |
| 11 | $TR = 3.517$ kW = 200 BTU/min | Cooling capacity conversion | kW |
| 12 | $COP = \frac{Q_L}{W_{net}}$ | Refrigeration performance | — |
| 13 | $P_{vs} = 0.61078 \cdot e^{17.27 T / (T + 237.3)}$ | Saturation pressure (kPa) | kPa |
| 14 | $EER = \frac{\dot{Q}_{cooling}}{P_{input}}$ | AC efficiency | W/W |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — LN₂ Cryogenic Freezer Design Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
A cryogenic freezer processes **500 kg/h of fish fillets** from an initial temperature of **+5 °C** down to a final temperature of **−40 °C**. The fish has:
- Specific heat above freezing: c_p,above = **3.5 kJ/kg·K**
- Specific heat below freezing: c_p,below = **1.8 kJ/kg·K**
- Latent heat of fusion: L = **235 kJ/kg**
- Initial moisture content: 70 % (so water fraction = 0.70)
- Freezing point: −2 °C

The cryogen is **LN₂** at −196 °C. The vented N₂ gas leaves at **+10 °C**.
Find: **(a)** Total heat load (kW), **(b)** LN₂ consumption (kg/h), **(c)** Tunnel length if 1.5 kW/m² average heat flux.

### Step (a): Total Heat Load

The freezing process involves three stages:

**Stage 1: Sensible cooling from +5 °C to −2 °C (above freezing)**
$$Q_1 = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,above} \cdot \Delta T_1$$
$$Q_1 = 500 \times 3.5 \times (5 - (-2)) = 500 \times 3.5 \times 7$$
$$Q_1 = 12{,}250 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Stage 2: Latent heat of freezing (at −2 °C)**
$$Q_2 = \dot{m} \cdot x_{water} \cdot L$$
$$Q_2 = 500 \times 0.70 \times 235 = 82{,}250 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Stage 3: Sensible cooling from −2 °C to −40 °C (below freezing)**
$$Q_3 = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,below} \cdot \Delta T_3$$
$$Q_3 = 500 \times 1.8 \times (-2 - (-40)) = 500 \times 1.8 \times 38$$
$$Q_3 = 34{,}200 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Total Heat Load:**
$$Q_{total} = Q_1 + Q_2 + Q_3 = 12{,}250 + 82{,}250 + 34{,}200$$
$$\boxed{Q_{total} = 128{,}700 \text{ kJ/h} = 35.75 \text{ kW}}$$

### Step (b): LN₂ Consumption

The effective specific cooling capacity of LN₂ (vaporized at −196 °C and warmed to +10 °C):
$$q_{LN_2} = h_{fg,LN_2} + c_{p,N_2} \cdot (T_{vent} - T_{b,LN_2})$$
$$q_{LN_2} = 199.3 + 1.04 \times (10 - (-196))$$
$$q_{LN_2} = 199.3 + 1.04 \times 206 = 199.3 + 214.24$$
$$q_{LN_2} = 413.54 \text{ kJ/kg LN}_2$$

**LN₂ mass flow rate:**
$$\dot{m}_{LN_2} = \frac{Q_{total}}{q_{LN_2}} = \frac{128{,}700}{413.54}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{LN_2} = 311.2 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**Specific LN₂ consumption per kg of fish:**
$$q_{specific} = \frac{311.2}{500} = 0.622 \text{ kg LN}_2/\text{kg fish}$$

### Step (c): Tunnel Length (Heat Flux Based)

Given average heat flux $\dot{q}'' = 1.5$ kW/m²:
$$A_{required} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\dot{q}''} = \frac{35.75}{1.5} = 23.83 \text{ m}^2$$

For a typical tunnel with width W = 1.2 m, perimeter contacting product (top + bottom):
$$L = \frac{A}{2W} = \frac{23.83}{2 \times 1.2} = 9.93 \text{ m}$$
$$\boxed{L \approx 10 \text{ m (tunnel length)}}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Air Conditioning: Coil Bypass Factor, SHF, Air Flow

**Problem Statement (KTU Model):**
An air-conditioned hall has:
- Outdoor conditions: DBT = 35 °C, WBT = 27 °C, P = 101.325 kPa
- Required indoor conditions: DBT = 25 °C, RH = 50 %
- Room Sensible Heat: RSH = 20 kW
- Room Latent Heat: RLH = 10 kW
- Cooling coil ADP = 10 °C

**Find:** (a) RSHF, (b) Required SHF of coil, (c) BPF of coil, (d) Coil leaving DBT and humidity ratio, (e) Supply air mass flow.

### Step (a): Compute Room Sensible Heat Factor
$$\boxed{RSHF = \frac{RSH}{RSH + RLH} = \frac{20}{20 + 10} = 0.667}$$

### Step (b): Required Coil SHF
For thermal equilibrium: **SHF_coil = RSHF**
$$\boxed{SHF_{coil} = 0.667}$$

### Step (c): BPF from SHF

First, find air properties at state 1 (35 °C DBT, 27 °C WBT):
- $P_{vs}(35°C) = 0.61078 \cdot e^{17.27 \times 35 / (35 + 237.3)} = 0.61078 \cdot e^{2.218} = 0.61078 \times 9.193 = 5.616$ kPa
- $P_v = P_{vs}(27°C) - (P - P_{vs}(27°C)) \cdot (T - T_w)/(1530 - 1.3\,T_w)$ (WBT formula)
  - $P_{vs}(27°C) = 0.61078 \cdot e^{17.27 \times 27 / (27 + 237.3)} = 0.61078 \cdot e^{1.761} = 0.61078 \times 5.815 = 3.552$ kPa
  - $P_v = 3.552 - (101.325 - 3.552) \cdot (35 - 27)/(1530 - 1.3 \times 27) = 3.552 - 97.773 \times 8/1494.9$
  - $P_v = 3.552 - 0.523 = 3.029$ kPa
- $\omega_1 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.029}{101.325 - 3.029} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{3.029}{98.296} = 0.01917$ kg/kg
- $h_1 = 1.005(35) + 0.01917(2501 + 1.82 \times 35) = 35.175 + 0.01917 \times 2564.7 = 35.175 + 49.165 = 84.34$ kJ/kg

At ADP (10 °C, saturated, RH = 100%):
- $P_{vs}(10°C) = 0.61078 \cdot e^{17.27 \times 10 / (10 + 237.3)} = 0.61078 \cdot e^{0.6994} = 0.61078 \times 2.013 = 1.229$ kPa
- $\omega_3 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.229}{101.325 - 1.229} = 0.622 \times 0.01226 = 0.00763$ kg/kg
- $h_3 = 1.005(10) + 0.00763(2501 + 1.82 \times 10) = 10.05 + 0.00763 \times 2519.2 = 10.05 + 19.22 = 29.27$ kJ/kg

**Apply SHF to find leaving enthalpy:**
$$SHF = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_3} \cdot \frac{1}{(1.005\,(T_1 - T_2) / (h_1 - h_3) + \omega\text{-component})}$$

Better approach — use the standard relation for coil with SHF:
$$h_2 = h_1 - SHF_{coil} \cdot (h_1 - h_3)$$
$$h_2 = 84.34 - 0.667 \times (84.34 - 29.27) = 84.34 - 0.667 \times 55.07$$
$$h_2 = 84.34 - 36.73 = 47.61 \text{ kJ/kg d.a.}$$

### Step (d): Find DBT₂ and ω₂

The coil process is on a straight line from state 1 to ADP (state 3) on the psychrometric chart. The BPF is the ratio along this line.

Let BPF = B. Then:
$$T_2 = T_1 - (1-B)(T_1 - T_3) = 35 - (1-B)(25) = 35 - 25 + 25B = 10 + 25B$$
$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 - (1-B)(\omega_1 - \omega_3) = 0.01917 - (1-B)(0.01154)$$

Enthalpy at state 2:
$$h_2 = 1.005 \cdot T_2 + \omega_2 \cdot (2501 + 1.82 \cdot T_2)$$

Substituting and solving for B gives **BPF ≈ 0.18 to 0.22** for typical 6-row chilled water coil.
$$\boxed{BPF \approx 0.20}$$

Therefore:
$$T_2 = 10 + 25 \times 0.20 = 15.0 \text{ °C}$$
$$\omega_2 = 0.01917 - 0.80 \times 0.01154 = 0.01917 - 0.00923 = 0.00994 \text{ kg/kg}$$

### Step (e): Supply Air Mass Flow

Total room heat = 30 kW. Supply air enthalpy drop:
$$\Delta h = h_1 - h_2 = 84.34 - 47.61 = 36.73 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$\dot{m}_{a} = \frac{Q_{total}}{\Delta h} = \frac{30 \text{ kW}}{36.73 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.817 \text{ kg/s}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{m}_a = 2{,}941 \text{ kg/h} = 81.7 \text{ kg/min}}$$

In volume flow (at supply condition, v ≈ 0.85 m³/kg):
$$\dot{V} = 0.817 \times 0.85 \times 3600 = 2{,}500 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 694.4 \text{ CFM}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use What (KTU Decision Tree)

| Given | Find | Approach |
|---|---|---|
| DBT + WBT | ω, h, v | Use psychrometric chart or Magnus eqn. |
| DBT + RH | ω, h, v | Compute P_v = φ · P_vs(T) |
| DBT + DPT | ω, h | ω is same as at DPT (saturation); P_v = P_vs(T_dp) |
| ADP + BPF | T_2, ω_2 | Use BPF formula along the process line |
| RSH, RLH | RSHF | Direct ratio |
| RSHF + Coil SHF | Check feasibility | RSHF must equal coil SHF |
| Total Q + Δh | Air flow m_a | m_a = Q_total / (h_1 − h_2) |
| Q + UA + LMTD | Heat exchanger area | A = Q / (U · LMTD) |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]
**"Define cryogenic freezing. List any four advantages of cryogenic freezing over conventional mechanical freezing."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Cryogenic freezing is a rapid freezing technique that uses extremely low-temperature cryogenic fluids like liquid nitrogen (LN₂, −196 °C) or liquid carbon dioxide (LCO₂, −78.5 °C) to extract heat from food, biological, or pharmaceutical products at very high rates (1–5 cm/h), forming micro-crystalline ice that preserves product quality.

**Four Advantages (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Ultra-rapid freezing rates** (3–10 cm/h) — micro-crystals (< 10 µm) preserve cell structure and reduce drip loss on thawing.
2. **Lower product dehydration** (no air movement in some configurations) — minimal surface shrinkage, better weight retention.
3. **No mechanical refrigeration cycle** — eliminates compressor, condenser, evaporator maintenance; system is mechanically simple.
4. **Hygienic & bacteriostatic** — very low temperatures (-80 to -196 °C) immediately arrest microbial growth, extending shelf life.
5. (Bonus) **Compact equipment footprint** — high heat transfer rate allows smaller freezer area.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**"Define Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) and Bypass Factor (BPF) of a cooling coil. Why is BPF important in AC design?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**ADP Definition (1 mark):** Apparatus Dew Point (ADP) is the temperature to which air can be cooled by the cooling coil, assuming 100% contact between air and coil surface. It is the saturation temperature corresponding to the coil surface temperature.

**BPF Definition (1 mark):** Bypass Factor (BPF) is the ratio of the difference between leaving DBT and ADP to the difference between entering DBT and ADP:
$$BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$$

**Importance of BPF in AC Design (1 mark):**
- A **lower BPF** (more rows, higher coil velocity) means closer approach to saturation, more dehumidification, but higher fan power.
- A **higher BPF** (fewer rows) means less coil contact, primarily sensible cooling, but reduced energy efficiency.
- BPF governs **SHF of the coil** — must be matched to RSHF of the space; otherwise the room condition drifts from the design setpoint.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Common mistakes that cost marks in this module:
> 1. **Forgetting the latent heat of fusion of water in product** — always include L = 235 kJ/kg (water fraction).
> 2. **Using DBT where WBT is required** — check which temperature is given; WBT implies adiabatic saturation, not simple DBT.
> 3. **Sign errors in BPF formula** — write it as $\frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$, NOT $\frac{T_1 - T_2}{T_1 - T_{ADP}}$ (that one is the contact factor 1−BPF).
> 4. **Ignoring moisture content** in product heat load — fish is 70% water, but dried foods are < 10%.
> 5. **Mixing LN₂ vapor sensible and latent components** — when LN₂ is vented at 10 °C, the full formula is $h_{fg,LN_2} + c_{p,N_2}(T_{vent} - T_b)$ — students often forget the sensible part.

---

### Question A: [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** A cryogenic freezer uses liquid nitrogen to freeze **800 kg/h of beef** from +5 °C to −30 °C. The product has 65% moisture, c_p above freezing = 3.2 kJ/kg·K, c_p below freezing = 1.7 kJ/kg·K, latent heat of fusion = 235 kJ/kg, freezing point = −1.5 °C. LN₂ enters at −196 °C and vapor leaves at +5 °C. Calculate: (i) Total heat load in kW, (ii) LN₂ consumption in kg/h, (iii) Specific LN₂ consumption per kg of product.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Explain with neat sketches the **IQF cryogenic tunnel** and the **immersion cryogenic freezer**. Compare their heat transfer coefficients and typical applications.

### Solution A:

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Total Heat Load:**

Three-stage heat removal:

**Stage 1 — Sensible cooling from +5 °C to −1.5 °C:**
$$Q_1 = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,above} \cdot \Delta T_1 = 800 \times 3.2 \times (5 - (-1.5)) = 800 \times 3.2 \times 6.5$$
$$Q_1 = 16{,}640 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Stage 2 — Latent heat of freezing:**
$$Q_2 = \dot{m} \cdot x_{water} \cdot L = 800 \times 0.65 \times 235$$
$$Q_2 = 122{,}200 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Stage 3 — Sensible cooling below freezing:**
$$Q_3 = \dot{m} \cdot c_{p,below} \cdot \Delta T_3 = 800 \times 1.7 \times (-1.5 - (-30)) = 800 \times 1.7 \times 28.5$$
$$Q_3 = 38{,}760 \text{ kJ/h}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = \frac{16{,}640 + 122{,}200 + 38{,}760}{3600} = \frac{177{,}600}{3600} = 49.33 \text{ kW}}$$

**(ii) LN₂ Consumption:**

Effective cooling per kg LN₂:
$$q_{LN_2} = h_{fg,LN_2} + c_{p,N_2} \cdot (T_{vent} - T_b)$$
$$q_{LN_2} = 199.3 + 1.04 \times (5 - (-196)) = 199.3 + 1.04 \times 201 = 199.3 + 209.04$$
$$q_{LN_2} = 408.34 \text{ kJ/kg LN}_2$$

$$\dot{m}_{LN_2} = \frac{Q_{total} \times 3600}{q_{LN_2}} = \frac{49.33 \times 3600}{408.34} = \frac{177{,}600}{408.34}$$
$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{LN_2} = 434.93 \text{ kg/h}}$$

**(iii) Specific consumption:**
$$q_{spec} = \frac{434.93}{800} = \boxed{0.544 \text{ kg LN}_2/\text{kg beef}}$$

This is below the typical 1.0–1.5 kg/kg — indicating the calculation omits tunnel heat-infiltration losses (which would add 15–25 % to LN₂ use in real design).

#### Part (b) — IQF Tunnel vs. Immersion Freezer

**IQF Cryogenic Tunnel (Spray Freezer):**
- LN₂ is atomized through spray nozzles above and below a conveyor belt.
- Product travels through a multi-zone tunnel: pre-cool, spray, deep-freeze, tempering.
- Heat transfer coefficient: **h ≈ 200–500 W/m²K**.
- Best for: IQF berries, diced vegetables, shrimp, fish fillets.
- **Advantage:** Continuous operation, uniform freezing, individual quick freezing of loose pieces.

**Immersion Cryogenic Freezer:**
- Product is dipped directly into a bath of LN₂ or LCO₂.
- Boiling regime (Leidenfrost/Nukiyama transition) gives very high h.
- Heat transfer coefficient: **h ≈ 1000–1500 W/m²K** (nucleate boiling).
- Best for: dense products in bulk (large fish blocks, packaged meat).
- **Advantage:** Fastest possible freezing (5–10 cm/h), uniform surface.
- **Disadvantage:** Product may absorb cryogen; harder to integrate with continuous conveyors.

---

### Question B: [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** Moist air at DBT = 38 °C and WBT = 26 °C is cooled and dehumidified by a cooling coil with ADP = 12 °C. The coil BPF = 0.25. Find: (i) humidity ratio of entering air, (ii) DBT and humidity ratio of leaving air, (iii) SHF of the coil.

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Describe the **vapour compression refrigeration cycle** used in a split air conditioner with a P-h diagram. Explain the role of each component and the effect of using R-32 vs R-410A refrigerant.

### Solution B:

#### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Humidity ratio of entering air (State 1):**

Use Magnus equation for saturation pressures:
$$P_{vs}(38°C) = 0.61078 \cdot e^{17.27 \times 38 / (38 + 237.3)} = 0.61078 \cdot e^{2.376}$$
$$P_{vs}(38°C) = 0.61078 \times 10.764 = 6.575 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{vs}(26°C) = 0.61078 \cdot e^{17.27 \times 26 / (26 + 237.3)} = 0.61078 \cdot e^{1.704}$$
$$P_{vs}(26°C) = 0.61078 \times 5.498 = 3.358 \text{ kPa}$$

Using the relation $P_v = P_{vs}(T_w) - \frac{(P - P_{vs}(T_w))(T - T_w)}{1530 - 1.3\,T_w}$:
$$P_v = 3.358 - \frac{(101.325 - 3.358)(38 - 26)}{1530 - 1.3 \times 26}$$
$$P_v = 3.358 - \frac{97.967 \times 12}{1496.2} = 3.358 - 0.7857$$
$$P_v = 2.572 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\omega_1 = 0.622 \cdot \frac{2.572}{101.325 - 2.572} = 0.622 \times \frac{2.572}{98.753}$$
$$\boxed{\omega_1 = 0.01620 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}}$$

**(ii) DBT and humidity ratio of leaving air (State 2):**

At ADP = 12 °C, RH = 100%:
$$P_{vs}(12°C) = 0.61078 \cdot e^{17.27 \times 12 / (12 + 237.3)} = 0.61078 \cdot e^{0.8315}$$
$$P_{vs}(12°C) = 0.61078 \times 2.2965 = 1.403 \text{ kPa}$$

$$\omega_{ADP} = 0.622 \cdot \frac{1.403}{101.325 - 1.403} = 0.622 \times 0.01403 = 0.00873 \text{ kg/kg}$$

**Apply BPF formula for DBT₂:**
$$BPF = \frac{T_2 - T_{ADP}}{T_1 - T_{ADP}} \implies T_2 = T_{ADP} + BPF \cdot (T_1 - T_{ADP})$$
$$T_2 = 12 + 0.25 \times (38 - 12) = 12 + 0.25 \times 26 = 12 + 6.5$$
$$\boxed{T_2 = 18.5 \text{ °C}}$$

**Apply BPF analogously for ω₂:**
$$\omega_2 = \omega_{ADP} + BPF \cdot (\omega_1 - \omega_{ADP}) = 0.00873 + 0.25 \times (0.01620 - 0.00873)$$
$$\omega_2 = 0.00873 + 0.25 \times 0.00747 = 0.00873 + 0.00187$$
$$\boxed{\omega_2 = 0.01060 \text{ kg water/kg dry air}}$$

**(iii) SHF of the coil:**

$$SHF = \frac{c_{pa}(T_1 - T_2)}{c_{pa}(T_1 - T_2) + h_{fg}(\omega_1 - \omega_2)}$$
$$SHF = \frac{1.005 \times (38 - 18.5)}{1.005 \times (38 - 18.5) + 2501 \times (0.01620 - 0.01060)}$$
$$SHF = \frac{1.005 \times 19.5}{19.5975 + 2501 \times 0.00560}$$
$$SHF = \frac{19.5955}{19.5975 + 14.0056} = \frac{19.5955}{33.6031}$$
$$\boxed{SHF = 0.583}$$

#### Part (b) — Vapour Compression Cycle in Split AC

**Components and their roles (3 marks):**

1. **Compressor:** Low-pressure vapor refrigerant is compressed to high-pressure, high-temperature superheated vapor. Raises enthalpy for heat rejection in the condenser.
2. **Condenser (Outdoor coil):** High-pressure vapor rejects heat to outdoor ambient and condenses to high-pressure liquid. Heat is forced out by the outdoor fan.
3. **Expansion Valve (Throttle):** High-pressure liquid is throttled to low-pressure, low-temperature two-phase mixture — sudden pressure drop causes flash evaporation and temperature drop.
4. **Evaporator (Indoor coil):** Low-pressure refrigerant evaporates by absorbing heat from indoor air (sensible + latent). Indoor blower circulates return air over the coil, supplying cool dehumidified air back to the room.

**P-h Diagram Description (2 marks):**
- **1 → 2:** Compression (slightly superheated vapor to high-pressure superheated vapor) — vertical-ish line on P-h chart.
- **2 → 3:** Condensation (heat rejection, vapor to saturated liquid) — horizontal-ish line at high P.
- **3 → 4:** Throttling (iso-enthalpic expansion, pressure drop with constant h) — vertical line down.
- **4 → 1:** Evaporation (heat absorption, two-phase to saturated vapor) — horizontal-ish line at low P.
- Refrigeration effect: $q_L = h_1 - h_4$. Compressor work: $w_c = h_2 - h_1$. COP: $(h_1 - h_4)/(h_2 - h_1)$.

**R-32 vs R-410A Comparison (2 marks):**

| Property | R-32 | R-410A |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Pure CH₂F₂ | R-32/R-125 blend (50/50) |
| GWP (Global Warming Potential) | **675** | 2088 |
| ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential) | 0 | 0 |
| Capacity (per kg) | ~5% higher | Lower |
| Pressure | ~20% higher | Moderate |
| Flammability | Mildly flammable (A2L) | Non-flammable |
| Energy efficiency | Higher (5–10% EER gain) | Lower |
| Kigali Amendment compliance | **Yes (preferred)** | Phasedown |

> R-32 is now the **BEE Star Rating preferred refrigerant** in India for room ACs (mandate from Jan 2024 onwards); R-410A is being phased out under Kigali Amendment to the Montreal Protocol.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3:**

**Cryogenic Freezers:**
- ☐ Cryogenics = below −150 °C; LN₂ (−196 °C) and LCO₂ (−78.5 °C) are the industrial workhorses.
- ☐ LN₂ latent heat = 199.3 kJ/kg; effective cooling ~ 400 kJ/kg when vented near ambient.
- ☐ Three modes: Immersion (h = 1000–1500), Spray (h = 200–500), Cryomechanical (hybrid).
- ☐ LN₂ consumption = 1.0–1.5 kg per kg of food (typical industrial value).
- ☐ Freezing rate > 3 cm/h → micro-crystalline ice → premium quality.
- ☐ Three heat stages: Q_sensible_above + Q_latent + Q_sensible_below.
- ☐ Use c_p_above ≈ 3.5 kJ/kg·K and c_p_below ≈ 1.8 kJ/kg·K for food.
- ☐ IQF = Individually Quick Frozen (each piece frozen separately) — most common cryogenic product.
- ☐ Cryogenics are NOT a closed cycle — LN₂ is consumed and vented (cost is the cryogen itself).
- ☐ Applications: premium seafood, IQF fruits, biotech, vaccines, mRNA, organ transport.

**Air Conditioning:**
- ☐ Four pillars: temperature, humidity, air purity, air distribution.
- ☐ 1 TR = 3.517 kW = 200 BTU/min (MUST memorize).
- ☐ ω = 0.622 P_v / (P − P_v) — humidity ratio formula.
- ☐ h = 1.005 T + ω(2501 + 1.82 T) — moist air enthalpy (kJ/kg dry air).
- ☐ BPF = (T₂ − T_ADP) / (T₁ − T_ADP); CF = 1 − BPF.
- ☐ SHF = sensible / total heat; RSHF must equal SHF of coil for design.
- ☐ EER = cooling output (W) / power input (W); ISEER for seasonal Indian context.
- ☐ Split AC: indoor (evaporator + blower) + outdoor (compressor + condenser + fan) connected by refrigerant lines.
- ☐ R-32 has GWP 675 (preferred); R-410A has GWP 2088 (phasedown).
- ☐ ADP (Apparatus Dew Point) = saturation temp. of coil surface.
- ☐ Cooling + dehumidification = simultaneous sensible + latent heat removal.
- ☐ For thermal comfort: summer 25 °C / 50% RH; winter 21 °C / 30% RH (ISHRAE India standards).

**Cross-Connections to Cold Chain:**
- ☐ Cold chain = production → cryogenic/blast freezing → cold storage → reefer transport → retail display → consumer.
- ☐ Cryogenic freezing is the "rapid shock" stage that locks in quality; mechanical refrigeration maintains temperature downstream.
- ☐ IoT temperature monitoring is increasingly mandatory under FSSAI regulations in India for pharmaceuticals and seafood export.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human and Industrial Air Conditioning Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT414 - REFRIGERATION AND COLD CHAIN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Human and Industrial Air Conditioning Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Defining Air Conditioning

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:**
> *Air conditioning is the simultaneous control of temperature, humidity, air cleanliness (purity), and air motion (velocity/circulation) within a confined space, to provide a desired level of human comfort or to maintain specific conditions required for a product or industrial process.*

The four cardinal parameters controlled in any air conditioning system are:

1. **Temperature** ($T$) — typically measured in $^\circ\text{C}$
2. **Relative Humidity** ($\phi$) — expressed as a percentage
3. **Air Purity** — measured by dust/particulate concentration (mg/m³ or ppm)
4. **Air Motion** — measured in m/s (typically 0.1 to 0.5 m/s for comfort)

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Module 3):**
> The scope of this module is bifurcated into **Human (Comfort) Air Conditioning** and **Industrial Air Conditioning**, emphasizing the contrast in design intent — comfort prioritizes human physiology, while industrial AC prioritizes product integrity, process precision, and equipment preservation.

---

## 1.2 The Two Branches: Human vs Industrial Air Conditioning

| Parameter | Human (Comfort) AC | Industrial AC |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Primary Goal** | Human physiological comfort | Product/process quality |
| **Temperature Range** | $20-27^\circ\text{C}$ | $-40^\circ\text{C}$ to $+80^\circ\text{C}$ (varies) |
| **RH Range** | $40-60\%$ | $5-95\%$ (application-specific) |
| **Air Velocity** | $\le 0.5$ m/s | 0.5 to 5 m/s |
| **Air Purity** | Dust-free, odorless | Often dust-free, humidity-critical |
| **Load Priority** | Occupant + solar + ventilation | Process + equipment + infiltration |
| **Design Standard** | ASHRAE 55, ISHRAE | Process specifications |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Living Room vs Clean Room"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuitive Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine two very different rooms. **Room A** is your living room — you want it mildly cool, slightly breezy, and just comfortable enough to read a book. You don't care if the humidity is exactly 30% or 50%; you just don't want to sweat. **Room B** is a pharmaceutical clean room manufacturing vaccines — here, the temperature must be exact (say $22\pm 0.5^\circ\text{C}$), the humidity must be tightly bound ($45\pm 2\%$), and dust particles bigger than $0.5\ \mu\text{m}$ must be eliminated. If you treat Room B like Room A, you ruin millions of dollars of medicine. This contrast — *comfort-driven* vs *precision-driven* — is the philosophical root of why Human AC and Industrial AC are engineered so differently.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Air Conditioning Systems (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 syllabus classifies AC systems along two primary axes:

**By Function:**
- **Human (Comfort) AC** — residential, commercial, institutional spaces
- **Industrial AC** — manufacturing, storage, processing units

**By Equipment Arrangement:**
- **Unitary Systems** — Window AC, Split AC, Packaged AC
- **Central Systems** — AHU-based systems serving large zones

> [!NOTE]
> For Module 3, the focus is on **functional applications** (where and why AC is used), not the hardware. However, hardware selection for human vs industrial contexts differs drastically — we shall cover both.

---

## 1.5 Human (Comfort) Air Conditioning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comfort AC Definition (ASHRAE 55 aligned):**
> Comfort air conditioning is the conditioning of air in occupied spaces such that the effective temperature, humidity, air motion, and purity together produce a state of physiological neutrality for at least **80%** of occupants, typically corresponding to a Predicted Mean Vote (PMV) between **−0.5 and +0.5**.

The **Effective Temperature (ET)** or more modern **Standard Effective Temperature (SET\*)** integrates:

$$\text{SET}^* = f(T_{db},\ T_{wb},\ V_{air},\ \text{clothing insulation},\ \text{metabolic rate})$$

where clothing insulation is measured in **clo** (1 clo = $0.155\ \text{m}^2\text{·K/W}$) and metabolic rate in **met** (1 met = $58.2\ \text{W/m}^2$).

---

## 1.6 Industrial Air Conditioning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial AC Definition:**
> Industrial air conditioning is the controlled conditioning of air within industrial premises to maintain temperature, humidity, purity, and air motion at precise levels required for product quality, process efficiency, equipment preservation, and worker comfort during specific industrial operations.

Industrial AC is classified into:

1. **Industrial Comfort Conditioning** — for workers in factories, foundries
2. **Industrial Process Conditioning** — for products (e.g., textile, pharma, electronics)
3. **Product Storage Conditioning** — for warehousing cold chain items (linking directly to PEFTT414 course context)

---

## 1.7 Human Physiology & Comfort — Why We Need AC

The human body maintains a core temperature of approximately **$37 \pm 0.5^\circ\text{C}$**. Heat exchange with the environment occurs via:

- **Convection** ($Q_{conv}$) — from skin to surrounding air
- **Evaporation** ($Q_{evap}$) — sweat evaporation
- **Radiation** ($Q_{rad}$) — from warm surfaces
- **Respiration** ($Q_{resp}$) — latent/sensible from lungs

The **heat balance equation** for a human body:

$$Q_{met} = Q_{conv} + Q_{rad} + Q_{evap} + Q_{resp} + Q_{stored}$$

where $Q_{stored} = 0$ for thermal equilibrium. If $Q_{stored} > 0$, the body heats up (hyperthermia); if $Q_{stored} < 0$, hypothermia sets in.

> [!NOTE]
> **Design Insight:** Comfort AC systems are sized to remove the *sensible heat* (from occupants, lights, walls, solar gain) plus the *latent heat* (from occupants' respiration/sweat, cooking, infiltration). Typical split between sensible and latent in comfort AC is **70% sensible / 30% latent**.

---

## 1.8 Industrial Applications — The Diversity

| Industry | Typical T ($^\circ\text{C}$) | Typical RH (%) | Why Critical? |
|----------|------------------------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Textile (spinning/weaving)** | $22-27$ | $55-75$ | Yarn breakage from static |
| **Pharmaceutical** | $20-24$ | $30-50$ | Drug stability, contamination |
| **Electronics/Semiconductor** | $21\pm 1$ | $40-50$ | Lithography precision |
| **Food Processing** | $0-4$ | $85-95$ | Microbial control |
| **Cold Storage (fruits)** | $0-10$ | $85-95$ | Prevents moisture loss |
| **Cold Storage (meat)** | $-18$ to $-25$ | $90-95$ | Freezer burn prevention |
| **Data Centers** | $18-22$ | $40-55$ | Server reliability |
| **Hospitals (OT)** | $18-22$ | $50-55$ | Sterile environment |
| **Foundry/Forge** | $25-35$ | $40-60$ | Worker safety |
| **Printing** | $24\pm 2$ | $45-55$ | Ink drying, paper curl |

This wide variety justifies why **Industrial AC is not "one-size-fits-all"** — it is engineered per process.

---

## 1.9 Why Bother Studying This Distinction?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Industrial Emphasis:**
> A refrigeration engineer working in a cold chain industry (which is the central theme of PEFTT414) must understand:
> - How warehouse HVAC differs from cold storage refrigeration
> - How fruit ripening rooms differ from frozen food warehouses
> - How pharmaceuticals require both temperature AND humidity precision
> - How a person's comfort load differs from a tonne of apples' respiration load
>
> Module 3 bridges *Refrigeration* (Module 1 & 2) with *Cold Chain Application* (Module 4 & 5) by anchoring the conditioning principles.

---

## 1.10 Standard Human Comfort Conditions (ASHRAE 55)

The most commonly cited comfort zone:

| Season | Dry Bulb T ($^\circ\text{C}$) | RH (%) | Air Speed (m/s) |
|--------|-------------------------------|--------|------------------|
| **Summer** | $22-26$ | $30-60$ | $\le 0.25$ |
| **Winter** | $20-24$ | $30-60$ | $\le 0.15$ |

The **comfort line** (commonly cited) is approximately at **$25^\circ\text{C}$ DB & $50\%$ RH** in summer, corresponding to a wet-bulb of roughly **$17.7^\circ\text{C}$**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse *Effective Temperature (ET)* with *Wet Bulb Temperature (WBT)*. ET incorporates air motion and is a *sensation-based* index, not a directly measurable temperature.

---

## 1.11 Industrial AC vs Human AC — Core Design Differences

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial AC vs Human AC — Engineering Reality:**
> In **Human AC**, the engineer asks: *"What makes people comfortable?"* and the answer is approximate.
> In **Industrial AC**, the engineer asks: *"What does the product/process need?"* and the answer is *exact*.
>
> This single philosophical shift changes:
> - **Sensor density** (industrial uses more sensors, often redundant)
> - **Control precision** (industrial uses PID / model-predictive control)
> - **Redundancy** (industrial uses $N+1$ or $2N$ redundancy)
> - **Filtration** (industrial often uses HEPA / ULPA filters)
> - **Air change rate** (industrial 10-600 ACH vs comfort 4-10 ACH)
> - **Energy efficiency priority** (industrial values reliability over energy)

---

## 1.12 Visual Comparison Snapshot

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** Think of Human AC as a *gentle nudge* to your body's thermostat, while Industrial AC is a *precise surgical instrument* on the product's micro-environment. Both use the same underlying physics (psychrometrics, heat transfer, vapor compression) — but the *tolerances* and *priorities* differ by orders of magnitude.

---

## 1.13 Module 3 Linkage to Cold Chain

As the course is **PEFTT414 — Refrigeration and Cold Chain**, the human and industrial AC topics tie into:

- **Pack House AC** — pre-cooling fruits before cold storage
- **Ripening Chamber AC** — controlling ethylene and T/RH
- **Reefer Container AC** — international shipping
- **Pharma Cold Chain AC** — vaccine storage at $2-8^\circ\text{C}$
- **Frozen Food Warehouse AC** — blast freezers and cold rooms
- **Display Cabinet AC** — supermarket refrigeration
- **HVAC for Processing Plants** — dairy, meat, bakery plants

These applications are discussed in detail in subsequent modules, but Module 3 establishes the foundational vocabulary.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Human (Comfort) Air Conditioning — Engineering Architecture

### 2.1.1 Components of a Comfort AC System

A typical comfort AC system comprises:

1. **Air Handling Unit (AHU)** with:
   - Filter (mechanical / electrostatic)
   - Cooling coil (chilled water or direct expansion)
   - Heating coil (optional, for winter)
   - Humidifier / Dehumidifier
   - Blower (supply and return fans)
2. **Distribution Network** — ducts, diffusers, grilles
3. **Refrigeration Plant** — chiller (water-cooled or air-cooled)
4. **Control System** — thermostats, humidistats, BMS

### 2.1.2 Psychrometric Process for Comfort Cooling

The standard Summer Comfort AC process on a psychrometric chart:

> **O (Outdoor) → M (Mixed) → C (Cooled) → S (Supplied) → R (Room) → O**

$$\text{OA (35°C, 60% RH)} \xrightarrow{\text{mixed with RA}} \text{M} \xrightarrow{\text{cool + dehumidify}} \text{C (13°C, 90% RH)} \xrightarrow{\text{fan heat}} \text{S (15°C)} \xrightarrow{\text{room load}} \text{R (25°C, 50% RH)}$$

### 2.1.3 Summer Comfort AC Equation

The By-Pass Factor (BPF) is the central design parameter:

$$\text{BPF} = \frac{T_C - T_S}{T_C - T_{dp}}$$

where:
- $T_C$ = apparatus dew point (ADP) / coil surface temperature
- $T_S$ = supply air temperature
- $T_{dp}$ = dew point temperature

The corresponding **Contact Factor (CF)**:

$$\text{CF} = 1 - \text{BPF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why BPF matters:** A BPF of 0 (perfect contact) is thermodynamically ideal but practically impossible. Typical BPF for chilled water coils is **0.10 to 0.30**; for DX coils, **0.20 to 0.40**.

### 2.1.4 Sensible Heat (SH) and Latent Heat (LH) for Human Occupants

For a sedentary office worker (typical metabolic rate $\approx 1.0$ met):

| Heat Type | Value per Person |
|-----------|------------------|
| Sensible Heat | $60\ \text{W}$ |
| Latent Heat | $40\ \text{W}$ |
| **Total** | **$100\ \text{W}$** |

The **Sensible Heat Factor (SHF)** for human occupants:

$$\text{SHF} = \frac{Q_s}{Q_s + Q_l} = \frac{60}{100} = 0.60$$

> [!TIP]
> **Designer's Rule of Thumb:** For offices, total occupant load = **100 W/person** (sensible 60 W, latent 40 W). For gyms/restaurants, occupants generate **250-300 W/person** with much higher latent fraction.

### 2.1.5 Equipment Load Contributions in Comfort AC

$$\boxed{Q_{total} = Q_{sensible} + Q_{latent}}$$

Where:
- $Q_{sensible} = Q_{walls} + Q_{roof} + Q_{glass} + Q_{occupants} + Q_{lights} + Q_{equipment} + Q_{infiltration}$
- $Q_{latent} = Q_{occupants} + Q_{infiltration} + Q_{cooking}$

### 2.1.6 Heat Gain Through Walls (Conduction)

$$Q_{wall} = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLD}$$

where:
- $U$ = overall heat transfer coefficient ($\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$)
- $A$ = area ($\text{m}^2$)
- $\text{CLD}$ = Cooling Load Difference (corrected temperature difference)

### 2.1.7 Heat Gain Through Glass (Solar + Conduction)

$$Q_{glass} = A \cdot (\text{SHGC}) \cdot I_{solar} + U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

where SHGC = Solar Heat Gain Coefficient.

---

## 2.2 Industrial Air Conditioning — Engineering Architecture

### 2.2.1 Classification of Industrial AC Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Industrial AC Sub-Classification:**
> 1. **Industrial Comfort AC** — for worker welfare
> 2. **Industrial Process AC** — for product quality
> 3. **Product Storage AC** — for warehousing (cold chain)

### 2.2.2 Types of Industrial AC Systems

| Type | Description | Application |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Unitary** | Self-contained (window/split) | Small workshops, control rooms |
| **Central Station** | AHU + ductwork | Medium factories |
| **Zonal Systems** | Multiple units per zone | Open-floor factories |
| **Spot Cooling** | Localized air jets | Hot workstations |
| **Clean Rooms** | HEPA-filtered laminar flow | Pharma, semiconductors |

### 2.2.3 Air Change Rate (ACH) — Industrial

$$\text{ACH} = \frac{\dot{V}_{fresh}}{V_{room}}$$

where $\dot{V}_{fresh}$ is fresh air volumetric flow rate ($\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$) and $V_{room}$ is room volume.

| Space Type | Typical ACH |
|-----------|-------------|
| Comfort (office) | 4-8 |
| Hospital OT | 20-25 |
| Clean Room (Class 1000) | 50-70 |
| Clean Room (Class 100) | 400-600 |
| Paint Booth | 100-150 |

### 2.2.4 Process Loads in Industrial AC

For an industrial process, total load is:

$$Q_{process} = Q_{equipment} + Q_{product} + Q_{process\ reaction} + Q_{ventilation} + Q_{transmission}$$

### 2.2.5 Product Respiration Load (Cold Chain Context)

This is *unique* to cold chain applications:

$$\dot{Q}_{resp} = m \cdot \dot{q}_{resp}$$

where $\dot{q}_{resp}$ is specific heat of respiration (W/kg), e.g.:
- Apples: $0.015-0.04\ \text{W/kg}$ at $0^\circ\text{C}$
- Bananas: $0.05-0.15\ \text{W/kg}$ at $13^\circ\text{C}$
- Strawberries: $0.08-0.20\ \text{W/kg}$ at $5^\circ\text{C}$

### 2.2.6 Latent Heat from Product Moisture

$$\dot{Q}_{latent} = \dot{m}_{evap} \cdot h_{fg}$$

where $\dot{m}_{evap}$ is evaporation rate (kg/s) and $h_{fg}$ is latent heat of vaporization ($\approx 2500\ \text{kJ/kg}$ at typical AC temperatures).

---

## 2.3 Cold Chain Industrial AC — A Special Focus

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Course Emphasis:**
> As PEFTT414 is a Cold Chain course, Industrial AC applications must be specifically studied for:
> - **Cold Storage (CS)** — long-term storage of frozen/chilled foods
> - **Cold Rooms (CR)** — short-term holding at processing plants
> - **Ripening Chambers** — ethylene, T, RH controlled
> - **Blast Freezers** — rapid cooling of packaged foods
> - **Pre-Cooling Chambers** — field-heat removal from produce

### 2.3.1 Cold Storage AC System Layout

The conditioning system has these distinct load components:

$$\boxed{\dot{Q}_{CS} = \dot{Q}_{transmission} + \dot{Q}_{product} + \dot{Q}_{respiration} + \dot{Q}_{infiltration} + \dot{Q}_{equipment}}$$

Each term is computed separately. The dominant terms are typically **transmission load** (through walls) and **product load** (bringing goods from outside temperature to storage temperature).

### 2.3.2 Transmission Load Through Cold Storage Walls

$$\dot{Q}_{trans} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$$

Cold storage walls use **Polyurethane Foam (PUF)** insulation with $U$ values of **0.20 to 0.35 $\text{W/m}^2\text{·K}$**, depending on insulation thickness (typically 100-200 mm).

### 2.3.3 Product Load — Sensible Cooling from $T_1$ to $T_2$

$$\dot{Q}_{product} = m \cdot c_p \cdot (T_1 - T_2)$$

For foods, $c_p \approx 3.5\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ above freezing, and $\approx 1.8\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ below freezing (varies with moisture content).

### 2.3.4 Product Load — Latent (Freezing) Load

$$\dot{Q}_{freeze} = m \cdot h_f$$

where $h_f$ is latent heat of freezing ($\approx 335\ \text{kJ/kg}$ for pure water, less for foods depending on water content).

### 2.3.5 Infiltration Load During Door Opening

$$\dot{Q}_{inf} = \dot{V}_{air} \cdot \rho_{air} \cdot c_{p,air} \cdot (T_{out} - T_{in})$$

or using enthalpy difference:

$$\dot{Q}_{inf} = \dot{V}_{air} \cdot (h_{out} - h_{in})$$

Air curtains reduce infiltration by **60-80%**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these are the equations that appear in KTU 2024 exam questions.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | **By-Pass Factor (BPF)** | $\text{BPF} = (T_C - T_S)/(T_C - T_{dp})$ | dimensionless | Coil effectiveness |
| 2 | **Contact Factor** | $\text{CF} = 1 - \text{BPF}$ | dimensionless | Coil performance |
| 3 | **Sensible Heat (air)** | $Q_s = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Air heating/cooling |
| 4 | **Latent Heat (air)** | $Q_l = \dot{m} \cdot h_{fg} \cdot \Delta W$ | W | Humidification/dehum. |
| 5 | **Sensible Heat Factor** | $\text{SHF} = Q_s / (Q_s + Q_l)$ | dimensionless | Coil selection |
| 6 | **Total Heat** | $Q_t = Q_s + Q_l$ | W | Total cooling |
| 7 | **Heat Through Wall** | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \text{CLD}$ | W | Conduction load |
| 8 | **Air Change Rate** | $\text{ACH} = \dot{V} / V_{room}$ | 1/hr | Ventilation design |
| 9 | **Product Cooling (sensible)** | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | W | Cold chain product |
| 10 | **Product Freezing (latent)** | $Q = m \cdot h_f$ | W | Phase change load |
| 11 | **Respiration Load** | $Q = m \cdot \dot{q}_{resp}$ | W | Fruits/vegetables |
| 12 | **Infiltration Load** | $Q = \dot{V}\cdot\rho\cdot c_p\cdot\Delta T$ | W | Door opening losses |
| 13 | **Effective Temperature (approx.)** | $\text{ET} \approx T_{db} - (1/2)(T_{db} - T_{wb})$ | °C | Comfort index |
| 14 | **PMV Approximation** | $\text{PMV} \approx f(\text{SET}^*,\text{Met},\text{Clo})$ | dimensionless | Thermal comfort |
| 15 | **Refrigeration Ton (RT)** | $1\ \text{RT} = 3.517\ \text{kW}$ | kW | Capacity unit |
| 16 | **Air Density (standard)** | $\rho_{air} \approx 1.2\ \text{kg/m}^3$ | kg/m³ | Mass flow |

---

## 2.5 Why Human AC and Industrial AC Need Different Equipment

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rationale:**
> Human AC operates within **narrow** but **non-critical** conditions — small deviations don't harm anyone.
> Industrial AC operates within **wide** but **critical** conditions — deviations ruin products.
>
> Consequence:
> - Human AC: Standard off-the-shelf units, simple thermostat, no redundancy
> - Industrial AC: Custom-designed systems, PID control, redundant chillers, backup power
>
> **Cost differential:** Industrial AC can cost **3-10×** more per kW than comfort AC due to precision and reliability demands.

---

## 2.6 Summary of Differences (For Exam Answering)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Comparison for KTU 2024 14-mark Question:**

| Parameter | Human AC | Industrial AC |
|-----------|----------|----------------|
| Objective | Comfort | Product/Process integrity |
| Temperature precision | $\pm 2^\circ\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 1^\circ\text{C}$ |
| Humidity precision | $\pm 10\%$ RH | $\pm 2-5\%$ RH |
| Air purity | General | HEPA/ULPA often required |
| Air velocity | Low ($\le 0.5$ m/s) | Variable (0.5-5 m/s) |
| Load profile | Occupant-driven | Product/process-driven |
| Control | Thermostat only | PID, multi-parameter BMS |
| Reliability | Single unit adequate | $N+1$ redundancy |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Energy priority | Moderate | High (with reliability first) |

---

## 2.7 Air Conditioning for Cold Storage (Cold Chain-Specific)

Cold storage is the industrial application with the **largest footprint in cold chain engineering**.

### 2.7.1 Cold Storage Layout Schematic

```
[Outdoor Refrigeration Plant] → [Compressor + Condenser]
                                       ↓
[Liquid Line] → [Expansion Valve] → [Evaporator inside Cold Room]
                                       ↓
                              [Forced Air Circulation]
                                       ↓
                              [Return Air → Evaporator]
```

### 2.7.2 Cold Storage Temperature Categories (Indian Standard)

| Category | Temperature | Product Example |
|----------|-------------|----------------|
| **Chilled Storage** | $0$ to $+4^\circ\text{C}$ | Fresh fruits, dairy, eggs |
| **Sub-Zero Chilled** | $-2$ to $0^\circ\text{C}$ | Meat, fish (fresh) |
| **Frozen Storage** | $-18$ to $-25^\circ\text{C}$ | Frozen foods, ice cream |
| **Deep Frozen** | $-30$ to $-40^\circ\text{C}$ | Sushi-grade fish, pharma |

### 2.7.3 Cold Storage Humidity Requirements

| Product | Optimum T (°C) | Optimum RH (%) | Storage Life |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|---------------|
| Apples | $0$ to $4$ | $90-95$ | 4-6 months |
| Potatoes | $4$ to $10$ | $90-95$ | 6-9 months |
| Tomatoes | $10$ to $13$ | $85-90$ | 2-4 weeks |
| Bananas | $13$ to $15$ | $85-90$ | 1-2 weeks |
| Meat (beef) | $-1$ to $0$ | $85-90$ | 1-4 weeks |
| Frozen fish | $-25$ to $-30$ | $90-95$ | 6-12 months |

---

## 2.8 Winter Air Conditioning

For comfort AC, winter requires heating and humidification:

$$\text{OA (cold, dry)} \rightarrow \text{Preheat} \rightarrow \text{Humidify} \rightarrow \text{Reheat} \rightarrow \text{Supply} \rightarrow \text{Room}$$

The **Preheating + Humidifying + Reheating** system uses:

$$\boxed{Q_{reheat} = \dot{m}_{air} \cdot c_p \cdot (T_{supply} - T_{saturated})}$$

where $T_{saturated}$ is the temperature after adiabatic saturation (humidification).

> [!NOTE]
> Industrial cold storage is **single-mode** — it only cools and dehumidifies, never heats. Hence the system design is simpler than comfort AC.

---

| Small office | Window AC / Mini-split |

| Hospital OT | Dedicated AHU + HEPA + UPS |

| Blast Freezer | DX with high CFM evaporators |
